topics.title: Setting Up and Installing Hadoop in Standalone, Pseudo Distributed, and Fully Distributed Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Setting Up and Installing Hadoop in Standalone, Pseudo Distributed, and Fully Distributed Modes ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Hadoop? **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source framework that allows for the **distributed storage and processing of large datasets** across clusters of commodity computers. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage. Hadoop is built on two core components: - **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System):** Manages storage across the cluster. - **MapReduce:** A programming model for parallel data processing. ### 1.2 The Three Operating Modes of Hadoop Hadoop can be configured to run in **three distinct modes**, primarily distinguished by the **degree of distribution and the number of nodes** involved. #### A. Standalone (Local) Mode > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** The **Standalone Mode** is the default mode of Hadoop where all Hadoop daemons (NameNode, DataNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager) run within a **single JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** on a single machine. It uses the local file system instead of HDFS and is intended purely for debugging and learning MapReduce logic. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of Standalone Mode as a **single chef cooking in a small home kitchen**. There is one person (JVM), one set of utensils (local file system), and they are just experimenting with a recipe (MapReduce program) before opening a real restaurant. No coordination with other chefs is needed. #### B. Pseudo-Distributed Mode > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** **Pseudo-Distributed Mode** simulates a fully distributed cluster on a **single physical machine**. Each Hadoop daemon (NameNode, DataNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager, Secondary NameNode) runs as a **separate Java process**, mimicking a real multi-node cluster, but all communication happens over `localhost` (127.0.0.1). **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a **one-person theater play** where the same actor plays five different characters (NameNode, DataNode, etc.) by switching masks and costumes. The play looks like a full production with many actors, but in reality, it's all happening on a single small stage (one machine). It is used to test distributed logic before deploying on a real cluster. #### C. Fully Distributed Mode > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition:** **Fully Distributed Mode** is the production-grade deployment where Hadoop daemons run on **multiple physical or virtual machines** (a real cluster). There is at least one dedicated **master node** (running NameNode and ResourceManager) and multiple **slave/worker nodes** (running DataNode and NodeManager), communicating over a real network. **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of Fully Distributed Mode as a **real industrial restaurant chain** with branches across the city. Each branch (worker node) has its own chefs and storage (DataNode), while a central headquarters (master node) coordinates orders and resource allocation (NameNode + ResourceManager). Orders flow over the city's road network (LAN/WAN). ### 1.3 Summary Snapshot | Mode | Daemons | File System | Nodes | Purpose | |------|---------|-------------|-------|---------| | Standalone | None (single JVM) | Local FS (file:///) | 1 | Learning / Debugging | | Pseudo-Distributed | All as separate processes | HDFS (hdfs:///) | 1 | Testing / Development | | Fully Distributed | All distributed | HDFS (hdfs:///) | 2+ | Production / Real Clustering | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students are expected to know the **default configuration file parameters** for each mode (`mapred-site.xml`, `core-site.xml`, `hdfs-site.xml`, `yarn-site.xml`) and the **default port numbers** of each Hadoop daemon. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Configuration Sheet ### 2.1 Why Three Modes? The Operational Logic Hadoop's three modes are not arbitrary. They exist to provide a **progressive learning and deployment ladder**: 1. **Standalone Mode** → Validates **logic** (does the MapReduce code compile and produce correct output?). 2. **Pseudo-Distributed Mode** → Validates **distribution mechanics** (does HDFS work? Do daemons coordinate?). 3. **Fully Distributed Mode** → Validates **scalability and fault tolerance** (does the cluster handle multiple nodes?). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Configuration & Port Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is **examination-critical**. KTU lab exam questions frequently ask students to identify daemon-to-port mappings and configuration parameter defaults. | Daemon / Component | Default Port (Standalone) | Default Port (Distributed) | Configuration File | |---|---|---|---| | NameNode (HDFS Web UI) | N/A | **9870** | hdfs-site.xml | | NameNode (RPC) | N/A | **9000** | core-site.xml | | Secondary NameNode | N/A | **9868** | hdfs-site.xml | | DataNode | N/A | **9864** | hdfs-site.xml | | ResourceManager (Web UI) | N/A | **8088** | yarn-site.xml | | NodeManager (Web UI) | N/A | **8042** | yarn-site.xml | | JobHistory Server | N/A | **19888** | mapred-site.xml | ### 2.3 Core Configuration Parameters (High-Yield) The four XML configuration files govern Hadoop's behavior. Each mode toggles specific properties. #### `core-site.xml` | Property | Standalone Value | Pseudo-Distributed / Fully Distributed Value | |---|---|---| | `fs.defaultFS` | `file:///` | `hdfs://localhost:9000` (pseudo) or `hdfs://namenode-host:9000` (full) | #### `hdfs-site.xml` | Property | Standalone Value | Pseudo/Fully Distributed Value | |---|---|---| | `dfs.replication` | 1 | **3** (full) / 1 (pseudo) | | `dfs.namenode.name.dir` | N/A | `/path/to/namenode` | | `dfs.datanode.data.dir` | N/A | `/path/to/datanode` | #### `mapred-site.xml` | Property | Standalone Value | Pseudo/Fully Distributed Value | |---|---|---| | `mapreduce.framework.name` | `local` | `yarn` | #### `yarn-site.xml` | Property | Standalone Value | Pseudo/Fully Distributed Value | |---|---|---| | `yarn.nodemanager.aux-services` | (not set) | `mapreduce_shuffle` | | `yarn.resourcemanager.hostname` | localhost | `` | ### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Configurations - **`fs.defaultFS` → `hdfs://`:** Tells Hadoop to use HDFS instead of the local file system. - **`dfs.replication` → 3:** Ensures **fault tolerance** by storing 3 copies of each block across different DataNodes. - **`mapreduce.framework.name` → `yarn`:** Switches the execution engine from local JVM execution to YARN's resource-managed execution. - **`yarn.nodemanager.aux-services`:** Enables the **shuffle** service, which is mandatory for MapReduce to function on YARN. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In production environments, the choice of mode is dictated by the **stage of the SDLC**: - **Development Teams** → Pseudo-Distributed on VMs for testing cluster behavior. - **QA Teams** → Fully Distributed on a mini-cluster (3-5 nodes). - **Production Deployment** → Fully Distributed on 100s/1000s of commodity nodes (e.g., Yahoo, Facebook clusters). - **Education** → Standalone for beginner MapReduce tutorials. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Even in production, Hadoop 3.x supports **Federation** (multiple NameNodes) and **High Availability (HA)** NameNode pairs, but the foundation remains the Fully Distributed Mode covered in this experiment. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Installation & Configuration Procedure ### 3.1 Pre-Requisites (Common to All Modes) Before installing Hadoop, the following must be installed on a **Ubuntu/Linux** system: 1. **Java Development Kit (JDK 8 or 11)** — Hadoop is written in Java. 2. **SSH (Secure Shell)** — Required for Hadoop daemons to communicate. 3. **Hadoop Binary Tarball** — Downloaded from `https://hadoop.apache.org/releases.html`. #### Setting Up SSH and Password-less Login The following steps enable **SSH password-less authentication** between the master and worker nodes (critical for Fully Distributed Mode): ``` # Step 1: Install SSH sudo apt-get install openssh-server openssh-client # Step 2: Generate RSA key pair ssh-keygen -t rsa -P "" # Step 3: Copy public key to authorized_keys cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys chmod 0600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys # Step 4: Verify password-less login ssh localhost ``` **Why this matters:** Hadoop's master node uses SSH to remotely start and stop daemons on worker nodes. Without password-less SSH, the cluster cannot auto-manage worker processes. ### 3.2 Installing Hadoop #### Step 1: Download and Extract ``` wget https://downloads.apache.org/hadoop/common/hadoop-3.3.6/hadoop-3.3.6.tar.gz tar -xvzf hadoop-3.3.6.tar.gz sudo mv hadoop-3.3.6 /usr/local/hadoop ``` #### Step 2: Set Environment Variables (`~/.bashrc`) ``` export HADOOP_HOME=/usr/local/hadoop export HADOOP_INSTALL=$HADOOP_HOME export HADOOP_MAPRED_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME export HADOOP_COMMON_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME export HADOOP_HDFS_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME export YARN_HOME=$HADOOP_HOME export PATH=$PATH:$HADOOP_HOME/bin:$HADOOP_HOME/sbin export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64 ``` Reload the shell: `source ~/.bashrc` ### 3.3 Mode 1: Standalone Mode Setup > [!NOTE] > **Standalone Mode requires NO configuration changes.** Hadoop's default configuration is Standalone. #### Verification Commands ``` # Verify Java access java -version # Verify Hadoop version hadoop version # Run a sample MapReduce word count on local FS hadoop jar $HADOOP_HOME/share/hadoop/mapreduce/hadoop-mapreduce-examples-3.3.6.jar wordcount /home/user/input.txt /home/user/output ``` **Output Location:** `/home/user/output/part-r-00000` (local file system) **Key Insight:** The absence of `hdfs://` in the input/output paths confirms the local file system is being used. ### 3.4 Mode 2: Pseudo-Distributed Mode Setup #### Step 1: Edit `core-site.xml` ``` fs.defaultFS hdfs://localhost:9000 ``` > **Logic:** This tells the NameNode to listen for client connections on `localhost:9000` using the HDFS protocol. #### Step 2: Edit `hdfs-site.xml` ``` dfs.replication 1 dfs.namenode.name.dir file:///usr/local/hadoop_store/hdfs/namenode dfs.datanode.data.dir file:///usr/local/hadoop_store/hdfs/datanode ``` > **Logic:** `dfs.replication=1` is used because we have only one DataNode in this mode. In a real cluster, this would be set to 3 for fault tolerance. #### Step 3: Edit `mapred-site.xml` ``` mapreduce.framework.name yarn ``` > **Logic:** This redirects MapReduce jobs to YARN instead of the local execution engine. #### Step 4: Edit `yarn-site.xml` ``` yarn.nodemanager.aux-services mapreduce_shuffle ``` > **Logic:** Enables the shuffle service required for the Reduce phase to pull intermediate data from the Map phase. #### Step 5: Format the NameNode (One-Time Operation) ``` hdfs namenode -format ``` > [!WARNING] > **Formatting should only be done ONCE** before the first start. Re-formatting a live NameNode **destroys all HDFS data** (simulating a disaster recovery scenario). #### Step 6: Start All Daemons ``` start-dfs.sh # Starts NameNode, DataNode, Secondary NameNode start-yarn.sh # Starts ResourceManager, NodeManager ``` #### Step 7: Verify Running Processes ``` jps ``` **Expected Output (Pseudo-Distributed):** ``` XXXX NameNode XXXX DataNode XXXX SecondaryNameNode XXXX ResourceManager XXXX NodeManager XXXX Jps ``` #### Step 8: Test with a MapReduce Job ``` # Create input directory and upload file to HDFS hdfs dfs -mkdir /user/input hdfs dfs -put /home/user/sample.txt /user/input # Run WordCount hadoop jar $HADOOP_HOME/share/hadoop/mapreduce/hadoop-mapreduce-examples-3.3.6.jar wordcount /user/input /user/output # View output hdfs dfs -cat /user/output/part-r-00000 ``` ### 3.5 Mode 3: Fully Distributed Mode Setup > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fully Distributed Mode requires a minimum of 2 nodes:** 1 Master + 1 Worker. For academic lab purposes, **virtual machines (VirtualBox/VMware)** or **cloud instances** are commonly used. #### Assumed Cluster Architecture | Node | Hostname | Role | Daemons | |------|----------|------|---------| | Master | `master.hadoop` | NameNode, ResourceManager | NameNode, SecondaryNameNode, ResourceManager | | Slave 1 | `slave1.hadoop` | Worker | DataNode, NodeManager | | Slave 2 | `slave2.hadoop` | Worker | DataNode, NodeManager | #### Step 1: Configure `/etc/hosts` on All Nodes ``` 192.168.1.10 master.hadoop 192.168.1.11 slave1.hadoop 192.168.1.12 slave2.hadoop ``` #### Step 2: Configure SSH from Master to All Slaves ``` ssh-copy-id hduser@slave1.hadoop ssh-copy-id hduser@slave2.hadoop ``` #### Step 3: Edit `core-site.xml` (on Master) ``` fs.defaultFS hdfs://master.hadoop:9000 ``` #### Step 4: Edit `hdfs-site.xml` (on Master) ``` dfs.replication 3 dfs.namenode.name.dir file:///usr/local/hadoop_store/hdfs/namenode dfs.datanode.data.dir file:///usr/local/hadoop_store/hdfs/datanode ``` > **Logic:** `dfs.replication=3` is the production default, ensuring data is available even if 2 DataNodes fail. #### Step 5: Edit `mapred-site.xml` (on Master) ``` mapreduce.framework.name yarn mapreduce.jobhistory.address master.hadoop:10020 ``` #### Step 6: Edit `yarn-site.xml` (on Master) ``` yarn.resourcemanager.hostname master.hadoop yarn.nodemanager.aux-services mapreduce_shuffle ``` #### Step 7: Configure Slaves File (`workers` in Hadoop 3.x) Edit `$HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/workers`: ``` slave1.hadoop slave2.hadoop ``` #### Step 8: Copy Configuration to All Slaves ``` scp -r $HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop hduser@slave1.hadoop:$HADOOP_HOME/etc/ scp -r $HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop hduser@slave2.hadoop:$HADOOP_HOME/etc/ ``` > **Logic:** The `workers` file on the master tells Hadoop which slave nodes should host DataNode and NodeManager daemons. The master reads this file and uses SSH to remotely start daemons on each listed worker. #### Step 9: Format NameNode and Start Cluster ``` # On Master only hdfs namenode -format start-dfs.sh start-yarn.sh # Verify on all nodes jps ``` #### Step 10: Web UI Verification Open browser on master: - **HDFS UI:** `http://master.hadoop:9870` → Should show 2 live DataNodes. - **YARN UI:** `http://master.hadoop:8088` → Should show 2 active NodeManagers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** KTU frequently asks "What is the role of the `workers` file?" — Answer: It lists all slave node hostnames on which the DataNode and NodeManager daemons are launched by the master during `start-dfs.sh` and `start-yarn.sh`. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hadoop Installation] --> B{Choose Operating Mode} B -->|Default: No config change| C[Standalone Mode] B -->|Edit 4 XML files| D[Pseudo-Distributed Mode] B -->|Multi-node + workers file| E[Fully Distributed Mode] C --> C1[Single JVM
Local FS file:///
Use: Learning] D --> D1[Single Machine
HDFS hdfs://localhost
Use: Development] E --> E1[Multiple Machines
HDFS hdfs://master:9000
Use: Production] ``` ### 4.2 Standalone Mode Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ONE_SINGLE_MACHINE[Single Machine] JVM[Single JVM Process] MR[MapReduce Job] LFS[Local File System
file:////home/user/] end JVM --> MR MR --> LFS MR_OUT[Output: part-r-00000] --> LFS ``` ### 4.3 Pseudo-Distributed Mode Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SINGLE_HOST[Single Physical Host: localhost] NN[NameNode
Port 9000] DN[DataNode
Port 9864] SNN[Secondary NameNode
Port 9868] RM[ResourceManager
Port 8088] NM[NodeManager
Port 8042] end CLIENT[Client Application] -->|RPC: 9000| NN NN -->|Block Reports| DN SNN -->|Checkpoint| NN CLIENT -->|Job Submission| RM RM -->|Container Allocation| NM DN -->|Storage| HDFS_BLOCKS[(HDFS Blocks
128 MB each)] ``` ### 4.4 Fully Distributed Mode Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MASTER_NODE[Master Node: master.hadoop] NN[NameNode
Port 9000] SNN[Secondary NameNode] RM[ResourceManager
Port 8088] JHS[JobHistory Server] end subgraph SLAVE1_NODE[Slave 1: slave1.hadoop] DN1[DataNode] NM1[NodeManager] end subgraph SLAVE2_NODE[Slave 2: slave2.hadoop] DN2[DataNode] NM2[NodeManager] end subgraph SLAVE3_NODE[Slave 3: slave3.hadoop] DN3[DataNode] NM3[NodeManager] end CLIENT[Client] -->|Read/Write RPC| NN NN -->|Heartbeat & Block Reports| DN1 NN -->|Heartbeat & Block Reports| DN2 NN -->|Heartbeat & Block Reports| DN3 SNN -->|FSImage + EditLog Checkpoint| NN CLIENT -->|Submit Job| RM RM -->|Allocate Containers| NM1 RM -->|Allocate Containers| NM2 RM -->|Allocate Containers| NM3 HDFS_DATA[(HDFS Replicated Blocks
Replication Factor = 3)] --- DN1 HDFS_DATA --- DN2 HDFS_DATA --- DN3 ``` ### 4.5 HDFS Block Replication Process (Fully Distributed) ```mermaid flowchart LR FILE[Input File 256 MB] --> SPLIT[Split into 2 Blocks
128 MB each] SPLIT --> B1[Block 1] SPLIT --> B2[Block 2] B1 --> R1A[Replica on slave1] B1 --> R1B[Replica on slave2] B1 --> R1C[Replica on slave3] B2 --> R2A[Replica on slave2] B2 --> R2B[Replica on slave3] B2 --> R2C[Replica on slave1] ``` ### 4.6 Configuration File Dependency Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Hadoop Startup] --> CORECFG[core-site.xml
fs.defaultFS] START --> HDFSCFG[hdfs-site.xml
replication, name dirs] START --> MAPREDCFG[mapred-site.xml
framework = yarn] START --> YARNCFG[yarn-site.xml
hostname, aux-services] CORECFG --> NN_START[NameNode Binds to HDFS URI] HDFSCFG --> DN_START[DataNode Stores Blocks] MAPREDCFG --> JOB_RUN[MapReduce Jobs Run on YARN] YARNCFG --> RM_START[ResourceManager Schedules Containers] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU Exams:** When asked to draw a diagram, KTU expects labeled **daemon names, port numbers, and data flow arrows**. Always include replication factor when drawing HDFS diagrams. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Hadoop's Standalone and Pseudo-Distributed Modes.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Standalone Mode | Pseudo-Distributed Mode | |-----------|-----------------|-------------------------| | Number of Nodes | Single Node | Single Node | | Daemons | Run in a single JVM | Run as separate Java processes | | File System | Local file system (`file:///`) | HDFS (`hdfs://localhost:9000`) | | Configuration | Default (no changes) | Requires 4 XML file edits | | Use Case | Debugging, learning MapReduce logic | Testing distributed cluster behavior | | Web UIs | Not available | Available on default ports | **(3 marks — Awarded for clear differentiation across at least 4 parameters)** #### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **What is the purpose of the `workers` file in Hadoop's Fully Distributed Mode?** **Model Answer:** The **`workers` file** (formerly `slaves` in Hadoop 2.x) is located at `$HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/workers` and contains a **list of hostnames** of all worker/slave nodes in the cluster. When the master runs `start-dfs.sh` or `start-yarn.sh`, it reads this file and uses **SSH** to remotely launch the **DataNode** and **NodeManager** daemons on each listed worker. Without this file, the master would have no knowledge of which nodes are part of the cluster. **(3 marks — Awarded for stating the file's location, contents, and its role in daemon startup)** --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU ESE follows strict **Module Internal Choice**. Students answer **ONE** full question from the two alternatives below. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the architecture of Hadoop's **Fully Distributed Mode** with a neat diagram. List all the daemons and their default port numbers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the step-by-step procedure to configure Hadoop in **Pseudo-Distributed Mode**. Include the contents of all four configuration files. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Fully Distributed Mode Architecture:** In the **Fully Distributed Mode**, Hadoop runs on a cluster of two or more physical/virtual machines. The cluster typically has one **master node** and multiple **worker nodes**. **Master Node Hosts:** - **NameNode** (Port 9000 for RPC, 9870 for Web UI) — Manages the file system metadata and namespace. - **Secondary NameNode** (Port 9868) — Performs periodic checkpoints of the FSImage. - **ResourceManager** (Port 8088) — Allocates cluster resources and schedules applications. - **JobHistory Server** (Port 19888) — Stores logs of completed jobs. **Worker Nodes Host:** - **DataNode** (Port 9864) — Stores actual HDFS data blocks. - **NodeManager** (Port 8042) — Manages containers and monitors resource usage on the node. **Workflow:** 1. The client submits a job to the ResourceManager. 2. The ResourceManager negotiates containers with NodeManagers. 3. Data is read from DataNodes via NameNode-mediated metadata lookup. 4. Blocks are replicated 3 times across DataNodes for fault tolerance. 5. After job completion, logs are sent to the JobHistory Server. ``` Master (master.hadoop) Slaves (slave1, slave2) +------------------+ +------------------+ | NameNode :9000 | | DataNode :9864 | | ResourceMgr :8088|<----------->| NodeManager :8042| | SecondaryNN:9868 | SSH + RPC | | +------------------+ +------------------+ ``` **(7 marks — Awarded for: 3 marks for architecture/daemon list, 2 marks for ports, 2 marks for workflow/diagram)** --- **(b) Pseudo-Distributed Mode Configuration Procedure:** **Step 1:** Install JDK and Hadoop, set environment variables in `~/.bashrc`. **Step 2:** Configure `core-site.xml`: ```xml fs.defaultFS hdfs://localhost:9000 ``` **Step 3:** Configure `hdfs-site.xml`: ```xml dfs.replication 1 dfs.namenode.name.dir file:///usr/local/hadoop_store/hdfs/namenode dfs.datanode.data.dir file:///usr/local/hadoop_store/hdfs/datanode ``` **Step 4:** Configure `mapred-site.xml`: ```xml mapreduce.framework.name yarn ``` **Step 5:** Configure `yarn-site.xml`: ```xml yarn.nodemanager.aux-services mapreduce_shuffle ``` **Step 6:** Format NameNode: `hdfs namenode -format` **Step 7:** Start daemons: `start-dfs.sh` and `start-yarn.sh` **Step 8:** Verify with `jps` — Expect 5 daemons (NameNode, DataNode, SecondaryNameNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager). **(7 marks — Awarded for: 1 mark for prereqs, 4 marks for configuration files, 2 marks for startup + verification)** --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **(a)** Compare Hadoop's **Standalone, Pseudo-Distributed, and Fully Distributed Modes** in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture** and the block replication process. State the default block size and replication factor. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison of Hadoop Modes:** | Feature | Standalone | Pseudo-Distributed | Fully Distributed | |---------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Number of Nodes** | 1 | 1 | 2 or more | | **Daemons** | None (single JVM) | All (separate processes) | All (distributed) | | **File System** | Local (`file:///`) | HDFS (`hdfs://localhost:9000`) | HDFS (`hdfs://master:9000`) | | **Configuration** | Default | 4 XML files | 4 XML files + `workers` | | **Replication Factor** | N/A | 1 | 3 (default) | | **Fault Tolerance** | None | Limited | High | | **SSH Requirement** | No | Optional | Mandatory | | **Use Case** | Learning, debugging | Development, testing | Production | | **Web UIs** | Not accessible | Accessible | Accessible | | **Performance** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | **(7 marks — Awarded for: 7 distinct parameters × 1 mark each, or weighted marking on key parameters)** --- **(b) HDFS Architecture and Replication:** **HDFS Architecture Diagram:** ``` +--------------------+ | NameNode | | (Master - :9000) | +--------------------+ | | | | Heartbeat/Block Reports | | | +---------+ +---------+ +---------+ |DataNode | |DataNode | |DataNode | | (slave1)| | (slave2)| | (slave3)| +---------+ +---------+ +---------+ | Block A | | Block A | | Block A | | Block B | | Block B | | Block B | | Block C | | Block C | | Block C | +---------+ +---------+ +---------+ ``` **Components:** 1. **NameNode (Master):** Stores metadata — filename, block locations, permissions, replication factor. Runs on the master node. 2. **DataNode (Slave):** Stores the actual data blocks. Sends periodic heartbeats (every 3 seconds) and block reports to the NameNode. 3. **Secondary NameNode:** Performs checkpointing — periodically merges the EditLog with the FSImage to prevent the log from growing indefinitely. **Block Replication Process:** - **Default Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x) — previously 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x. - **Default Replication Factor:** **3** (one copy on a local rack, one on a different rack, one on the same different rack — the "Rack Awareness" strategy). When a file is written to HDFS: 1. The client contacts the NameNode for the file's metadata. 2. The file is split into 128 MB blocks. 3. Each block is replicated to 3 different DataNodes (chosen by the NameNode's rack-aware placement policy). 4. The pipeline is closed once all replicas are written and acknowledged. **(7 marks — Awarded for: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks for components, 1 mark for default values, 2 marks for replication process)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing modes:** Many students wrongly state that Standalone Mode uses HDFS — it does NOT. It uses the local file system. > 2. **Wrong default ports:** Writing 50070 instead of 9870 for NameNode Web UI (50070 was the Hadoop 2.x port; Hadoop 3.x uses 9870). > 3. **Omitting `workers` file:** Forgetting to mention the `workers` file in Fully Distributed Mode is a common 2-mark loss. > 4. **Formatting an already-formatted NameNode:** Students sometimes run `hdfs namenode -format` on a running cluster, which corrupts the file system. > 5. **Forgetting SSH setup:** Forgetting password-less SSH configuration between master and slaves is a top reason for `start-dfs.sh` failure in Fully Distributed Mode. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exams):** - **3 Modes:** Standalone (single JVM, local FS), Pseudo-Distributed (single host, all daemons, HDFS), Fully Distributed (multi-host, all daemons, HDFS). - **4 Configuration Files:** `core-site.xml` (defaultFS), `hdfs-site.xml` (replication, name/datanode dirs), `mapred-site.xml` (framework=yarn), `yarn-site.xml` (aux-services, RM hostname). - **Default Port Numbers (Hadoop 3.x):** NameNode Web UI: **9870**, NameNode RPC: **9000**, SecondaryNameNode: **9868**, DataNode: **9864**, ResourceManager: **8088**, NodeManager: **8042**, JobHistory: **19888**. - **Default Block Size:** **128 MB**. - **Default Replication Factor:** **3** in Fully Distributed; **1** in Pseudo-Distributed (since only 1 DataNode exists). - **Critical Commands:** `hdfs namenode -format` (one-time only), `start-dfs.sh`, `start-yarn.sh`, `jps` (verification), `hdfs dfs -put/get/cat/ls`. - **SSH:** Password-less SSH from master to all slaves is **mandatory** in Fully Distributed Mode. - **`workers` file:** Lists all slave hostnames; master uses this to launch daemons on workers via SSH. - **HDFS vs Local FS:** `file:///` = local, `hdfs://` = HDFS — distinguishing these in commands/paths is critical. - **YARN vs Local MR:** `mapreduce.framework.name=yarn` switches the execution engine from local JVM to YARN. - **Rack Awareness:** NameNode's policy of placing replicas across racks for fault tolerance. - **Exam Pattern:** Expect at least one 7-mark question on mode comparison + one 7-mark question on configuration/architecture per exam cycle.

topics.title: Exploring Various Shell Commands in Hadoop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exploring Various Shell Commands in Hadoop # 🔍 Module Experiment 2: Exploring Various Shell Commands in Hadoop ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Hadoop Shell Commands** are a set of command-line utilities provided by the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** that allow users to interact with the distributed file system using a syntax closely resembling standard Unix/Linux shell commands. These commands are executed using the `hdfs dfs` (or legacy `hadoop fs`) interface and operate against files and directories stored across a Hadoop cluster. > [!IMPORTANT] > The underlying engine is the **org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem** Java API, which provides a uniform abstraction over HDFS and local filesystems (`file:///`). Every shell command is internally a Java class implementing `FsShell`. **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard):** Hadoop Shell commands are CLI-based file system operations that enable administrators and developers to perform CRUD-style (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations, manage permissions, inspect storage utilization, and handle data lifecycle tasks within HDFS without writing any Java or MapReduce code. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!TIP] > **🗄️ Real-World Analogy: "The National Warehouse Network"** Imagine you own a chain of **100 warehouses** spread across the country, but you only manage them from a **single office in Kerala**. You cannot physically walk into each warehouse to check inventory, move boxes, or add new stock. What you need is a **remote control** that lets you speak commands like: - *"List all boxes in the Mumbai warehouse"* - *"Move this box from Delhi to Chennai"* - *"How much empty space is left in the Bangalore warehouse?"* That remote control is the **Hadoop Shell**, and the national warehouse network is **HDFS**. The **NameNode** in Hadoop acts like the **head office that knows where every box is stored**, while the **DataNodes** are the actual warehouses storing the data blocks. | 🏢 Real Warehouse | 🐘 Hadoop Equivalent | |---|---| | Central head office | **NameNode** (Master) | | Branch warehouses | **DataNodes** (Workers/Slaves) | | Box labeled with barcode | **Data Block (default 128 MB)** | | Office phone (intercom) | **HDFS Client / Shell** | | Warehouse register | **fsimage + EditLog (Metadata)** | > [!NOTE] > Just as you don't need to know *which specific warehouse* holds a box, HDFS shell commands are **location-transparent** — you simply provide the path, and the NameNode figures out the routing. --- ## 1.3 The HDFS URI Standard Every Hadoop shell command operates on a **Uniform Resource Identifier (URI)** with the following structure: $$ \text{URI} = \texttt{hdfs://}\langle \text{NameNode Host} \rangle\texttt{:}\langle \text{Port} \rangle\texttt{/}\langle \text{Path} \rangle $$ - **Default HDFS Port:** **9000** (internal client RPC) - **Default Web UI Port:** **9870** (NameNode) / **9864** (DataNode) — Hadoop 3.x - **Legacy Web UI Port:** **50070** — Hadoop 2.x > [!WARNING] > In lab examinations, forgetting to start the `namenode` and `datanode` daemons is the **#1 reason** shell commands fail. Always verify with `jps` before running any `hdfs dfs` command. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Table ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How a Shell Command Travels When a student types `hdfs dfs -ls /` in the terminal, the following sequence executes behind the scenes: ### Step-by-Step Logical Flow 1. **Command Parsing** — The `hdfs` script invokes the Java class `org.apache.hadoop.fs.FsShell`, which parses arguments using Apache CLI (Command Line Interpreter). 2. **Configuration Loading** — Reads `core-site.xml` and `hdfs-site.xml` from `$HADOOP_CONF_DIR` to determine the **default filesystem URI** (e.g., `hdfs://localhost:9000`). 3. **Client RPC Handshake** — The HDFS client establishes a **Remote Procedure Call (RPC)** connection to the **NameNode** on port 9000. 4. **Metadata Lookup** — The NameNode consults its in-memory metadata (loaded from `fsimage` and merged with `edits` log) to determine the **block locations** for the requested path. 5. **Data Transfer** — For read/write operations, the client then talks **directly** to the relevant **DataNodes** using the **HDFS Data Transfer Protocol** (TCP, typically port 50010 for data, 50020 for RPC). 6. **Response Rendering** — The output is streamed back to the shell in Unix-style format. > [!IMPORTANT] > The NameNode **never moves data**. It only provides a "map" (metadata) and lets the client talk to DataNodes directly. This is called the **"Metadata-Data Separation Principle"** and is the secret to HDFS scalability. --- ## 2.2 Master Command Reference Table (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The table below consolidates every command validated by the **KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Manual** for this experiment. Memorize the **command name, syntax, and purpose** — the examiner rarely tests the verbose flag. | # | Command | Syntax Pattern | Purpose / Use Case | |:-:|---------|----------------|--------------------| | 1 | `-ls` | `hdfs dfs -ls ` | List files/directories with size, owner, permissions, replication factor | | 2 | `-mkdir` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir ` | Create a new directory (supports `-p` for parents) | | 3 | `-put` / `-copyFromLocal` | `hdfs dfs -put ` | Upload a local file into HDFS | | 4 | `-get` / `-copyToLocal` | `hdfs dfs -get ` | Download a file from HDFS to local filesystem | | 5 | `-cat` | `hdfs dfs -cat ` | Display the **entire content** of a file to stdout | | 6 | `-tail` | `hdfs dfs -tail ` | Display the **last 1 KB** (useful for logs) | | 7 | `-mv` | `hdfs dfs -mv ` | Move a file/directory within HDFS | | 8 | `-cp` | `hdfs dfs -cp ` | Copy a file/directory within HDFS | | 9 | `-rm` | `hdfs dfs -rm ` | Delete a file; use `-r` for recursive directory deletion | | 10 | `-rmdir` | `hdfs dfs -rmdir ` | Remove an **empty** directory only | | 11 | `-du` | `hdfs dfs -du ` | Show **disk usage** (size and its replicated size) | | 12 | `-df` | `hdfs dfs -df ` | Show **filesystem capacity** (free / used / total) | | 13 | `-count` | `hdfs dfs -count ` | Count directories, files, and bytes under a path | | 14 | `-chmod` | `hdfs dfs -chmod ` | Change file permissions (e.g., 755, 644) | | 15 | `-chown` | `hdfs dfs -chown : ` | Change file ownership | | 16 | `-chgrp` | `hdfs dfs -chgrp ` | Change group ownership only | | 17 | `-expunge` | `hdfs dfs -expunge` | Permanently delete files in `.Trash` | | 18 | `-setrep` | `hdfs dfs -setrep ` | Change replication factor of a file | | 19 | `-stat` | `hdfs dfs -stat ` | Print file statistics (block size, modification time) | | 20 | `-test` | `hdfs dfs -test -[ezd] ` | Test existence (`-e`), zero-size (`-z`), directory (`-d`) | | 21 | `-touchz` | `hdfs dfs -touchz ` | Create an **empty** file (zero-byte) | | 22 | `-appendToFile` | `hdfs dfs -appendToFile ` | Append local content to an HDFS file | | 23 | `-moveFromLocal` | `hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal ` | Upload + delete local source atomically | | 24 | `-getmerge` | `hdfs dfs -getmerge ` | Merge multiple HDFS files into one local file | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where are these commands used in production?** > - **Data Ingestion Pipelines**: `put` and `copyFromLocal` are scripted in `cron`/`Oozie` jobs to feed raw data into the data lake. > - **Backup & Archival**: `cp` is used by administrators to replicate critical datasets across clusters. > - **Log Analysis**: `cat` and `tail` are essential for debugging failing YARN/MR jobs by examining driver logs stored in HDFS. > - **Storage Auditing**: `du`, `df`, and `count` enable capacity planning in enterprise clusters that hold petabytes of data. # 3. Step-by-Step Execution Guide & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Pre-Experiment Setup (The KTU Board Expects This in Your Record!) > [!IMPORTANT] > Before any shell command is executed, the Hadoop daemons must be running. This is a **mandatory step** in the KTU Lab Record. ### Step 1 — Verify Java Installation ```bash java -version ``` **Expected Output:** ``` java version "11.0.x" 2024-xx-xx Java(TM) SE Runtime Environment (build 11.0.x+xx) Java HotSpot(TM) 64-Bit Server VM (build 11.0.x+xx, mixed mode) ``` ### Step 2 — Start HDFS Daemons ```bash start-dfs.sh ``` ### Step 3 — Verify Daemons with `jps` ```bash jps ``` > [!NOTE] > The `jps` (Java Virtual Machine Process Status Tool) command should display at minimum: **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **SecondaryNameNode**. Absence of `NameNode` indicates a configuration error. --- ## 3.2 Command-by-Command Exhaustive Demonstration ### 🔹 Command 1: `-ls` (List Operation) **Syntax:** ```bash hdfs dfs -ls / ``` **Detailed Output Analysis:** | Field | Value | Meaning | |-------|-------|---------| | Permissions | `drwxr-xr-x` | `d` = directory, `rwx` = owner, `r-x` = group, `r-x` = others | | Replication | `-` | Directories don't have replication factor | | Owner | `hduser` | Unix user owning the directory | | Group | `supergroup` | Unix group owning the directory | | Size | `0` | Directory metadata size in bytes | | Mod Time | `2024-01-15 10:30` | Last modification timestamp | | Path | `/` | Directory path | > [!TIP] > The `-h` flag (human-readable) makes sizes readable: `hdfs dfs -ls -h /data` --- ### 🔹 Command 2: `-mkdir` (Create Directory) **Syntax (Single Directory):** ```bash hdfs dfs -mkdir /user_input ``` **Syntax (Recursive — Like `mkdir -p`):** ```bash hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /bigdata/lab/module2/experiments ``` > [!WARNING] > HDFS does **NOT** support creating intermediate directories by default. Forgetting the `-p` flag when creating nested paths will throw `FileNotFoundException: Parent directory does not exist`. --- ### 🔹 Command 3: `-put` and `-copyFromLocal` (Upload to HDFS) **Step-by-Step Execution:** ```bash # Step A: Create a sample local file echo "Hello Hadoop World from KTU Lab" > ~/input_data.txt cat ~/input_data.txt ``` ```bash # Step B: Upload using -put hdfs dfs -put ~/input_data.txt /user_input/ ``` ```bash # Step C: Upload using -copyFromLocal hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal ~/input_data.txt /user_input/copy_data.txt ``` ```bash # Step D: Verify upload hdfs dfs -ls /user_input/ ``` **Result of Step D:** ``` Found 2 items -rw-r--r-- 3 hduser supergroup 34 2024-01-15 10:45 /user_input/copy_data.txt -rw-r--r-- 3 hduser supergroup 34 2024-01-15 10:40 /user_input/input_data.txt ``` | Aspect | `-put` | `-copyFromLocal` | |--------|--------|------------------| | Functionality | Identical | Identical | | Stdout/Error Reporting | Verbose | More verbose | | KTU Recommendation | ✅ Acceptable | ✅ Acceptable | | Production Preference | More common | More explicit | > [!NOTE] > Both commands are **functionally equivalent**. The default **replication factor** is **3**, which is why `-rw-r--r-- 3` appears in the listing. --- ### 🔹 Command 4: `-cat` (Read File Content) **Syntax:** ```bash hdfs dfs -cat /user_input/input_data.txt ``` **Output:** ``` Hello Hadoop World from KTU Lab ``` > [!WARNING] > `cat` loads the **entire file** into the driver's memory. For files larger than 1 GB, this can crash the terminal. Use `head`, `tail`, or `hdfs dfs -text` for safer large-file inspection. --- ### 🔹 Command 5: `-get` and `-copyToLocal` (Download from HDFS) **Execution:** ```bash hdfs dfs -get /user_input/input_data.txt ~/downloaded_data.txt ls -la ~/downloaded_data.txt cat ~/downloaded_data.txt ``` > [!TIP] > Use `-getmerge` to download **multiple HDFS part files** (e.g., MapReduce outputs `part-r-00000`, `part-r-00001`) into a single local file — this is a **favourite KTU viva question**. --- ### 🔹 Command 6: `-cp` and `-mv` (Copy / Move Within HDFS) **Execution:** ```bash hdfs dfs -cp /user_input/input_data.txt /bigdata/lab/module2/experiments/data_copy.txt hdfs dfs -mv /user_input/copy_data.txt /bigdata/lab/module2/experiments/ ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Difference**: > - `-cp` — Original remains intact. Two copies exist post-execution. > - `-mv` — Original is **renamed/rehomed**; only one path exists post-execution. > Both operations update the NameNode metadata instantly, which is why HDFS performs them in **O(1) time** (no data is physically moved for `mv`). --- ### 🔹 Command 7: `-rm` and `-rmdir` (Deletion) **Execution:** ```bash hdfs dfs -rm /user_input/input_data.txt # Delete a single file hdfs dfs -rm -r /user_input/ # Recursive delete hdfs dfs -rmdir /bigdata/lab/empty_folder # Only works if directory is empty ``` > [!CAUTION] > - `hdfs dfs -rm` does **NOT** bypass the **Trash (Recycle Bin)** by default. Deleted files move to `/user//.Trash/`. > - Use `hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash ` to **permanently delete** without the recovery option. > - Use `hdfs dfs -expunge` to **manually empty the Trash**. --- ### 🔹 Command 8: `-du` and `-df` (Storage Diagnostics) **Disk Usage of a Specific Path:** ```bash hdfs dfs -du /user_input/ ``` **Output:** ``` 34 102 /user_input/input_data.txt ``` | Column | Value | Meaning | |--------|-------|---------| | 1 (raw size) | `34` | Actual file size in bytes | | 2 (replicated size) | `102` | 34 × 3 (replication factor) | **Filesystem Capacity:** ```bash hdfs dfs -df / ``` **Output:** ``` Filesystem Size Used Available Use% hdfs://localhost:9000 5013423104 81920 4355489792 0% ``` --- ### 🔹 Command 9: `-count` (Path Statistics) ```bash hdfs dfs -count /user_input/ ``` **Output:** ``` 1 1 34 hdfs://localhost:9000/user_input ``` > [!NOTE] > The three numbers represent: **Directory Count, File Count, Total Bytes**. This is the KTU-preferred command for showing storage statistics in lab records. --- ### 🔹 Command 10: `-chmod` and `-chown` (Permission Management) ```bash hdfs dfs -chmod 755 /user_input/input_data.txt hdfs dfs -chown newuser:newgroup /user_input/input_data.txt ``` **Permission Decoding for `755`:** $$ 7_{(8)} = 4_{(r)} + 2_{(w)} + 1_{(x)} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{read, write, execute for owner} $$ | Octal | Binary | Permission | Symbolic | |:-----:|:------:|:----------:|:--------:| | **7** | 111 | rwx | Owner full access | | **5** | 101 | r-x | Group/Others read + execute | | **0** | 000 | --- | No access | > [!WARNING] > HDFS uses **POSIX-style permissions** but **does not support ACLs** in the basic license. For ACLs, you must enable `dfs.namenode.acls.enabled` in `hdfs-site.xml`. --- ### 🔹 Command 11: `-setrep` (Change Replication Factor) ```bash hdfs dfs -setrep 2 /user_input/input_data.txt ``` > [!NOTE] > This command does **not immediately** redistribute data. The NameNode marks the target replication, and the DataNodes converge asynchronously over the next replication interval. --- ## 3.3 Comprehensive Validation Script (Lab Record Final Section) A complete lab record should conclude with a verification pass: ```bash echo "=== Final Verification ===" hdfs dfs -ls -R /bigdata/ echo "=== File Count ===" hdfs dfs -count /bigdata/ echo "=== Disk Usage ===" hdfs dfs -du -h /bigdata/ echo "=== Stopping Daemons ===" stop-dfs.sh ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HDFS Architecture — Command Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph clientLayer["User Terminal Layer"] terminal["Bash Terminal
hdfs dfs -put file.txt /data"] javaAPI["Java FileSystem API
org.apache.hadoop.fs.FileSystem"] end subgraph masterLayer["Master Layer - NameNode"] rpcServer["NameNode RPC Server
Port 9000"] metadata["In-Memory Metadata
fsimage + Edits"] blockMap["Block Map
filename -> BlockIDs -> DataNodes"] end subgraph workerLayer["Worker Layer - DataNodes"] dn1["DataNode 1
Port 50010
/data/fileblk1, blk2"] dn2["DataNode 2
Port 50010
/data/fileblk1, blk3"] dn3["DataNode 3
Port 50010
/data/fileblk2, blk3"] end terminal -->|"1. Parse & Submit"| javaAPI javaAPI -->|"2. RPC Call connect()"| rpcServer rpcServer -->|"3. Query"| metadata metadata -->|"4. Retrieve"| blockMap blockMap -->|"5. Return Block Locations"| javaAPI javaAPI -->|"6. Direct Data Transfer
DFSOutputStream"| dn1 javaAPI -->|"7. Direct Data Transfer"| dn2 javaAPI -->|"8. Direct Data Transfer"| dn3 dn1 -->|"9. Replicate Pipeline"| dn2 dn2 -->|"10. Replicate Pipeline"| dn3 dn1 -->|"11. ACK Success"| javaAPI dn2 -->|"12. ACK Success"| javaAPI dn3 -->|"13. ACK Success"| javaAPI javaAPI -->|"14. Unregister Block"| rpcServer style clientLayer fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style masterLayer fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style workerLayer fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style terminal fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0 style rpcServer fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17 style dn1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 style dn2 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 style dn3 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram**: Notice the client talks to the NameNode (steps 2–5) **only for metadata**, then directly streams data to a pipeline of DataNodes (steps 6–10). The NameNode never sees the data bytes — only block locations. This is the **classic HDFS write path**. --- ## 4.2 Command Classification Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph groupA["Group A - File Content Operations"] cmd1["-cat"] cmd2["-text"] cmd3["-tail"] cmd4["-head"] end subgraph groupB["Group B - Directory Navigation"] cmd5["-ls"] cmd6["-mkdir"] cmd7["-du"] cmd8["-df"] cmd9["-count"] cmd10["-stat"] end subgraph groupC["Group C - Data Movement"] cmd11["-put"] cmd12["-get"] cmd13["-copyFromLocal"] cmd14["-copyToLocal"] cmd15["-moveFromLocal"] cmd16["-getmerge"] end subgraph groupD["Group D - File Management"] cmd17["-cp"] cmd18["-mv"] cmd19["-rm"] cmd20["-rmdir"] cmd21["-expunge"] cmd22["-touchz"] end subgraph groupE["Group E - Security and Admin"] cmd23["-chmod"] cmd24["-chown"] cmd25["-chgrp"] cmd26["-setrep"] cmd27["-test"] end shellHDFS["HDFS Shell
hdfs dfs"] --> groupA shellHDFS --> groupB shellHDFS --> groupC shellHDFS --> groupD shellHDFS --> groupE style shellHDFS fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style groupA fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style groupB fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#1a237e style groupC fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40 style groupD fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style groupE fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Data Flow for `hdfs dfs -put` ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant U as User Terminal participant C as HDFS Client participant NN as NameNode participant DN1 as DataNode 1 participant DN2 as DataNode 2 participant DN3 as DataNode 3 U->>C: hdfs dfs -put local.txt /data/ C->>NN: RPC: create(/data/local.txt) NN-->>C: OK (allocate blockID: blk_001) Note over C,DN1: Client splits file into 128 MB blocks C->>DN1: WRITE blk_001 packet 1 DN1->>DN2: Replicate packet 1 DN2->>DN3: Replicate packet 1 DN3-->>DN2: ACK DN2-->>DN1: ACK DN1-->>C: ACK C->>NN: complete(/data/local.txt) NN-->>C: Success - file closed C-->>U: Command completed ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > Step **Autonumber 6** — DataNodes form a **replication pipeline** in the order specified by the NameNode's rack-awareness policy. The ACK flows back upstream to the client. This is the **write-pipeline topology**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** ### **Q1. Differentiate between `hdfs dfs -put` and `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal`. Are they functionally equivalent?** **Model Answer:** Yes, `hdfs dfs -put` and `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal` are **functionally equivalent**. Both commands copy a file from the local filesystem to HDFS. The only minor difference is that `-copyFromLocal` is more **explicit** about its source being local, while `-put` can also accept HDFS-to-HDFS paths in some versions. In KTU lab evaluations, both are accepted interchangeably. > `hdfs dfs -put ~/file.txt /data/` ← ✅ Valid > `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal ~/file.txt /data/` ← ✅ Valid --- ### **Q2. What is the role of the `NameNode` when you execute `hdfs dfs -ls /`?** **Model Answer:** The `NameNode` acts as the **central metadata server**. When `hdfs dfs -ls /` is executed, the HDFS client contacts the NameNode via RPC on port **9000**. The NameNode queries its in-memory metadata (constructed from `fsimage` + `edits` log) to retrieve the directory listing, and returns it to the client. **The NameNode does not store actual data** — it only stores the *location information* (which DataNodes hold which blocks). --- ## 📗 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** List any **seven (7)** Hadoop shell commands with their syntax and one-line purpose. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Demonstrate the complete procedure to **(i)** create a directory `/ktu/lab/module2`, **(ii)** upload a local file `student.txt` into it, **(iii)** verify its replication factor, **(iv)** download it back locally, and **(v)** permanently delete it. Include the **exact output** at each step. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(a):** | # | Command | Syntax | Purpose | |:-:|---------|--------|---------| | 1 | `-ls` | `hdfs dfs -ls ` | Display file/directory metadata | | 2 | `-mkdir` | `hdfs dfs -mkdir -p ` | Create directories (recursive with `-p`) | | 3 | `-put` | `hdfs dfs -put ` | Upload local file to HDFS | | 4 | `-cat` | `hdfs dfs -cat ` | Print file contents to stdout | | 5 | `-rm` | `hdfs dfs -rm -r ` | Recursively delete a file/directory | | 6 | `-chmod` | `hdfs dfs -chmod 755 ` | Change file permissions (Octal mode) | | 7 | `-du` | `hdfs dfs -du -h ` | Show disk usage in human-readable format | --- #### **Model Solution for Question A(b):** **Step 1 — Create the directory:** ```bash hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /ktu/lab/module2 ``` **Output:** ``` (No output on success) ``` **Step 2 — Create a sample local file:** ```bash echo "Name: Rahul, RegNo: KTU2024B57" > ~/student.txt ``` **Step 3 — Upload using `put`:** ```bash hdfs dfs -put ~/student.txt /ktu/lab/module2/ ``` **Output:** ``` (No output on success) ``` **Step 4 — Verify replication factor:** ```bash hdfs dfs -ls /ktu/lab/module2/ ``` **Output:** ``` Found 1 items -rw-r--r-- 3 hduser supergroup 33 2024-05-12 14:22 /ktu/lab/module2/student.txt ``` > 📌 **Reading the output**: The `3` after permissions indicates the **replication factor = 3** (default in `hdfs-site.xml`). **Step 5 — Download back locally:** ```bash hdfs dfs -get /ktu/lab/module2/student.txt ~/student_downloaded.txt cat ~/student_downloaded.txt ``` **Output:** ``` Name: Rahul, RegNo: KTU2024B57 ``` **Step 6 — Permanent deletion (bypassing Trash):** ```bash hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash /ktu/lab/module2/student.txt ``` **Output:** ``` Deleted /ktu/lab/module2/student.txt ``` **Step 7 — Verify deletion:** ```bash hdfs dfs -ls /ktu/lab/module2/ ``` **Output:** ``` (Empty result - file deleted) ``` --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a labelled diagram. Describe the role of **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode** in processing a shell command. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Compare and contrast the following command pairs: **(i)** `-put` vs `-moveFromLocal`, **(ii)** `-cp` vs `-mv`, **(iii)** `-rm` vs `-rmdir`, **(iv)** `-du` vs `-df`, **(v)** `-chmod 644` vs `-chmod 755`. **[7 Marks]** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(a):** **HDFS follows a Master-Slave (Master-Worker) architecture.** **1. NameNode (Master):** - Stores **metadata** in RAM: filename → block IDs → DataNode locations - Receives all client RPC requests on **port 9000** - Persists metadata via `fsimage` (snapshot) + `edits` log - **Single point of failure** in Hadoop 2.x; resolved by HA (High Availability) in Hadoop 3.x **2. DataNode (Worker):** - Stores **actual data blocks** (default size: **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x, configurable) - Sends **heartbeat signals** to NameNode every **3 seconds** by default - Default storage port: **50010** for data, **50020** for internal RPC **3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):** - **Misnomer** — it is NOT a backup NameNode - Periodically merges `fsimage` + `edits` into a new `fsimage` (default: every 1 hour or when edits reach 64 MB) - Prevents the edits log from growing unbounded - In Hadoop 3.x, this role is replaced by the **CheckpointNode** or **BackupNode** **Command Flow Example** — `hdfs dfs -cat /file.txt`: 1. Client → NameNode (RPC on port 9000): "Where is /file.txt?" 2. NameNode → Client: "Block blk_001 is on DN1, DN2, DN3" 3. Client → DN1 (closest one): "Send me blk_001" 4. DN1 → Client: streams the data 5. Client renders the content to stdout --- #### **Model Solution for Question B(b):** | Pair | Comparison | |------|------------| | **(i) `-put` vs `-moveFromLocal`** | `-put` copies the file; local source remains. `-moveFromLocal` copies **and deletes** the local file in the same atomic operation. | | **(ii) `-cp` vs `-mv`** | `-cp` creates a duplicate (source intact). `-mv` relocates/renames (source gone). Both are O(1) NameNode operations. | | **(iii) `-rm` vs `-rmdir`** | `-rm` deletes files or non-empty directories (with `-r`). `-rmdir` only deletes **empty** directories. | | **(iv) `-du` vs `-df`** | `-du` shows size consumed by a **specific path** (raw & replicated). `-df` shows **overall filesystem capacity** (used / free / total). | | **(v) `-chmod 644` vs `-chmod 755`** | `644` = owner can read+write (`rw-`), group/others read-only (`r--`). `755` = owner read+write+execute (`rwx`), group/others read+execute (`r-x`). | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > > 1. **Forgetting `start-dfs.sh`**: Running shell commands without starting daemons results in `ConnectionRefusedException`. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if you don't show `jps` output as proof. > > 2. **Missing `-p` flag with `mkdir`**: When creating nested paths, omitting `-p` causes `No such file or directory` errors. **Always use `hdfs dfs -mkdir -p`** for multi-level directories. > > 3. **Confusing `-rm` with `-rmdir`**: Trying to delete a non-empty directory with `-rmdir` fails. Use `-rm -r` for recursive deletion. > > 4. **Ignoring replication factor**: Students often state "uploaded successfully" but fail to show the `-ls` output proving replication. Always include the **full permission block** (`-rw-r--r-- 3 hduser supergroup ...`) in your answer. > > 5. **Using absolute vs relative paths incorrectly**: HDFS paths must start with `/` (e.g., `/ktu/lab`), not `~/ktu/lab` — the tilde is a local-filesystem shortcut only. > > 6. **No mention of HDFS ports**: A common viva question is "What is the default port of NameNode?" Answer: **9000** (RPC) and **9870** (Web UI for Hadoop 3.x). --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 🎯 **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read Before the Exam:** - [x] **Hadoop Shell** = CLI tool (`hdfs dfs` or legacy `hadoop fs`) for HDFS operations. - [x] **NameNode** stores metadata only; **DataNodes** store actual block data (default **128 MB** per block). - [x] Default **replication factor = 3**; default **block size = 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x). - [x] NameNode RPC port: **9000**; Web UI: **9870** (Hadoop 3.x). - [x] Always run **`start-dfs.sh`** and verify with **`jps`** before any shell command. - [x] `-put` ≡ `-copyFromLocal`; `-get` ≡ `-copyToLocal`. - [x] Use **`-mkdir -p`** for nested directories; HDFS does not auto-create parents. - [x] `-mv` and `-cp` are **O(1)** NameNode operations; no actual data is shifted for `mv`. - [x] `-rm` moves files to **`.Trash/`** by default. Use `-skipTrash` for permanent deletion. - [x] `-du` shows **per-path usage**; `-df` shows **cluster-wide capacity**. - [x] `-chmod 755` = `rwxr-xr-x`; `-chmod 644` = `rw-r--r--`. - [x] `-setrep ` changes replication factor but the change is **asynchronous**. - [x] HDFS uses **rack-awareness** to place replicas across different racks for fault tolerance. - [x] `Secondary NameNode` is **NOT a hot standby**; it is a checkpoint helper. - [x] Always include **output screenshots or text** in lab records — KTU examiners value empirical proof.

topics.title: File Management Tasks in Hadoop: Adding, Retrieving, and Deleting Files and Directories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** File Management Tasks in Hadoop: Adding, Retrieving, and Deleting Files and Directories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** is the primary storage layer of the Apache Hadoop framework, designed to store very large files across multiple commodity machines in a fault-tolerant manner. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Big Data Processing Lab (PCCDL607)*, the **File Management Tasks in HDFS** encompass a set of shell-level and Java API operations used to *add (write/ingest)*, *retrieve (read/download)*, and *delete (remove/expunge)* both individual files and entire directory hierarchies in the distributed namespace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** Students must be able to demonstrate proficiency with the `hadoop fs` and `hdfs dfs` command-line interface, including mastering at least the following operations: `put`, `get`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`, `rmr`, `mkdir`, `ls`, `cat`, `du`, `df`, `chmod`, `chown`, and `expunge`.

HDFS follows a **write-once, read-many (WORM)** model. Once a file is written and closed, it is generally not modified. This design trade-off dramatically simplifies data coherency and enables high throughput for batch processing engines such as **MapReduce**, **Spark**, and **Hive**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nationwide Library" Intuition

Imagine HDFS as a **gigantic nationwide library system**:

- The **NameNode** is the **central librarian's index card cabinet** — it does not store the books, only the *metadata* (title, shelf location, author, number of copies).
- The **DataNodes** are the **branch libraries spread across the country** — they actually store the physical books (data blocks).
- A **block** is like a **chapter of a book** — HDFS splits every large file into chunks (default **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x/3.x), and each chapter is replicated (default **3 copies**) across multiple branches.
- When you **"put"** a book into the system, the librarian stamps three copies and ships them to three different branch libraries.
- When you **"get"** the book back, the librarian locates the nearest copy and lends it to you.
- When you **"delete"** the book, the librarian marks the index card as "trash", and a janitor (`expunge`) physically incinerates the copies at the end of the day.

This mental model makes the HDFS command set immediately intuitive: every command is essentially an instruction sent to the librarian (NameNode), which then coordinates the branch libraries (DataNodes) accordingly.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** The examiner expects you to launch the DFS (`start-dfs.sh`), verify the safe-mode status of the NameNode, perform all CRUD-like file operations, and finally verify the **Web UI** at `http://localhost:50070` (Hadoop 2.x) or `http://localhost:9870` (Hadoop 3.x) before submitting your lab record.

## 1.3 Why File Management in HDFS is Critical

| Real-World Engineering Need | HDFS Feature That Addresses It |
|---|---|
| Storing petabytes of sensor/IoT logs | Horizontal scaling across DataNodes |
| Surviving disk/hardware failures | Block replication (default factor = 3) |
| Streaming huge files at GB/sec | Block-sequential, append-only writes |
| Co-locating computation with data | Data locality awareness |
| Auditing and access control | POSIX-style permissions (`chmod`, `chown`) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Command Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Foundation for File Management

HDFS exposes a **hierarchical file system view** (`/`, `/user`, `/tmp`, `/data`, …) to the client even though the underlying data is sharded across hundreds of physical disks. Every file operation traverses the following logical pipeline:

1. **Client RPC**: The user invokes `hadoop fs -put localFile /hdfs/path/`.
2. **NameNode Negotiation**: The NameNode checks permissions, namespace, and available DataNodes.
3. **Pipeline Construction**: A write pipeline of DataNodes is established (e.g., DN1 → DN2 → DN3).
4. **Block Streaming**: The client streams 128 MB blocks into the pipeline, with packet-level ACKs.
5. **Acknowledgment**: On success, the NameNode commits the block locations to its metadata.

> [!TIP]
> **Why two command prefixes — `hadoop fs` vs `hdfs dfs`?**
> - `hadoop fs` is a **generic** FileSystem shell that can target *any* Hadoop-supported FS (HDFS, S3, local, …).
> - `hdfs dfs` is **HDFS-specific** and is the **recommended** prefix per the official Apache documentation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Command Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **canonical revision grid** for the KTU lab viva and semester exam. It is intentionally presented in a pipe-free format to survive markdown rendering.

| Command (Syntax) | Operation Category | Effect on HDFS | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>` | Add (Ingest) | Uploads local file to HDFS path | Loading CSV/log files into `/user/student/` |
| `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal <local> <hdfs>` | Add (Ingest) | Like `-put`, but local copy remains intact | Copying reference datasets without losing originals |
| `hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal <local> <hdfs>` | Add (Ingest) | Like `-put`, but **deletes** the local file after upload | Freeing local disk after secure ingest |
| `hdfs dfs -get <hdfs> <local>` | Retrieve (Download) | Downloads HDFS file to local FS | Pulling processed output for inspection |
| `hdfs dfs -copyToLocal <hdfs> <local>` | Retrieve (Download) | Like `-get`, local file is *not* deleted | Mirroring output to backup folder |
| `hdfs dfs -cat <hdfs>` | Retrieve (View) | Prints entire file to stdout | Quick content verification |
| `hdfs dfs -text <hdfs>` | Retrieve (View) | Like `-cat`, but supports compressed formats (`.gz`, `.bz2`) | Viewing compressed SequenceFiles |
| `hdfs dfs -tail <hdfs>` | Retrieve (View) | Prints last 1 KB of file | Live log monitoring |
| `hdfs dfs -ls <hdfs_path>` | Inspect | Lists files/directories | Verifying uploads |
| `hdfs dfs -mkdir <hdfs_dir>` | Add Directory | Creates one or more directories (use `-p` for parents) | Organizing datasets by year/month |
| `hdfs dfs -rm <hdfs>` | Delete (Move to Trash) | Removes a **file**; HDFS supports an optional trash facility | Cleaning intermediate output |
| `hdfs dfs -rm -r <hdfs_dir>` | Delete (Recursive) | Recursively removes files and directories | Wiping an entire experiment output folder |
| `hdfs dfs -rm -skipTrash <hdfs>` | Delete (Permanent) | Bypasses trash; permanent deletion | Freeing space urgently |
| `hdfs dfs -expunge` | Delete (Vacuum) | Empties the `.Trash` directory | Forcing trash collection |
| `hdfs dfs -mv <src> <dst>` | Move / Rename | Atomically renames files/directories within HDFS | Renaming output folders |
| `hdfs dfs -cp <src> <dst>` | Copy | Duplicates files/directories within HDFS | Creating replicas for parallel experiments |
| `hdfs dfs -du <hdfs>` | Inspect | Shows disk usage of files/dirs | Quota management |
| `hdfs dfs -df <hdfs>` | Inspect | Shows **free** space on the HDFS filesystem | Pre-flight capacity check |
| `hdfs dfs -count <hdfs>` | Inspect | Counts dirs, files, and bytes | Sanity check before MapReduce job |
| `hdfs dfs -chmod <mode> <hdfs>` | Permissions | Changes POSIX permissions (`755`, `644`, …) | Restricting read/write access |
| `hdfs dfs -chown <owner:group> <hdfs>` | Permissions | Changes ownership | Multi-user lab environments |
| `hdfs dfs -chgrp <group> <hdfs>` | Permissions | Changes group only | Lab group policies |

## 2.3 Core Engineering Concepts Behind the Commands

### 2.3.1 Block Size & Replication Math

The **effective storage footprint** of a file in HDFS is given by:

$$ \text{Total Cluster Storage} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \lceil \frac{\text{Size}(F_i)}{\text{BlockSize}} \rceil \times \text{ReplicationFactor} \times \text{BlockSize} \right) $$

where:
- $N$ = number of distinct files in the namespace
- $F_i$ = size of the $i$-th file
- $\text{BlockSize}$ = default **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x; configurable via `dfs.blocksize`)
- $\text{ReplicationFactor}$ = default **3** (configurable via `dfs.replication`)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Insight:** A 300 MB file with default settings occupies ⌈300/128⌉ × 3 × 128 MB = **3 × 128 MB = 384 MB** on the cluster. The rounding-up at block granularity is why HDFS *always* over-allocates slightly per file.

### 2.3.2 The HDFS Trash Mechanism

HDFS has a **server-side trash** analogous to the Linux desktop trash. By default, `fs.trash.interval = 1` day. When a user runs `-rm`, the file is moved to `/user/<username>/.Trash/Current/`. To permanently free space:

1. Delete with `-rm` (file goes to trash).
2. Wait for the trash interval, OR
3. Run `hdfs dfs -expunge` to force an immediate vacuum.

This two-stage deletion is a **safety net** that prevents accidental data loss — a frequent KTU viva question.

### 2.3.3 Return Codes and Exit Semantics

| Exit Code | Meaning | KTU Exam Implication |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| `0` | Success | Command executed; HDFS metadata updated |
| `1` | Generic failure | Network error / NameNode unreachable |
| `2` | Filesystem error | Path not found, permission denied |
| `-1` | Unknown exception | Corrupt block, checksum mismatch |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often assume `-rm` is *permanent*. In KTU 2024 lab evaluations, you will be **marked down** if you cannot explain the trash mechanism and the role of `fs.trash.interval`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Execution Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Flight Checklist (Common to All Operations)

Before running any HDFS file management command, the student must:

1. **Start the HDFS daemons**:
   ```bash
   start-dfs.sh
   ```
2. **Verify NameNode is out of safe mode**:
   ```bash
   hdfs dfsadmin -safemode get
   ```
   Expected output: `Safe mode is OFF`.
3. **Confirm DataNode liveness**:
   ```bash
   hdfs dfsadmin -report
   ```
   Look for `Live DataNodes: 1` (or more).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the NameNode is in **safe mode**, HDFS becomes *read-only*. You will be able to `cat` and `get`, but `-put` and `-mkdir` will fail with `org.apache.hadoop.hdfs.server.namenode.SafeModeException`.

## 3.2 Walkthrough 1 — Adding Files (PUT / copyFromLocal / moveFromLocal)

### Step-by-Step Execution

```bash
# Step 1: Create a sample local file
echo "KTU Big Data Lab - Module 2 Experiment" > /home/student/lab_input.txt

# Step 2: Verify local file exists
ls -l /home/student/lab_input.txt

# Step 3: Create an HDFS directory tree
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/student/experiment2

# Step 4: Upload the file (HDFS retains local copy)
hdfs dfs -put /home/student/lab_input.txt /user/student/experiment2/

# Step 5: Verify the upload
hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/experiment2/

# Step 6: Check HDFS-wide statistics
hdfs dfs -df -h
hdfs dfs -du -h /user/student/experiment2/
```

### Analytical Trace

| Step | Action | NameNode Operation | DataNode Operation |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Local file creation | None | None |
| 2 | `ls -l` | None | None |
| 3 | `mkdir -p` | Allocates namespace inode | None |
| 4 | `-put` | Validates path, picks DN pipeline | Streams 128 MB blocks; replicates × 3 |
| 5 | `-ls` | Returns file metadata (size, owner, replication) | None |
| 6 | `-df`, `-du` | Aggregates Capacity / Used / Remaining | Reads per-block length stats |

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge Case:** If you `-put` a 50 MB file, only **1 block** is created (since 50 < 128). The replication logic still applies — three identical 50 MB blocks live on three different DataNodes.

## 3.3 Walkthrough 2 — Retrieving Files (GET / cat / tail)

### Step-by-Step Execution

```bash
# Step 1: Print the file to terminal (no local copy)
hdfs dfs -cat /user/student/experiment2/lab_input.txt

# Step 2: View the last 1 KB
hdfs dfs -tail /user/student/experiment2/lab_input.txt

# Step 3: Download to local filesystem
hdfs dfs -get /user/student/experiment2/lab_input.txt /tmp/lab_input_copy.txt

# Step 4: Verify the download
ls -l /tmp/lab_input_copy.txt
diff /home/student/lab_input.txt /tmp/lab_input_copy.txt
```

### Analytical Trace

| Step | Command | Direction | Caching Effect |
|:---:|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | `-cat` | HDFS → stdout (no local write) | DFSInputStream opened, block read sequentially |
| 2 | `-tail` | HDFS → stdout (last 1 KB) | Opens last block, reads from offset `size - 1024` |
| 3 | `-get` | HDFS → local file | Identical data; `CRC32` checksum verified at end |
| 4 | `diff` | local ↔ local | Should be empty → confirms **data integrity** |

> [!TIP]
> For very large files, **always** prefer `hdfs dfs -text <file>` over `-cat` if the file might be compressed with `GzipCodec`, `Bzip2Codec`, or stored as a `SequenceFile` / `Avro` container.

## 3.4 Walkthrough 3 — Deleting Files and Directories (rm / rmr / expunge)

### Step-by-Step Execution

```bash
# Step 1: Soft delete (file moves to trash)
hdfs dfs -rm /user/student/experiment2/lab_input.txt

# Step 2: Confirm file is in trash, not in original location
hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/experiment2/
hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/.Trash/Current/

# Step 3: Recursively remove a directory
hdfs dfs -rm -r /user/student/experiment2

# Step 4: Force-vacuum the trash
hdfs dfs -expunge
hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/.Trash/
```

### Analytical Trace

| Step | Command | Effect on NameNode | Effect on DataNodes | Reversible? |
|:---:|---|---|---|:---:|
| 1 | `-rm` | INode marked deleted in namespace; new INode created under `.Trash` | Blocks **not** freed yet | ✅ Yes (from `.Trash`) |
| 2 | `ls` | Read-only metadata query | None | N/A |
| 3 | `-rm -r` | Walks tree, moves every leaf into `.Trash` | Blocks reserved, not released | ✅ Yes |
| 4 | `-expunge` | Permanently deletes `.Trash` checkpoint INodes | Sends `deleteBlock` RPC; disk freed | ❌ No |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Viva Point:** After `-expunge`, the HDFS `fs.trash.interval` countdown resets. HDFS uses a **checkpoint-based** trash, not a per-file TTL. This is the *exact* reason why `-expunge` is sometimes called the *"Janitor"* operation.

## 3.5 Walkthrough 4 — Combined End-to-End Lab Script

The following is the **canonical KTU Module-2 experiment** shell transcript. Master this and you can answer any viva question on the topic.

```bash
# --- A. START HDFS ---
start-dfs.sh
jps

# --- B. CREATE LOCAL WORK FILE ---
mkdir -p /home/student/exp2
echo "Big Data Lab | KTU 2024 Scheme"   > /home/student/exp2/data1.txt
echo "Module 2: HDFS File Management"   > /home/student/exp2/data2.txt
echo "Block Size = 128 MB | Rep = 3"    > /home/student/exp2/data3.txt

# --- C. CREATE HDFS TREE ---
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /ktu/lab/exp2/input
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /ktu/lab/exp2/output

# --- D. UPLOAD ---
hdfs dfs -put /home/student/exp2/data1.txt /ktu/lab/exp2/input/
hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal /home/student/exp2/data2.txt /ktu/lab/exp2/input/
hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal /home/student/exp2/data3.txt /ktu/lab/exp2/input/

# --- E. VERIFY ---
hdfs dfs -ls /ktu/lab/exp2/input/
hdfs dfs -count /ktu/lab/exp2/input/
hdfs dfs -du -h /ktu/lab/exp2/input/

# --- F. RETRIEVE & VERIFY CONTENT ---
hdfs dfs -cat /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data1.txt
hdfs dfs -get /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data1.txt /tmp/data1_verify.txt
diff /home/student/exp2/data1.txt /tmp/data1_verify.txt

# --- G. RENAME / COPY ---
hdfs dfs -mv  /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data1.txt /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data1_renamed.txt
hdfs dfs -cp  /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data2.txt /ktu/lab/exp2/output/

# --- H. PERMISSIONS ---
hdfs dfs -chmod 640 /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data1_renamed.txt
hdfs dfs -chown student:supergroup /ktu/lab/exp2/output/data2.txt

# --- I. DELETE ---
hdfs dfs -rm /ktu/lab/exp2/input/data2.txt
hdfs dfs -rm -r /ktu/lab/exp2/output
hdfs dfs -expunge
```

### Verification Matrix (Expected Output Shapes)

| Command | Expected Output Snippet | What It Proves |
|---|---|---|
| `hdfs dfs -ls /ktu/lab/exp2/input/` | `-rw-r--r-- 1 student supergroup 38 2024-... data1_renamed.txt` | File exists, correct permissions after `chmod` |
| `hdfs dfs -count /ktu/lab/exp2/input/` | `1 1 38 /ktu/lab/exp2/input` | 1 dir, 1 file, 38 bytes |
| `hdfs dfs -du -h /ktu/lab/exp2/input/` | `38 114 /ktu/lab/exp2/input` | Logical 38 B, replicated 114 B (× 3) |
| `hdfs dfs -df -h` | `Filesystem Size Used Available Use% ...` | Cluster capacity snapshot |
| `diff ...` | *(no output)* | Local ↔ HDFS content is byte-identical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 38 B vs 114 B anomaly:** `du` shows *logical* size (38 B) on the left and *replicated* size (114 B = 38 × 3) on the right. This is a **favourite KTU viva question**.

## 3.6 Hands-On Variant: Using Java FileSystem API (Conceptual)

Although the KTU lab is shell-driven, examiners occasionally ask the Java equivalent. The conceptual skeleton is:

```java
Configuration conf = new Configuration();
FileSystem fs = FileSystem.get(conf);

// ADD
fs.copyFromLocalFile(new Path("/local/data.txt"),
                     new Path("/ktu/lab/exp2/data.txt"));

// RETRIEVE
fs.copyToLocalFile(new Path("/ktu/lab/exp2/data.txt"),
                   new Path("/local/data_copy.txt"));

// DELETE
fs.delete(new Path("/ktu/lab/exp2/data.txt"), false);   // single file
fs.delete(new Path("/ktu/lab/exp2/output"),    true);   // recursive
```

> [!NOTE]
> The third argument of `fs.delete(path, recursive)` is the **only** difference between deleting a file and deleting a directory in the Java API. Setting it to `false` on a non-empty directory throws `IOException: Path is not empty`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS File-Write Pipeline (PUT Operation)

The diagram below traces a single `-put` call from the shell down to the disk blocks on three DataNodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Shell: hadoop fs -put] --> B[DistributedFileSystem API]
    B --> C[NameNode: create RPC]
    C --> D{NameNode picks a 3-Node Pipeline}
    D --> E[DataNode 1]
    D --> F[DataNode 2]
    D --> G[DataNode 3]
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H[Persist Block to Local Disk]
    H --> I[Ack back to Client]
    I --> J[NameNode Commits Block Locations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The client streams packets to DN1, which forwards to DN2, then DN3. ACKs travel the *reverse* direction. This is the HDFS **write pipeline** that gives HDFS its high throughput.

## 4.2 Command Categorization Tree

```mermaid
mindmap
    root((HDFS File Management Commands))
        AddIngest
            put
            copyFromLocal
            moveFromLocal
        RetrieveView
            get
            copyToLocal
            cat
            text
            tail
        DeletePurge
            rm
            rmRecursive
            expunge
        InspectAdmin
            ls
            du
            df
            count
        Permission
            chmod
            chown
            chgrp
        DirectoryOps
            mkdir
            mv
            cp
```

## 4.3 End-to-End Lab Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLIENT[Client Workstation]
        LFS[Local File System: tmp or home]
        SHELL[hadoop fs shell]
    end

    subgraph NN[NameNode Server]
        META[Metadata: fsimage + edits]
        NS[Namespace Tree: hdfs: ktu lab exp2]
    end

    subgraph DN[DataNode Cluster]
        DN1[DataNode 1 Disk]
        DN2[DataNode 2 Disk]
        DN3[DataNode 3 Disk]
    end

    SHELL -->|RPC: create, mkdir| META
    SHELL -->|RPC: get, cat| META
    META -->|Block Locations| SHELL
    SHELL -->|Block Stream x 3| DN1
    SHELL -->|Block Stream x 3| DN2
    SHELL -->|Block Stream x 3| DN3
    LFS -->|put source| SHELL
    SHELL -->|get destination| LFS
    NS -->|HDFS path tree| META
```

## 4.4 Delete Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Present
    Present --> InTrash: rm
    Present --> PermanentlyGone: rm skipTrash
    InTrash --> Present: restore from Trash
    InTrash --> PermanentlyGone: expunge
    InTrash --> PermanentlyGone: trash interval expires
    PermanentlyGone --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **State Interpretation:** `InTrash` is the only recoverable state. `PermanentlyGone` is final and the block storage is released to the DataNode's free pool. This is the canonical lifecycle for KTU viva explanations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between `hdfs dfs -put` and `hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal`. What happens to the local file in each case?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `-put` | `-copyFromLocal` |
|---|---|---|
| Source filesystem | Any URI scheme | **Restricted to local FS only** |
| Behaviour on local file | **Local file is deleted** (deprecated behaviour) | **Local file is preserved** |
| Modern recommended use | Generic upload | Explicit *"copy up, keep original"* |

> Both commands ultimately perform the same RPC sequence against the NameNode. The difference is purely in the **client-side handling** of the source file. *(1 mark for the table; 1 mark for the delete behaviour; 1 mark for RPC equivalence.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`
**What is the role of the HDFS trash checkpoint, and how does `hdfs dfs -expunge` relate to it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The **HDFS trash** is a server-side safety net that intercepts `-rm` operations and moves files into `/user/<user>/.Trash/Current/` instead of immediately freeing their blocks. *(1 mark)*
2. The trash uses a **checkpoint** model rather than a per-file TTL; `fs.trash.checkpoint.interval` (default 0) determines when a new checkpoint is created. *(1 mark)*
3. The `hdfs dfs -expunge` command forces an **immediate checkpoint rotation and deletion** of all files in the trash, freeing disk space on the DataNodes. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Question A:**
**(a)** List and explain any **six** file-management commands in HDFS that are used to add files, with a sample invocation for each. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Describe the **HDFS write pipeline** that executes when a client invokes `hdfs dfs -put`. Include a sketch of the DataNode interaction. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The six "add" commands are:

1. **`hdfs dfs -put <local> <hdfs>`** — Uploads a local file to HDFS.
   ```bash
   hdfs dfs -put /home/student/sales.csv /ktu/data/input/
   ```
2. **`hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal <local> <hdfs>`** — Same as `-put` but **does not** delete the local file.
   ```bash
   hdfs dfs -copyFromLocal /home/student/products.csv /ktu/data/input/
   ```
3. **`hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal <local> <hdfs>`** — Uploads and **deletes** the local file (useful for staging directories).
   ```bash
   hdfs dfs -moveFromLocal /home/student/archive.csv /ktu/data/input/
   ```
4. **`hdfs dfs -appendToFile <local> <hdfs>`** — Appends a local file to an existing HDFS file.
   ```bash
   hdfs dfs -appendToFile /home/student/log_part2.txt /ktu/data/input/master.log
   ```
5. **`hdfs dfs -cp <src> <dst>`** — Copies a file *within HDFS*.
   ```bash
   hdfs dfs -cp /ktu/data/input/master.log /ktu/data/backup/
   ```
6. **`hdfs dfs -mv <src> <dst>`** — Renames or moves a file *within HDFS* (atomic, single-NameNode edit log entry).
