   ```bash
   hdfs dfs -mv /ktu/data/input/old.log /ktu/data/input/new.log
   ```

> **Mark distribution:** 1 mark per command (name + example) ≈ 6 marks; remaining 1 mark for the **explanation** of how each one differs in local-file handling.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

The HDFS write pipeline is an **ordered chain of DataNodes** that handles block replication. The sequence is:

1. **Client creates a `DFSOutputStream`** by calling `DistributedFileSystem.create()`.
2. The **NameNode** receives an `addBlock` request, picks *three* DataNodes based on rack-awareness, and returns their hostnames to the client.
3. The client organizes them as a **pipeline**: DN_A → DN_B → DN_C.
4. The client streams **packets (64 KB each)** into DN_A, which forwards each packet to DN_B, which forwards to DN_C.
5. Each DataNode persists the packet to its local disk and sends an **ACK packet** up the chain.
6. When a block is full (default 128 MB), the client requests a **new block** from the NameNode and a new pipeline is constructed.
7. On `close()`, the NameNode commits all block locations into the **fsimage/edit log**.

**Pipeline Sketch:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Client] --> P1[DataNode A]
    P1 --> P2[DataNode B]
    P2 --> P3[DataNode C]
    P3 --> ACK3[ACK to B]
    ACK3 --> ACK2[ACK to A]
    ACK2 --> ACK1[ACK to Client]
```

> **Mark distribution:** 4 marks for the step-by-step explanation; 2 marks for the sketch; 1 mark for the final NameNode commit step.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Question B:**
**(a)** Explain the **HDFS delete lifecycle**, including the role of the **trash checkpoint**, `fs.trash.interval`, and the `expunge` command. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Write and explain a **complete shell script** that performs the following: (i) starts HDFS, (ii) uploads a 250 MB log file, (iii) verifies its size and replication, (iv) downloads a copy, (v) deletes it from HDFS, and (vi) empties the trash. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The HDFS delete lifecycle is a **two-stage safety mechanism**:

- **Stage 1 — Soft delete (`-rm`):** The NameNode does not free the blocks. Instead, it moves the file's INode under `/user/<user>/.Trash/Current/`. The blocks remain on the DataNodes.
- **Stage 2 — Checkpoint-based purge:**
  - `fs.trash.interval` (default 1440 minutes = 1 day) defines the *retention* window.
  - `fs.trash.checkpoint.interval` (default 0 minutes) defines how often a new *checkpoint* directory is created under `.Trash`.
  - When the interval expires, the **oldest checkpoint is auto-deleted**, freeing the DataNode blocks.
  - `hdfs dfs -expunge` forces an immediate checkpoint creation and deletes *all* items in the previous checkpoint, bypassing the interval.

> The lifecycle is: `Live → .Trash/Current → .Trash/<timestamp> → Permanently Deleted`.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# KTU Big Data Lab - Module 2 Experiment: Full Lifecycle Script

# (i) Start HDFS
start-dfs.sh
sleep 5
hdfs dfsadmin -safemode get

# (ii) Generate and upload a 250 MB log file
dd if=/dev/urandom of=/tmp/biglog.log bs=1M count=250
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /ktu/lab/exp2/data
hdfs dfs -put /tmp/biglog.log /ktu/lab/exp2/data/

# (iii) Verify size and replication
hdfs dfs -ls -h /ktu/lab/exp2/data/biglog.log
hdfs dfs -stat %r /ktu/lab/exp2/data/biglog.log   # prints replication factor
hdfs dfs -du -h /ktu/lab/exp2/data/biglog.log

# (iv) Download a copy
hdfs dfs -get /ktu/lab/exp2/data/biglog.log /tmp/biglog_copy.log
ls -lh /tmp/biglog_copy.log

# (v) Soft-delete the file
hdfs dfs -rm /ktu/lab/exp2/data/biglog.log
hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/.Trash/Current/  # confirm presence in trash

# (vi) Empty the trash
hdfs dfs -expunge
hdfs dfs -ls /user/student/.Trash/          # confirm trash is empty
```

**Explanation of key lines:**
- `dd if=/dev/urandom of=…` generates a 250 MB file filled with random bytes — ensures the block-count math is non-trivial (⌈250/128⌉ = **2 blocks**).
- `hdfs dfs -stat %r` is a one-liner to print the **replication factor** — examiners love this.
- `hdfs dfs -du -h` shows the replicated footprint: 250 MB logical × 3 replication = **750 MB replicated**.
- `hdfs dfs -rm` is reversible; the next `-ls` proves the file is now under `.Trash/Current/`.
- `hdfs dfs -expunge` makes the deletion permanent.

> **Mark distribution:** 4 marks for the script, 2 marks for output verification, 1 mark for the `dd`/block-count math comment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting `start-dfs.sh`**: Many students submit scripts that fail because the NameNode is not running. The examiner will **deduct 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing `-rm` with permanent deletion**: If your answer does not mention the trash checkpoint, expect a **2-mark cut** in the *delete* sub-question.
> 3. **Not verifying replication**: Failing to use `-stat %r` or `-du` is a **favourite** way for examiners to deduct marks on the "inspect" sub-question.
> 4. **Wrong syntax for recursive delete**: Use `-rm -r` (Hadoop 2.x+) **not** `-rmr` (deprecated since Hadoop 2.x). Mixing the two costs a mark.
> 5. **Ignoring `hdfs dfsadmin -safemode get`**: A 0.5–1 mark cut if the safe-mode status is not checked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **HDFS** is the storage backbone of Hadoop; **YARN** is the compute backbone; together they decouple storage from processing.
- ✅ All HDFS commands are accessible via **two** prefixes: `hadoop fs` (generic) and `hdfs dfs` (recommended, HDFS-specific).
- ✅ The **default block size is 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x); the **default replication factor is 3**.
- ✅ `-put`, `-copyFromLocal`, and `-moveFromLocal` are the three "add" commands — they differ in **how they handle the local source file**.
- ✅ `-get`, `-cat`, `-text`, and `-tail` are the four common "retrieve/view" commands; `-text` is mandatory for compressed files.
- ✅ **Block count formula** for a file of size $S$ MB: $\text{Blocks} = \lceil S / 128 \rceil$. The actual on-disk footprint is $\text{Blocks} \times 128 \times \text{ReplicationFactor}$.
- ✅ `-rm` is **soft** (recoverable from `.Trash`); `-rm -skipTrash` is **hard** (irreversible). The HDFS trash uses a **checkpoint-based** retention model governed by `fs.trash.interval` and `fs.trash.checkpoint.interval`.
- ✅ `-expunge` is the **forced vacuum** that empties the trash immediately — a recurring viva question.
- ✅ `mkdir -p` creates **parent directories recursively**; `ls -R` and `ls -h` are useful for human-readable and recursive inspection.
- ✅ `du -h` reports **logical** (left) and **replicated** (right) sizes; `df -h` reports cluster-wide free/used space.
- ✅ `chmod`, `chown`, and `chgrp` apply **POSIX-style** permissions to HDFS files (mode 755, 644, 640 are common).
- ✅ Always **start HDFS, exit safe mode, and confirm DataNode liveness** before any file operation — this is the KTU pre-flight ritual.
- ✅ The **Web UI** at port **9870 (Hadoop 3.x)** or **50070 (Hadoop 2.x)** is the visual proof of every file-management operation — examiners often ask you to screenshot it.
- ✅ In the **Java API**, the third argument of `fs.delete(path, recursive)` controls whether a directory is removed recursively.
- ✅ **Trash is not on by default in older Hadoop**: If `fs.trash.interval = 0`, `-rm` is *permanent*. The KTU default is to keep the trash on.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Word Count Program Using MapReduce
R Program to Find Factorial and Check for Palindromes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** R Program to Find Factorial and Check for Palindromes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **R programming language**, *factorial computation* and *palindrome verification* are classical introductory control-flow exercises used to demonstrate the use of **recursion**, **loops**, **string manipulation**, and **built-in vectorized functions** like `rev()`, `strsplit()`, and `paste()`. They are foundational algorithms a Big Data engineer encounters in data cleaning, feature engineering, and statistical pre-processing pipelines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factorial**: For a non-negative integer $n$, the factorial $n!$ is the product of all positive integers from $1$ up to $n$. Mathematically: $n! = n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \ldots \times 2 \times 1$, with the base case $0! = 1$ by definition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Palindrome**: A string or integer that reads the same forwards and backwards. Examples: `"madam"`, `12321`, `"racecar"`. A palindrome sequence is invariant under a *reversal* transformation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Factorial** is like arranging people in a line for a photograph. If you have **5** people, the number of ways you can line them up is $5 \times 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 120$. Each position in the line consumes one person, multiplying the possibilities as you go.

**Palindrome** is like a word written on a transparent mirror. The word `"level"` looks the same whether you read it from left-to-right or right-to-left. In R, we can test this by literally *reversing* the string and checking if the original and reversed are equal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key R Functions Used in This Experiment**
> - `factorial(n)` — built-in factorial function.
> - `rev(x)` — reverses the order of a vector.
> - `strsplit(x, "")` — splits a string into individual characters.
> - `paste(..., collapse="")` — joins character vectors into one string.
> - `nchar(x)` — counts the number of characters in a string.
> - `tolower(x)` / `toupper(x)` — case normalization for case-insensitive checks.

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Big Data Processing

In real-world Big Data workflows (e.g., Hadoop MapReduce, Spark, dplyr pipelines), the same control-flow logic is re-implemented in distributed form. For example, palindrome checks are used in **DNA sequence bioinformatics**, **log anomaly detection**, and **string deduplication**. Factorials appear in **combinatorial feature generation** and **probability mass functions** (e.g., binomial, Poisson distributions).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Factorial — Mathematical Foundation

The factorial function is defined for $n \geq 0$ as:

$$n! = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } n = 0 \\ n \times (n-1)! & \text{if } n > 0 \end{cases}$$

**Two Implementation Strategies in R:**

1. **Iterative (Loop-based)**: Initialize accumulator = 1; multiply by each integer from 1 to n.
2. **Recursive (Function self-calls itself)**: If n is 0, return 1; otherwise return n × factorial(n−1).

**Growth rate of factorial**: $n!$ grows *super-exponentially*. For $n = 20$, $n! = 2.43 \times 10^{18}$, which exceeds the range of a 64-bit signed integer. R's numeric type is **double-precision floating point**, so factorials up to roughly $n = 170$ are representable before overflow to `Inf`.

### 2.2 Palindrome — Mathematical Foundation

A string $S$ of length $L$ is a palindrome if and only if for every valid index $i$:

$$S[i] = S[L - i + 1]$$

Equivalently, the string equals its reversal:

$$S = \text{reverse}(S)$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the complete algorithmic toolkit required for the KTU board exam. All mathematical symbols are LaTeX-rendered to prevent table syntax breakage.

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Form | R Implementation | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Factorial (Recursive) | $n! = n \cdot (n-1)!$ | `if (n==0) 1 else n * factorial(n-1)` | $O(n)$ |
| Factorial (Iterative) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} i$ | `for(i in 1:n) result <- result * i` | $O(n)$ |
| Base Case | $0! = 1$ | `if (n == 0) return(1)` | $O(1)$ |
| Built-in | `factorial(n)` | `factorial(5)` returns 120 | $O(n)$ internally |
| Palindrome (Character) | $S = \text{rev}(S)$ | `s == paste(rev(strsplit(s, "")[[1]]), collapse="")` | $O(L)$ |
| Palindrome (Integer) | $\text{num} = \text{rev}(\text{num})$ | Convert to string, then apply string check | $O(L)$ |
| Case-Insensitive | Compare after `tolower()` | `tolower(s) == tolower(rev_s)` | $O(L)$ |
| Length Function | $L = \text{nchar}(S)$ | `nchar(s)` | $O(1)$ |
| Recursion Depth Limit | $n_{\max} \approx 1000$ (default) | `options(expressions = 10000)` to extend | Depends on stack |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics**: Palindromic sequences occur in DNA/RNA where they signal restriction enzyme cut sites.
- **Cryptography**: Palindromic primes (e.g., 101, 131) are studied in number theory for hash functions.
- **Combinatorics in Data Science**: Factorials compute combinations $C(n,k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$ used in **feature subset selection**.
- **NLP Pipelines**: Palindrome detection is a baseline test in tokenizer correctness.
- **Data Quality**: Removing palindromic duplicate keys in distributed databases (HBase, Cassandra) requires O(1) lookup of reversed keys.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Program 1: Factorial in R (Iterative + Recursive)

```r
# ---- Method 1: Built-in Function ----
fact_builtin <- function(n) {
  if (n < 0) {
    return(NaN)   # Factorial undefined for negative numbers
  }
  return(factorial(n))
}

# ---- Method 2: Iterative (Loop) ----
fact_iterative <- function(n) {
  if (n < 0) return(NaN)
  result <- 1
  for (i in 1:n) {
    result <- result * i
  }
  return(result)
}

# ---- Method 3: Recursive ----
fact_recursive <- function(n) {
  if (n < 0) return(NaN)
  if (n == 0) return(1)       # Base case
  return(n * fact_recursive(n - 1))
}

# Driver code
n <- 5
cat("Built-in  :", fact_builtin(n),     "\n")
cat("Iterative :", fact_iterative(n),   "\n")
cat("Recursive :", fact_recursive(n),   "\n")
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Built-in  : 120 
Iterative : 120 
Recursive : 120 
```

### 3.2 Dry Run (Trace Table) for `fact_iterative(5)`

We trace the loop step by step to confirm the algorithm:

| Iteration $i$ | `result` (before) | Operation | `result` (after) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | 1 | — | 1 |
| $i = 1$ | 1 | $1 \times 1$ | 1 |
| $i = 2$ | 1 | $1 \times 2$ | 2 |
| $i = 3$ | 2 | $2 \times 3$ | 6 |
| $i = 4$ | 6 | $6 \times 4$ | 24 |
| $i = 5$ | 24 | $24 \times 5$ | 120 |

Final result $= 5! = 120$. Confirmed.

### 3.3 Dry Run (Recursion Tree) for `fact_recursive(4)`

The recursive function unfolds as a chain of pending multiplications:

$$\text{fact}(4) \rightarrow 4 \times \text{fact}(3) \rightarrow 4 \times 3 \times \text{fact}(2) \rightarrow 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times \text{fact}(1) \rightarrow 4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 \times \text{fact}(0)$$

Since $\text{fact}(0) = 1$ (base case), the chain collapses:

$$4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1 \times 1 = 24$$

> [!NOTE]
> **R Recursion Caveat**: R has a default call stack limit (governed by `options("expressions")`, default 5000). For deep recursion on $n > 5000$, use the iterative method to avoid `Error: evaluation nested too deeply`.

### 3.4 Program 2: Palindrome Check in R (String and Integer)

```r
# ---- Palindrome for Strings (Case-Insensitive) ----
is_palindrome_string <- function(s) {
  s <- tolower(s)                                    # Normalize case
  chars <- strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]                    # Split into characters
  reversed_chars <- rev(chars)                       # Reverse
  reversed <- paste(reversed_chars, collapse = "")   # Join back
  return(s == reversed)
}

# ---- Palindrome for Integers ----
is_palindrome_number <- function(num) {
  s <- as.character(num)
  chars <- strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]
  reversed <- paste(rev(chars), collapse = "")
  return(s == reversed)
}

# Driver code
cat("'madam'  is palindrome? ", is_palindrome_string("madam"),  "\n")
cat("'hello'  is palindrome? ", is_palindrome_string("hello"),  "\n")
cat("'Level'  is palindrome? ", is_palindrome_string("Level"),  "\n")
cat("12321    is palindrome? ", is_palindrome_number(12321),    "\n")
cat("12345    is palindrome? ", is_palindrome_number(12345),    "\n")
```

**Expected Output:**

```
'madam'  is palindrome?  TRUE 
'hello'  is palindrome?  FALSE 
'Level'  is palindrome?  TRUE 
12321    is palindrome?  TRUE 
12345    is palindrome?  FALSE 
```

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Trace for `is_palindrome_string("madam")`

We now exhaustively show how each line transforms the input:

**Step 1**: Input is `s = "madam"`. Apply `tolower()`:

$$s \leftarrow \text{tolower}(\text{"madam"}) = \text{"madam"}$$

**Step 2**: Apply `strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]`. This splits the string into a *character vector* by treating `NULL` as a separator meaning *every character*:

$$\text{chars} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{"m"} \\ \text{"a"} \\ \text{"d"} \\ \text{"a"} \\ \text{"m"} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3**: Apply `rev(chars)`. This reverses the vector order:

$$\text{reversed\_chars} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{"m"} \\ \text{"a"} \\ \text{"d"} \\ \text{"a"} \\ \text{"m"} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4**: Apply `paste(..., collapse = "")`. This concatenates the vector elements into a single string with no separator:

$$\text{reversed} = \text{"madam"}$$

**Step 5**: Compare `s == reversed`:

$$s = \text{"madam"}, \quad \text{reversed} = \text{"madam"}$$

$$s = \text{reversed} \implies \text{return TRUE}$$

### 3.6 Step-by-Step Trace for `is_palindrome_string("hello")`

**Step 1**: $s \leftarrow \text{"hello"}$.

**Step 2**:

$$\text{chars} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{"h"} \\ \text{"e"} \\ \text{"l"} \\ \text{"l"} \\ \text{"o"} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3**:

$$\text{reversed\_chars} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{"o"} \\ \text{"l"} \\ \text{"l"} \\ \text{"e"} \\ \text{"h"} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4**:

$$\text{reversed} = \text{"olleh"}$$

**Step 5**: Compare:

$$s = \text{"hello"} \neq \text{"olleh"} = \text{reversed} \implies \text{return FALSE}$$

### 3.7 Combined Master Program (Ready for Lab Record Submission)

```r
# ============================================
# R Program: Factorial & Palindrome Checker
# Course   : PCCDL607 - Big Data Processing Lab
# Module   : 2
# ============================================

# -------- FACTORIAL --------
factorial_R <- function(n) {
  if (n < 0)            return(NaN)
  if (n == 0)           return(1)
  return(n * factorial_R(n - 1))
}

# -------- PALINDROME (String) --------
is_palindrome <- function(s) {
  s <- tolower(s)
  rev_s <- paste(rev(strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]), collapse = "")
  return(s == rev_s)
}

# -------- PALINDROME (Integer) --------
is_num_palindrome <- function(num) {
  s <- as.character(num)
  rev_s <- paste(rev(strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]), collapse = "")
  return(s == rev_s)
}

# -------- DRIVER CODE --------
cat("===== Factorial Tests =====\n")
for (k in 0:6) {
  cat(sprintf("%d! = %g\n", k, factorial_R(k)))
}

cat("\n===== Palindrome (String) Tests =====\n")
words <- c("madam", "racecar", "level", "hello", "R")
for (w in words) {
  cat(sprintf("%-10s -> %s\n", w, is_palindrome(w)))
}

cat("\n===== Palindrome (Integer) Tests =====\n")
nums <- c(121, 12321, 12345, 1001, 7)
for (nm in nums) {
  cat(sprintf("%-7d -> %s\n", nm, is_num_palindrome(nm)))
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
===== Factorial Tests =====
0! = 1
1! = 1
2! = 2
3! = 6
4! = 24
5! = 120
6! = 720

===== Palindrome (String) Tests =====
madam      -> TRUE
racecar    -> TRUE
level      -> TRUE
hello      -> FALSE
R          -> TRUE

===== Palindrome (Integer) Tests =====
121      -> TRUE
12321    -> TRUE
12345    -> FALSE
1001     -> TRUE
7        -> TRUE
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Factorial Computation (Recursive)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read input n/]
    B --> C{n is negative?}
    C -- Yes --> D[/Print NaN/] --> Z([End])
    C -- No --> E{n equals 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[/Return 1/] --> Z
    E -- No --> G[/Compute n times factorial of n minus 1/]
    G --> H[/Return result/]
    H --> Z
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Palindrome Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[/Read input string or number/]
    B --> C[Convert to lowercase string]
    C --> D[Split string into character vector]
    D --> E[Reverse the character vector]
    E --> F[Join characters into reversed string]
    F --> G{Original equals reversed?}
    G -- Yes --> H[/Output TRUE/] --> Z([End])
    G -- No  --> I[/Output FALSE/] --> Z
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Factorial + Palindrome Subsystem)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        A1[User Input n for Factorial]
        A2[User Input s for Palindrome]
    end

    subgraph LogicLayer["Processing Layer"]
        B1[Validation Module: Check n less than 0]
        B2[Recursive Engine: n times factorial of n minus 1]
        B3[String Normalizer: tolower]
        B4[Reversal Engine: rev and paste]
        B5[Comparator: original vs reversed]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"]
        C1[Print factorial value]
        C2[Print TRUE or FALSE]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 -- n equals 0 --> C1
    B1 -- n greater than 0 --> B2
    B2 --> C1

    A2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    B5 -- Match --> C2
    B5 -- Mismatch --> C2
```

### 4.4 Call Stack Visualization for `fact_recursive(4)`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[fact 4] --> L1[fact 3]
    L1 --> L2[fact 2]
    L2 --> L3[fact 1]
    L3 --> L4[fact 0 returns 1]
    L4 --> L3b[fact 1 returns 1 times 1 equals 1]
    L3b --> L2b[fact 2 returns 2 times 1 equals 2]
    L2b --> L1b[fact 3 returns 3 times 2 equals 6]
    L1b --> L0b[fact 4 returns 4 times 6 equals 24]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define a palindrome. Write an R function `isPalindrome(s)` that returns `TRUE` if the input string `s` is a palindrome (ignoring case) and `FALSE` otherwise.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark)**: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forward and backward. Formally, a string $S$ of length $L$ is a palindrome if $S[i] = S[L - i + 1]$ for all $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$.

**Code (2 Marks)**:

```r
isPalindrome <- function(s) {
  s <- tolower(s)
  rev_s <- paste(rev(strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]), collapse = "")
  return(s == rev_s)
}
```

The function converts the string to lowercase, splits it into a character vector, reverses it, joins it back, and compares it to the original.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between an iterative and recursive approach to compute a factorial. Which one is preferred in R for large values of n and why?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Iterative** (1 Mark): Uses a `for`/`while` loop to multiply numbers from 1 to n. Uses constant memory $O(1)$.
- **Recursive** (1 Mark): Calls itself with $(n-1)$ until the base case $n = 0$ is reached. Uses $O(n)$ stack memory.
- **Preferred for large n** (1 Mark): The **iterative** approach is preferred in R for large $n$ because R's default call stack is limited (around 5000 frames via `options("expressions")`), and deep recursion can throw `Error: evaluation nested too deeply`. Iterative is also faster due to no function-call overhead.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Missing `collapse = ""` in `paste()`** — results in a vector instead of a string. **−1 mark**.
> - **Forgetting `[[1]]` after `strsplit()`** — `strsplit()` returns a *list* of length 1; you must extract it. **−1 mark**.
> - **No case normalization** — `"Level"` will be flagged as non-palindrome. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if the question says "ignore case".
> - **Negative factorial returning 1 silently** — must return `NaN` or throw an error. **−1 mark**.
> - **Using `==` on factor variables** — R factors compare levels, not strings. Always use `as.character()` first.
> - **No base case in recursion** — infinite recursion crashes R. **−2 marks** (logical error).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**(a)** Write an R function that takes a non-negative integer `n` and returns its factorial using **recursion**. Include input validation. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write an R function that takes a **string** as input and checks whether it is a palindrome, ignoring case. Demonstrate with two test cases. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Recursive Factorial (7 Marks)**

```r
# Recursive factorial with input validation
factorial_recursive <- function(n) {
  # Input validation
  if (!is.numeric(n) || n < 0 || n != as.integer(n)) {
    stop("Input must be a non-negative integer.")
  }
  # Base case
  if (n == 0) {
    return(1)
  }
  # Recursive case
  return(n * factorial_recursive(n - 1))
}

# Demonstration
print(factorial_recursive(0))   # Output: 1
print(factorial_recursive(5))   # Output: 120
print(factorial_recursive(7))   # Output: 5040
```

**Explanation of marks (7):**
- Input validation (2 marks): checks `is.numeric`, `n < 0`, integer.
- Base case (2 marks): `if (n == 0) return(1)`.
- Recursive case (2 marks): `n * factorial_recursive(n - 1)`.
- Demonstration (1 mark): 3 print statements.

**(b) Palindrome String Check (7 Marks)**

```r
is_palindrome <- function(s) {
  # Step 1: Normalize
  s <- tolower(s)
  # Step 2: Reverse using built-ins
  rev_s <- paste(rev(strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]), collapse = "")
  # Step 3: Compare
  return(s == rev_s)
}

# Test Cases
cat("madam  ->", is_palindrome("madam"), "\n")    # TRUE
cat("Hello  ->", is_palindrome("Hello"), "\n")    # FALSE
cat("RaceCar->", is_palindrome("RaceCar"), "\n")   # TRUE
```

**Explanation of marks (7):**
- Case normalization (1 mark).
- String split using `strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]` (2 marks).
- Reversal using `rev()` and joining with `paste(..., collapse="")` (2 marks).
- Comparison `s == rev_s` and `return()` (1 mark).
- Test cases shown (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**(a)** Write an R function `factorialIter(n)` that computes $n!$ using a `for` loop. Also use R's built-in `factorial()` to verify the result. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write an R function that accepts an **integer** as input and determines whether it is a palindrome number. Test with `12321` and `12345`. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Iterative Factorial (7 Marks)**

```r
factorialIter <- function(n) {
  if (n < 0) {
    stop("Negative input not allowed.")
  }
  result <- 1
  for (i in 1:n) {
    result <- result * i
  }
  return(result)
}

# Verification with built-in
n <- 6
cat("Manual  :", factorialIter(n), "\n")   # 720
cat("Built-in:", factorial(n),     "\n")   # 720
```

**Explanation of marks (7):**
- Loop variable initialization `result <- 1` (1 mark).
- Loop structure `for (i in 1:n)` (2 marks).
- Multiplication inside loop (2 marks).
- Return statement and verification with `factorial()` (2 marks).

**(b) Palindrome Number Check (7 Marks)**

```r
is_palindrome_num <- function(num) {
  s <- as.character(num)
  rev_s <- paste(rev(strsplit(s, NULL)[[1]]), collapse = "")
  return(s == rev_s)
}

# Test Cases
cat("12321 ->", is_palindrome_num(12321), "\n")  # TRUE
cat("12345 ->", is_palindrome_num(12345), "\n")  # FALSE
```

**Explanation of marks (7):**
- Type conversion to character (1 mark).
- `strsplit()` with `NULL` (2 marks).
- `rev()` and `paste(collapse="")` (2 marks).
- Boolean comparison and return (1 mark).
- Test cases (1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exam)**
> - **Factorial base case**: $0! = 1$, undefined for negative integers.
> - **Two methods**: Recursive (uses stack, elegant) vs Iterative (uses loop, efficient in R).
> - **Built-in function**: `factorial(n)` exists in R — use it for verification.
> - **Palindrome core idea**: $S == \text{rev}(S)$ after case normalization.
> - **Five key R functions**: `tolower()`, `strsplit(..., NULL)[[1]]`, `rev()`, `paste(..., collapse="")`, `nchar()`.
> - **The double brackets `[[1]]` is mandatory** — `strsplit()` returns a list.
> - **Use `collapse = ""`** in `paste()` to join characters into a single string.
> - **R recursion limit**: ~5000 by default — use iterative for large `n`.
> - **Time complexity**: Both algorithms are $O(n)$ where $n$ is input size.
> - **Output type**: Palindrome check returns a logical `TRUE`/`FALSE`; factorial returns a numeric.
> - **Edge cases to test**: Empty string `""` (palindrome), $n = 0$ (factorial = 1), single character/digit.
> - **Big Data connection**: Combinatorics (factorial) used in feature engineering; palindromes used in bioinformatics & data deduplication.
> - **Most common board exam mistake**: Forgetting `collapse=""` in `paste()`, which yields a character *vector* instead of a *string*, causing the equality check to return `FALSE` even for true palindromes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R Program for Linear Regression and Predictions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** R Program for Linear Regression and Predictions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Linear Regression** is a supervised machine learning algorithm belonging to the family of *regression models* that establishes a **linear relationship** between a scalar dependent (response) variable $Y$ and one or more independent (explanatory/predictor) variables $X$. In the context of R programming, the `lm()` (linear model) function fits such models using **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** estimation, while the `predict()` function applies the fitted model to new data for forecasting.

The general form of a multiple linear regression model is expressed as:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \dots + \beta_p X_{pi} + \varepsilon_i$$

where:
- $Y_i$ is the **dependent variable** (response)
- $\beta_0$ is the **intercept**
- $\beta_1, \beta_2, \dots, \beta_p$ are the **regression coefficients** (slopes)
- $X_{1i}, X_{2i}, \dots, X_{pi}$ are the **independent variables** (predictors)
- $\varepsilon_i$ is the **error term** (residual), assumed to be $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a small coffee shop, and you want to **predict** tomorrow's sales (in cups). You suspect that the outside **temperature** affects sales — hotter days mean more cold coffee, colder days mean more hot coffee. You start collecting two columns of data over many days:
1. Temperature (X)
2. Cups sold (Y)

When you plot these points on a graph, you notice they roughly form a straight-line pattern. **Linear regression** draws the *best possible straight line* through these scattered points. The line is "best" because it minimizes the total squared vertical distance from each point to the line. Once you have this line, you can plug in tomorrow's predicted temperature and instantly estimate tomorrow's coffee sales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the Big Data Processing Lab, you are expected to implement linear regression using the inbuilt `mtcars`, `faithful`, or user-defined datasets in R, interpret the model output, and use `predict()` to perform forecasting on unseen data. The exam often asks you to write the complete R program along with output interpretation.

### 1.3 Types of Linear Regression

| Type | Predictors | R Function Call Example |
|------|-----------|------------------------|
| **Simple Linear Regression** | Exactly **1** independent variable | `lm(y ~ x, data=df)` |
| **Multiple Linear Regression** | **2 or more** independent variables | `lm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3, data=df)` |
| **Polynomial Regression** | Non-linear terms (squared, cubic) | `lm(y ~ x + I(x^2), data=df)` |

> [!TIP]
> In R's formula notation, the **tilde (`~`)** symbol means "is modeled as a function of". The variable on the **left** of `~` is the response, and variables on the **right** are predictors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Engine: Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

The OLS method finds the line that **minimizes the Residual Sum of Squares (RSS)**, which is the sum of the squared differences between the observed values ($Y_i$) and the predicted values ($\hat{Y}_i$):

$$\text{RSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(Y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i)\right)^2$$

The closed-form (Normal Equation) solution for simple linear regression is:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Steps in the R `lm()` Workflow

1. **Data Preparation:** Load or create a data frame with paired $(X, Y)$ observations.
2. **Model Specification:** Use `lm(formula, data)` to define the model. R internally constructs the **design matrix** $X$ (with a column of 1s for the intercept) and the response vector $Y$.
3. **Coefficient Estimation:** R solves the normal equation $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^TY$ to obtain the OLS estimates.
4. **Model Summary:** Calling `summary(model)` returns coefficients, standard errors, $t$-values, $p$-values, $R^2$, Adjusted $R^2$, and $F$-statistic.
5. **Prediction:** The `predict(model, newdata)` function computes $\hat{Y}_{new} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{new}$ for new observations.
6. **Diagnostics:** Functions like `plot(model)` produce four diagnostic plots (Residuals vs Fitted, Normal Q-Q, Scale-Location, Residuals vs Leverage) to validate assumptions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners frequently test for the linear regression lab.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Usage |
|------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Regression Line | $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ | Predicted value of $Y$ |
| Slope ($\beta_1$) | $\frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ | Change in $Y$ per unit $X$ |
| Intercept ($\beta_0$) | $\bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$ | Value of $Y$ when $X = 0$ |
| Residual ($e_i$) | $Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$ | Error of the $i^{th}$ prediction |
| SSR (Regression Sum of Squares) | $\sum (\hat{Y}_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Variation explained by model |
| SSE / RSS | $\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ | Unexplained variation |
| SST (Total Sum of Squares) | $\sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total variation in $Y$ |
| Coefficient of Determination ($R^2$) | $1 - \frac{SSE}{SST} = \frac{SSR}{SST}$ | Proportion of variance explained (range: $[0, 1]$) |
| Adjusted $R^2$ | $1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p-1}$ | Penalizes extra predictors |
| Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $\frac{1}{n}\sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ | Average squared residual |
| Root MSE (RMSE) | $\sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $Y$ |
| Prediction Interval (95%) | $\hat{Y} \pm t_{0.025, n-2} \cdot s_e \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_{new} - \bar{X})^2}{\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}}$ | Range for individual prediction |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** All the above formulas are written using $\vert$ for absolute value where needed, *not* the vertical pipe `|`, to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Linear regression in R is foundational to nearly every quantitative discipline:
- **Economics:** Forecasting GDP, inflation, demand-supply modeling.
- **Biology:** Modeling enzyme kinetics (Michaelis-Menten), dose-response curves.
- **Machine Learning:** Baseline predictor; building block for neural networks.
- **Quality Engineering:** Six Sigma regression analyses.
- **Big Data Analytics:** When extended via `biglm()` or `sparklyr` for distributed regression on massive datasets — directly linking this lab to the broader course theme of *Big Data Processing*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & R Program Walkthrough

### 3.1 Derivation of OLS Coefficients (Simple Linear Regression)

We begin with the simple model $\hat{Y}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i$ and seek to minimize:

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$$

**Step 1:** Take the partial derivative of $S$ with respect to $\beta_0$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

Solving gives:

$$\sum Y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum X_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

**Step 2:** Take the partial derivative of $S$ with respect to $\beta_1$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

Solving gives:

$$\sum X_i Y_i = \beta_0 \sum X_i + \beta_1 \sum X_i^2$$

**Step 3:** Solve the system of two normal equations simultaneously. After algebraic manipulation (substituting $\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$ into the second equation):

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

This closed-form result is exactly what R's `lm()` engine computes internally.

### 3.2 Complete R Program — Linear Regression and Predictions

Below is a **fully-commented, KTU-board-ready R program** for the experiment. It uses the built-in `mtcars` dataset, fits a multiple linear regression model predicting **miles per gallon (`mpg`)** from **horsepower (`hp`)** and **weight (`wt`)**, and then makes predictions for new cars.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before running the program:** Ensure R and RStudio are installed. The `mtcars` dataset is preloaded with base R, so no external packages are required for the core experiment. To produce nicer formatted output, you may optionally install `broom` using `install.packages("broom")`.

**Program File: `linear_regression.R`**

```r
# ==========================================================
# Experiment: Linear Regression and Predictions in R
# Course   : PCCDL607 - Big Data Processing Lab (KTU 2024)
# Dataset  : mtcars (built-in)
# Goal     : Predict 'mpg' using 'hp' and 'wt'
# ==========================================================

# -------- Step 1: Load and inspect the dataset --------
data(mtcars)
cat("--- First 6 rows of mtcars ---\n")
print(head(mtcars))
cat("\n--- Structure of mtcars ---\n")
str(mtcars)
cat("\n--- Summary statistics ---\n")
print(summary(mtcars[, c("mpg", "hp", "wt")]))

# -------- Step 2: Visualize relationships --------
# Open a 1x2 plotting window
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# Scatter plot: mpg vs hp
plot(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg,
     main = "MPG vs Horsepower",
     xlab = "Horsepower (hp)",
     ylab = "Miles per Gallon (mpg)",
     pch = 19, col = "steelblue")
abline(lm(mpg ~ hp, data = mtcars), col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Scatter plot: mpg vs wt
plot(mtcars$wt, mtcars$mpg,
     main = "MPG vs Weight",
     xlab = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
     ylab = "Miles per Gallon (mpg)",
     pch = 19, col = "darkgreen")
abline(lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars), col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Reset plotting layout
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# -------- Step 3: Fit the linear regression model --------
# Multiple linear regression: mpg depends on hp and wt
model <- lm(mpg ~ hp + wt, data = mtcars)

# -------- Step 4: View the detailed model summary --------
cat("\n========== MODEL SUMMARY ==========\n")
print(summary(model))

# -------- Step 5: Extract key statistics --------
cat("\n========== MODEL COEFFICIENTS ==========\n")
print(coef(model))
cat("\n========== CONFIDENCE INTERVALS ==========\n")
print(confint(model))

cat("\n========== MODEL ACCURACY ==========\n")
cat("R-squared       :", summary(model)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Adjusted R-sq   :", summary(model)$adj.r.squared, "\n")
cat("Residual Std Err:", summary(model)$sigma, "\n")
cat("F-statistic     :", summary(model)$fstatistic[1], "\n")

# -------- Step 6: Predict for new (unseen) data --------
# Define a new data frame with hypothetical new cars
new_cars <- data.frame(
  hp = c(100, 150, 200, 250),
  wt = c(2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0)
)
cat("\n========== NEW CARS DATA ==========\n")
print(new_cars)

# Generate predictions (point estimates)
predictions <- predict(model, newdata = new_cars)
cat("\n========== POINT PREDICTIONS (mpg) ==========\n")
print(predictions)

# Generate predictions with 95% confidence intervals
predictions_ci <- predict(model, newdata = new_cars,
                          interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)
cat("\n========== 95% CONFIDENCE INTERVALS ==========\n")
print(predictions_ci)

# Generate predictions with 95% prediction intervals
predictions_pi <- predict(model, newdata = new_cars,
                          interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)
cat("\n========== 95% PREDICTION INTERVALS ==========\n")
print(predictions_pi)

# -------- Step 7: Diagnostic plots --------
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model, main = "Diagnostic Plots")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# -------- Step 8: Residual analysis --------
residuals_vals <- residuals(model)
fitted_vals    <- fitted(model)

cat("\n========== RESIDUAL STATISTICS ==========\n")
cat("Mean of residuals  :", mean(residuals_vals), "\n")
cat("Std deviation      :", sd(residuals_vals), "\n")
cat("Min residual       :", min(residuals_vals), "\n")
cat("Max residual       :", max(residuals_vals), "\n")
```

### 3.3 Sample Output (Truncated, Lab-Exam Format)

When you execute the program, the **model summary** prints output resembling:

```
Call:
lm(formula = mpg ~ hp + wt, data = mtcars)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-3.0611 -1.6237 -0.5396  1.1018  5.6924 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 37.22727    1.59879  23.285  < 2e-16 ***
hp          -0.03177    0.00903  -3.518  0.00145 ** 
wt          -3.87783    0.63273  -6.129 1.12e-06 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.593 on 29 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8268,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8148 
F-statistic: 69.21 on 2 and 29 DF,  p-value: 9.109e-12
```

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Interpretation of the Output

| Output Component | Interpretation |
|------------------|----------------|
| `Estimate (Intercept) = 37.22727` | A baseline car (hp=0, wt=0) is predicted to get ~37.2 mpg (extrapolation; not meaningful physically). |
| `Estimate (hp) = -0.03177` | For each additional horsepower, mpg drops by ~0.032 units, holding weight constant. |
| `Estimate (wt) = -3.87783` | For each additional 1000 lbs of weight, mpg drops by ~3.88 units, holding hp constant. |
| `Pr(>|t|) < 0.05` for both predictors | Both `hp` and `wt` are **statistically significant** predictors of `mpg`. |
| `R-squared = 0.8268` | **82.68%** of the variation in `mpg` is explained by the model — a strong fit. |
| `Adjusted R-squared = 0.8148` | After penalizing for the number of predictors, the model still explains ~81.5% of variation. |
| `F-statistic p-value < 2.2e-16` | The overall model is highly significant. |

### 3.5 Mathematical Walkthrough: Manual Computation for a Toy Example

Consider 5 data points: $(X, Y) = \{(1, 2), (2, 2.9), (3, 3.8), (4, 5.1), (5, 6.2)\}$.

**Step 1: Compute means.**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5}{5} = 3, \qquad \bar{Y} = \frac{2+2.9+3.8+5.1+6.2}{5} = 4.0$$

**Step 2: Compute deviations and their products.**

| $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------|
| $-2$ | $-2.0$ | $4.0$ | $4$ |
| $-1$ | $-1.1$ | $1.1$ | $1$ |
| $0$  | $-0.2$ | $0.0$ | $0$ |
| $1$  | $1.1$  | $1.1$ | $1$ |
| $2$  | $2.2$  | $4.4$ | $4$ |
| **Sum** | — | **10.6** | **10** |

**Step 3: Compute slope.**

$$\beta_1 = \frac{10.6}{10} = 1.06$$

**Step 4: Compute intercept.**

$$\beta_0 = 4.0 - (1.06)(3) = 4.0 - 3.18 = 0.82$$

**Step 5: Final regression equation.**

$$\hat{Y} = 0.82 + 1.06 X$$

**Step 6: Predict for $X = 6$.**

$$\hat{Y}(6) = 0.82 + 1.06 \times 6 = 0.82 + 6.36 = 7.18$$

This manual derivation matches what R's `lm()` would output for the same dataset, demonstrating the OLS algorithm in action.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The R Linear Regression Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data Frame] --> B[Explore with head str summary]
    B --> C[Visualize with plot]
    C --> D[Specify Model: lm y ~ x1 x2 data]
    D --> E[R Constructs Design Matrix X with Intercept Column]
    E --> F[Solve Normal Equation beta = XtX inverse XtY]
    F --> G[Extract Coefficients Intercept and Slopes]
    G --> H[Call summary model for R2 p-values F-stat]
    H --> I{Coefficients Significant}
    I -- Yes --> J[Prepare newdata Data Frame]
    I -- No --> K[Revise Model Remove or Transform Predictors]
    J --> L[Call predict model newdata]
    L --> M[Optional interval confidence or prediction]
    M --> N[Generate Diagnostic Plots residuals vs fitted Q-Q]
    N --> O[Final Forecasted Output Y-hat]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Diagnostic Plot Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Residuals vs Fitted] --> Q1{Scattered Randomly with Zero Mean}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Linearity Assumption OK]
    Q1 -- No --> R1B[Add Polynomial Term or Transform Y]

    P2[Normal Q-Q Plot] --> Q2{Points on Diagonal Line}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Normality of Residuals OK]
    Q2 -- No --> R2B[Apply Box-Cox or Log Transform]

    P3[Scale-Location Plot] --> Q3{Horizontal Red Line}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Homoscedasticity OK]
    Q3 -- No --> R3B[Use Weighted Regression or Robust SE]

    P4[Residuals vs Leverage] --> Q4{Points Within Cooks Distance 0.5}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[No Influential Outliers]
    Q4 -- No --> R4B[Investigate and Possibly Remove Outliers]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Data Flow in the Lab Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input
        D1[mtcars dataset]
        D2[User-defined CSV]
    end

    subgraph Preprocessing
        E1[head and str Inspection]
        E2[Handle Missing Values with na omit]
    end

    subgraph Modeling
        F1[lm formula specification]
        F2[summary statistics extraction]
    end

    subgraph Prediction
        G1[Create newdata frame]
        G2[predict with confidence interval]
        G3[predict with prediction interval]
    end

    subgraph Validation
        H1[Diagnostic plot model]
        H2[Residual analysis]
    end

    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G1 --> G3
    F2 --> H1
    H1 --> H2
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Prediction Interval vs Confidence Interval

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CI[Confidence Interval for Mean Response]
        C1[Represents uncertainty in the estimated mean of Y]
        C2[Narrower band]
        C3[Formula uses 1 over n plus leverage term]
    end

    subgraph PI[Prediction Interval for Individual Response]
        P1[Represents uncertainty for a single new observation]
        P2[Wider band]
        P3[Formula adds 1 plus leverage term]
    end

    CI --- PI
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic for the Exam:** Always remember that a **Prediction Interval (PI)** is **wider** than a **Confidence Interval (CI)** because the PI must account for the variability of an individual data point *in addition to* the uncertainty in the mean estimate. The PI is what you report when forecasting a single new car; the CI is what you report when estimating the *average* mpg of many similar cars.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define linear regression. Write the R command to fit a simple linear regression model where `sales` is the dependent variable and `advertising` is the independent variable, using the dataset `mydata`.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Linear regression is a supervised learning algorithm that models the relationship between a scalar dependent variable $Y$ and one or more independent variables $X$ by fitting a linear equation $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ to the observed data using the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method, which minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

R command:

```r
model <- lm(sales ~ advertising, data = mydata)
summary(model)
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the role of the `predict()` function in R? Differentiate between `interval = "confidence"` and `interval = "prediction"`.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `predict()` function applies a previously fitted `lm()` model to a new dataset to generate forecasts. The two interval options are:

- **`interval = "confidence"`**: Produces a 95% confidence interval for the **mean** value of $Y$ at each new $X$. It is *narrower* and answers: *"What is the average mpg expected for cars with these specifications?"*
- **`interval = "prediction"`**: Produces a 95% prediction interval for an **individual** new observation. It is *wider* (adds a `+1` term under the square root) and answers: *"What is the range of mpg expected for a specific new car?"*

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the mathematical foundation of linear regression with a suitable diagram. Derive the OLS estimators $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ for a simple linear regression model. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete R program to perform simple linear regression on the `faithful` dataset (predicting `eruptions` from `waiting`). Display the model summary, plot the regression line, and predict the eruption duration for a new waiting time of 85 minutes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The simple linear regression model is:

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

The OLS method minimizes the Residual Sum of Squares:

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2\sum(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \bar{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2\sum X_i(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

Substituting $\beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$ into the second equation and simplifying yields the closed-form estimators:

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

A scatter plot with the best-fit line illustrates the geometric intuition: the line minimizes the sum of squared vertical distances to all data points.

**Model Solution for (b):**

```r
# Load dataset
data(faithful)

# Inspect
head(faithful)
plot(faithful$waiting, faithful$eruptions,
     main = "Old Faithful Eruptions vs Waiting Time",
     xlab = "Waiting Time (minutes)",
     ylab = "Eruption Duration (minutes)",
     pch = 19, col = "purple")

# Fit model
model <- lm(eruptions ~ waiting, data = faithful)
summary(model)

# Overlay regression line
abline(model, col = "red", lwd = 2)

# Predict for new waiting time
newdata <- data.frame(waiting = 85)
predicted_eruption <- predict(model, newdata)
cat("Predicted eruption duration for waiting = 85 min :",
    predicted_eruption, "minutes\n")

# Confidence and prediction intervals
predict(model, newdata, interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)
predict(model, newdata, interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)
```

**Expected Output (Approximate):**
- Slope $\beta_1 \approx 0.0756$, Intercept $\beta_0 \approx -1.874$
- Equation: $\widehat{\text{eruptions}} = -1.874 + 0.0756 \times \text{waiting}$
- Prediction at waiting = 85 min: $\approx 4.56$ minutes
- $R^2 \approx 0.811$ (81.1% of variance explained)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the workflow of fitting a linear regression model in R. Discuss the significance of the four diagnostic plots produced by `plot(model)`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the inbuilt `mtcars` dataset. Write an R program to fit a multiple linear regression model predicting `mpg` from `disp`, `hp`, and `wt`. Display the coefficients, $R^2$, and predict the mileage for a new car with `disp=200`, `hp=150`, `wt=3.2`. Also compute the 95% prediction interval. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Workflow of `lm()`:**

1. **Data Input** → Load or read data into a data frame.
2. **Data Exploration** → `head()`, `str()`, `summary()` for sanity checks.
3. **Visualization** → `plot()`, `pairs()`, `cor()` to assess relationships.
4. **Model Fitting** → `model <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data=df)`.
5. **Model Summary** → `summary(model)` for coefficients, $R^2$, p-values.
6. **Diagnostics** → `plot(model)` produces:
   - **Residuals vs Fitted:** Tests *linearity*. A horizontal red line at zero is desired; patterns suggest non-linearity.
   - **Normal Q-Q:** Tests *normality of residuals*. Points should fall on the diagonal dashed line.
   - **Scale-Location:** Tests *homoscedasticity* (constant variance). A horizontal red line indicates equal variance.
   - **Residuals vs Leverage:** Identifies *influential outliers* using Cook's distance. Points beyond the dashed contour are problematic.
7. **Prediction** → `predict(model, newdata)`.

**Model Solution for (b):**

```r
data(mtcars)

# Fit multiple regression
model <- lm(mpg ~ disp + hp + wt, data = mtcars)

# Coefficients
cat("Coefficients:\n")
print(coef(model))

# R-squared
cat("R-squared:", summary(model)$r.squared, "\n")
cat("Adjusted R-squared:", summary(model)$adj.r.squared, "\n")

# New car
new_car <- data.frame(disp = 200, hp = 150, wt = 3.2)
predicted_mpg <- predict(model, newdata = new_car)
cat("Predicted mpg:", predicted_mpg, "\n")

# 95% prediction interval
pred_interval <- predict(model, newdata = new_car,
                         interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)
print(pred_interval)
```

**Expected Output (Approximate):**
- Coefficients: $\beta_0 \approx 37.10$, $\beta_{disp} \approx -0.0009$, $\beta_{hp} \approx -0.0311$, $\beta_{wt} \approx -3.8008$
- $R^2 \approx 0.827$
- Predicted mpg for new car: $\approx 19.0$ mpg
- 95% Prediction Interval: approximately $(13.7, \; 24.3)$ mpg

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing `predict()` syntax:** Many students write `predict(newdata, model)` — the correct order is `predict(model, newdata)`. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 2. **Forgetting column names in `newdata`:** The new data frame **must** use the *exact same column names* as the predictors in the formula. If your formula is `mpg ~ hp + wt`, then `newdata` must be `data.frame(hp = ..., wt = ...)`. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 3. **Not interpreting output:** Writing the program without explaining what the coefficients or $R^2$ *mean* loses at least **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Mixing up CI and PI:** Confidence interval is for the *mean*, prediction interval is for an *individual*. Examiners specifically test this distinction. Marks deducted: **1–2**.
> 5. **Missing model diagnostics:** Simply fitting and predicting is *not enough*. Always include at least the four `plot(model)` diagnostic plots. Marks deducted: **2**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Lab Exam**

- **Core function:** `lm(formula, data)` fits the model; `predict(model, newdata)` generates forecasts; `summary(model)` gives full statistics.
- **Formula notation:** `y ~ x` (simple), `y ~ x1 + x2` (multiple), `y ~ x + I(x^2)` (polynomial), `y ~ .` (use all other columns as predictors).
- **OLS assumption:** The line minimizes $\sum(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$ — the residual sum of squares.
- **Key coefficients to remember:**
  - **Intercept** ($\beta_0$): Predicted $Y$ when all $X = 0$.
  - **Slope** ($\beta_1$): Change in $Y$ for a one-unit change in $X$.
- **Goodness-of-fit metrics:**
  - $R^2$ → proportion of variance explained (closer to 1 is better).
  - **Adjusted $R^2$** → accounts for the number of predictors (use for multiple regression).
  - **F-statistic p-value** → tests overall model significance.
  - **t-test p-value** → tests individual coefficient significance.
- **Prediction functions:**
  - `predict(model, newdata)` → point prediction.
  - `predict(model, newdata, interval = "confidence")` → 95% CI for the mean.
  - `predict(model, newdata, interval = "prediction")` → 95% PI for a single observation.
- **Diagnostic plots (always include all four):** Residuals vs Fitted, Normal Q-Q, Scale-Location, Residuals vs Leverage.
- **Built-in datasets used in lab:** `mtcars`, `faithful`, `iris` (for regression on numeric columns).
- **Common R errors to avoid:**
  - Mismatched column names in `newdata`.
  - Forgetting to set the working directory or load the CSV correctly with `read.csv()`.
  - Treating categorical variables as numeric (use `factor()` first).
- **Golden exam line:** *"For each one-unit increase in $X$, $Y$ changes by $\beta_1$ units, holding all other predictors constant."* — This single sentence can fetch **2 marks** in interpretation questions.
- **The prediction equation you must memorize:** $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_p X_p$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: R Program for Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** R Program for Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Logistic Regression** is a supervised machine learning classification algorithm used in the **KTU 2024 Big Data Processing Lab** to predict the probability of a categorical (typically binary) dependent variable. It models the log-odds of the event as a linear combination of the independent variables, mathematically represented using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Logistic Regression is a statistical model that uses a logistic function to model a binary dependent variable. In R, it is implemented using the Generalized Linear Model (GLM) framework via the `glm()` function with the argument `family = binomial(link = "logit")`.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor deciding whether a patient has a disease (Yes/No) based on symptoms like **fever level**, **age**, and **blood pressure**. A simple linear line cannot output "Yes" or "No" cleanly — but a **Logistic Regression** curve squashes any number into a value between **0** and **1** (a probability). 

Think of it as a **"Probability Squeeze"**:
- Linear Regression: outputs any real number (e.g., -200, 5, 1000)
- Logistic Regression: outputs a **probability** strictly between **0** and **1**

If the probability ≥ **0.5** → Class **1** (e.g., Disease Present)
If the probability < **0.5** → Class **0** (e.g., Disease Absent)

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Sigmoid Function** | The S-shaped curve mapping real values to probabilities |
| **Log-Odds** | Logarithm of the odds ratio: $\ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ |
| **GLM** | Generalized Linear Model — the R framework for logistic regression |
| **Confusion Matrix** | 2×2 table comparing predicted vs actual classes |
| **ROC Curve** | Receiver Operating Characteristic — visualizes classifier performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Logistic Regression is one of the most frequently asked experiments in Module 2 of Big Data Processing Lab. Expect viva questions on the **sigmoid function**, **odds ratio**, and **model evaluation metrics**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logistic (Sigmoid) Function — The Heart of the Algorithm

The sigmoid function transforms any real-valued input into a probability bounded between **0** and **1**:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

Where the linear combination $z$ is:

$$z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$$

The full logistic regression model is:

$$P(Y=1 \mid X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}$$

## 2.2 Log-Odds (Logit Transformation)

Taking the logarithm of the odds:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why log-odds?** Because $\frac{p}{1-p}$ (odds) ranges from 0 to ∞, but $\ln(\text{odds})$ ranges from -∞ to +∞ — perfect for a linear combination. This is the "linearization trick" behind logistic regression.

## 2.3 Why Not Linear Regression for Classification?

| Issue | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output Range | -∞ to +∞ | **0 to 1** (probability) |
| Activation | None | **Sigmoid** |
| Error Function | Squared Error | **Log-Likelihood / Cross-Entropy** |
| Suitability for Classes | Poor | **Excellent** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Sigmoid** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Convert linear output to probability |
| **Logit** | $\text{logit}(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ | Linearize the probability |
| **Odds Ratio** | $\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}$ | Interpret effect of predictors |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | Model optimization |
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Overall correctness |
| **Precision** | $\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions |
| **Recall** | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Recall}}$ | Balance of precision & recall |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Healthcare**: Disease diagnosis (malignant/benign tumor)
- **Finance**: Loan default prediction, credit scoring
- **Marketing**: Customer churn prediction
- **Big Data**: Spam email classification at scale (Kafka + Spark + R pipeline)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sigmoid Function

**Step 1: Define odds from probability $p$**

$$\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}$$

**Step 2: Take the natural logarithm to obtain log-odds**

$$\ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$$

**Step 3: Exponentiate both sides**

$$\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $p$ (multiply both sides by $1-p$)**

$$p = (1-p) \cdot e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}$$

**Step 5: Expand and collect $p$ terms**

$$p = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x} - p \cdot e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}$$

**Step 6: Factor out $p$**

$$p + p \cdot e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}$$

**Step 7: Isolate $p$**

$$p\left(1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}\right) = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}$$

**Step 8: Final sigmoid form**

$$p = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Cross-Entropy Loss

For a single training example, the loss is:

$$L(\hat{y}, y) = -\left[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\right]$$

**Logic:** When $y = 1$, the second term vanishes → $-\log(\hat{y})$, penalizing $\hat{y} \to 0$ heavily. When $y = 0$, the first term vanishes → $-\log(1-\hat{y})$, penalizing $\hat{y} \to 1$ heavily.

The total cost over $m$ training examples is:

$$J(\beta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\right]$$

## 3.3 Confusion Matrix — Analytical Breakdown

For binary classification, predictions are categorized as:

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **TP (True Positive)** | Model predicts 1, actual is 1 ✓ |
| **TN (True Negative)** | Model predicts 0, actual is 0 ✓ |
| **FP (False Positive)** | Model predicts 1, actual is 0 ✗ (Type I Error) |
| **FN (False Negative)** | Model predicts 0, actual is 1 ✗ (Type II Error) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Given:** A model predicts on 100 patients. 
- TP = 40, FP = 10, FN = 5, TN = 45

**Step 1: Accuracy**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{40 + 45}{40 + 45 + 10 + 5} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85$$

**Step 2: Precision**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{40}{40 + 10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80$$

**Step 3: Recall (Sensitivity)**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{40 + 5} = \frac{40}{45} \approx 0.889$$

**Step 4: F1-Score**

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \times 0.889}{0.80 + 0.889} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.711}{1.689} \approx 0.842$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Logistic Regression Workflow in R

```mermaid

    B --> C["Output Probability p between 0 and 1"]
    C --> D{"p is greater than or equal to 0.5"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Predict Class 1"]
    D -- No --> F["Predict Class 0"]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000

```

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Confusion Matrix: 2 by 2 Grid"] --> B["Top Left: True Negative TN"]
    A --> C["Top Right: False Positive FP Type I Error"]
    A --> D["Bottom Left: False Negative FN Type II Error"]
    A --> E["Bottom Right: True Positive TP"]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    style B fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    style E fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Complete R Program for Logistic Regression (Lab Record)

> **Dataset Used:** The built-in `mtcars` dataset, predicting whether a car has automatic (0) or manual (1) transmission using `wt` (weight) and `hp` (horsepower) as predictors.

```r
# =====================================================
# EXPERIMENT: Logistic Regression in R
# KTU 2024 Scheme - Big Data Processing Lab
# Module 2 Experiment 2
# =====================================================

# Step 1: Install and load required packages
# install.packages("caTools")
# install.packages("caret")
# install.packages("pROC")
library(caTools)
library(caret)
library(pROC)

# Step 2: Load and explore the dataset
data(mtcars)
df <- mtcars[, c("mpg", "wt", "hp", "am")]
df$am <- as.factor(df$am)   # Convert target to factor
str(df)
summary(df)

# Step 3: Split data into Training (70%) and Testing (30%)
set.seed(123)
split <- sample.split(df$am, SplitRatio = 0.7)
train_data <- subset(df, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(df, split == FALSE)

# Step 4: Build the Logistic Regression model
model <- glm(am ~ wt + hp,
             data = train_data,
             family = binomial(link = "logit"))

# Step 5: Summarize the model
summary(model)

# Step 6: Predict probabilities on the test set
prob_pred <- predict(model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
print(prob_pred)

# Step 7: Convert probabilities to class labels (threshold = 0.5)
class_pred <- ifelse(prob_pred >= 0.5, 1, 0)
class_pred <- as.factor(class_pred)

# Step 8: Build the Confusion Matrix
actual <- test_data$am
cm <- table(Predicted = class_pred, Actual = actual)
print(cm)

# Step 9: Compute Evaluation Metrics
accuracy <- mean(class_pred == actual)
precision <- cm[2,2] / sum(cm[2,])
recall    <- cm[2,2] / sum(cm[,2])
f1_score  <- 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)

cat("Accuracy :", round(accuracy, 4), "\n")
cat("Precision:", round(precision, 4), "\n")
cat("Recall   :", round(recall, 4),    "\n")
cat("F1-Score :", round(f1_score, 4),  "\n")

# Step 10: Plot the ROC Curve
roc_obj <- roc(as.numeric(actual), as.numeric(prob_pred))
plot(roc_obj, col = "blue", main = "ROC Curve - Logistic Regression")
auc(roc_obj)
```

---

## 5.2 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` 
**What is the sigmoid function and why is it used in logistic regression?**

**Model Answer:**
The sigmoid function is defined as $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$. It maps any real-valued input to a value strictly between **0** and **1**, making it suitable for representing probabilities in classification problems. In logistic regression, it converts the linear combination of features into a probability estimate, ensuring the output is interpretable as the likelihood of belonging to a particular class. Its derivative is computationally simple, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Linear Regression and Logistic Regression.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Predicts continuous values | Predicts categorical (class) labels |
| **Output Range** | $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ | **0 to 1** (probability) |
| **Activation Function** | None | **Sigmoid** |
| **Loss Function** | Mean Squared Error | **Log-Loss / Cross-Entropy** |
| **Use Case** | House price prediction | Disease diagnosis (Yes/No) |

---

## 5.3 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of Logistic Regression. Derive the sigmoid function from the logit transformation. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Architecture (Block Diagram)**

The logistic regression pipeline consists of:
1. **Input Layer** — receives feature vector $X = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_n)$
2. **Linear Combination** — computes $z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + ... + \beta_n x_n$
3. **Sigmoid Activation** — applies $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$
4. **Decision Threshold** — classifies as 1 if $p \geq 0.5$, else 0
5. **Loss Computation** — uses cross-entropy to update weights

**Step 2: Derivation**

Starting from log-odds:

$$\ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$$

Exponentiating:

$$\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}$$

Solving for $p$:

$$p = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}$$

This is the **sigmoid function**, which always returns a value in $(0, 1)$.

**(b)** Write an R program to fit a logistic regression model on the `mtcars` dataset using `wt` and `hp` as predictors for `am` (transmission type). Display the confusion matrix and accuracy. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```r
# Load data
data(mtcars)
df <- mtcars[, c("wt", "hp", "am")]
df$am <- as.factor(df$am)

# Train-test split
set.seed(42)
split <- sample.split(df$am, SplitRatio = 0.75)
train <- subset(df, split == TRUE)
test  <- subset(df, split == FALSE)

# Fit logistic regression
model <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = train, family = binomial)

# Predict
pred_prob <- predict(model, test, type = "response")
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob >= 0.5, 1, 0)

# Confusion matrix & accuracy
cm <- table(Predicted = pred_class, Actual = test$am)
print(cm)
accuracy <- sum(diag(cm)) / sum(cm)
cat("Accuracy =", round(accuracy * 100, 2), "%\n")
```

**Sample Output (illustrative):**

```
        Actual
Predicted  0  1
        0  4  1
        1  1  3
Accuracy = 77.78 %
```

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the following evaluation metrics with formulas: **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score**. Why is accuracy alone insufficient for imbalanced datasets? **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Accuracy** measures overall correctness:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Precision** measures how many predicted positives are truly positive:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Recall** measures how many actual positives are correctly identified:

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of precision and recall:

$$F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

**Why Accuracy is Insufficient:**
In imbalanced datasets (e.g., 95% non-fraud, 5% fraud), a model predicting "non-fraud" for all cases achieves **95% accuracy** but is useless. **Precision and Recall** provide meaningful insight into the minority class performance, making them essential for real-world evaluation.

**(b)** Write an R program to compute the **ROC curve and AUC** for a logistic regression model. Interpret the AUC value. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```r
# Assuming 'model' and 'test_data' from previous experiment
library(pROC)

# Generate probabilities
prob <- predict(model, test_data, type = "response")

# Compute ROC
roc_curve <- roc(response = test_data$am, predictor = prob)

# Plot ROC
plot(roc_curve, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 3,
     main = "ROC Curve for Logistic Regression")
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "red")

# Compute AUC
auc_value <- auc(roc_curve)
print(auc_value)
```

**Interpretation of AUC:**

| AUC Value | Model Quality |
|---|---|
| **0.5** | Random guess (no discrimination) |
| **0.7 – 0.8** | Acceptable |
| **0.8 – 0.9** | Excellent |
| **> 0.9** | Outstanding |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Forgetting to set `family = binomial(link = "logit")` in `glm()`. Without this, R will silently perform **linear** regression. **Always verify** with `summary(model)` that the family is correct.
> - **Mistake 2:** Using `predict()` without `type = "response"` — this returns logits, not probabilities, breaking threshold-based classification.
> - **Mistake 3:** Not converting predicted values to **factor** before confusion matrix — R throws an error or returns incorrect counts.
> - **Mistake 4:** Reporting accuracy without computing **Precision/Recall** for imbalanced classes — KTU examiners deduct up to **3 marks** for this.
> - **Mistake 5:** Not setting `set.seed()` before train-test split — results become non-reproducible, which is a lab record evaluation penalty.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Logistic Regression** is a **classification** algorithm, not regression despite its name.
- It uses the **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ to squash outputs into **probabilities**.
- In R, always use `glm(formula, data, family = binomial(link = "logit"))`.
- `type = "response"` in `predict()` is **mandatory** for probability outputs.
- **Train-Test Split** is typically **70:30** or **80:20**; always set `set.seed()`.
- **Confusion Matrix** structure: rows = Predicted, columns = Actual.
- **Accuracy** alone is misleading for imbalanced data — always report **Precision, Recall, F1**.
- **ROC-AUC ≥ 0.8** is considered good; **0.5** implies a random model.
- **Packages used:** `caTools` (split), `caret` (confusion matrix), `pROC` (ROC curve).
- The **logit transformation** $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$ linearizes probability, enabling a linear regression-style equation.
- For **multiclass** classification, use `family = multinomial` with the `nnet` package.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Viva Tip:** Be prepared to answer: *"Why can't we use MSE for logistic regression?"* — Answer: Because the sigmoid output creates a **non-convex** loss surface with MSE, causing gradient descent to get stuck in local minima. **Cross-entropy** ensures convexity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Operations Using R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Statistical Operations Using R # 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — STATISTICAL OPERATIONS USING R ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statistical Operations** refer to the mathematical computations performed on numerical datasets to **summarise, describe, and infer** patterns. In the context of **R Programming** (a free, open-source language for statistical computing and graphics), these operations are executed using **built-in functions** of the base R installation and specialized packages such as `dplyr`, `tidyr`, `ggplot2`, and `stats`. R was created by **Ross Ihaka** and **Robert Gentleman** at the University of Auckland, New Zealand (1993). It is an interpreted, dynamically-typed language that is the *de-facto* standard in academia and analytics for: - **Descriptive statistics** (mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation) - **Inferential statistics** (t-tests, chi-square, ANOVA, correlation, regression) - **Data visualisation** (histograms, boxplots, scatter plots) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🩺 The Doctor's Stethoscope Analogy** > Imagine you are a doctor and a patient walks in carrying a giant sack of 10,000 medical reports. You cannot read each report one-by-one. Instead, you use a **stethoscope** — a single instrument that gives you the *heartbeat* of the whole patient in seconds. > > **R is your statistical stethoscope.** You give it a massive dataset (the sack of reports), and with a single function call like `mean()` or `sd()`, it instantly tells you the *vital signs* of the data — its centre, spread, and shape. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning in R Context | |---|---| | **Vector** | A 1-D sequence of elements of the same data type (e.g., `c(10, 20, 30)`) | | **Data Frame** | A 2-D table (rows = observations, columns = variables) | | **Factor** | A categorical variable with predefined levels | | **NA** | Not Available — R's representation of missing data | | **Working Directory** | The folder R reads/writes files from | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Statistical Operations Statistical operations in R can be broadly classified into the following categories: ### A. Measures of Central Tendency These identify the *centre* or *typical value* of a dataset. - **Arithmetic Mean** $\bar{x}$ - **Median** — middle value of sorted data - **Mode** — most frequently occurring value (R has no built-in `mode()` for stats; we create one) ### B. Measures of Dispersion These describe the *spread* of data around the centre. - **Range** = max − min - **Variance** $s^2$ - **Standard Deviation** $s$ - **Interquartile Range (IQR)** = Q3 − Q1 ### C. Measures of Shape - **Skewness** — asymmetry of distribution - **Kurtosis** — "tailedness" of distribution ### D. Inferential Statistics - **Correlation** $r$ — strength of linear relationship - **Linear Regression** $\hat{y} = b_0 + b_1 x$ - **Hypothesis Testing** (t-test, chi-square) ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the **high-yield formula sheet** you must memorise.
