\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \log_2\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \\ &= -(0.6429) \cdot \log_2(0.6429) - (0.3571) \cdot \log_2(0.3571) \\ &= -(0.6429) \cdot (-0.6374) - (0.3571) \cdot (-1.4854) \\ &= 0.4098 + 0.5304 \\ &= 0.9402 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ So the **initial entropy** of the system is **0.940 bits**. ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Compute Information Gain for `Outlook` `Outlook` has 3 values: Sunny (5 samples), Overcast (4), Rain (5). **Sunny subset** (Days 1, 2, 8, 9, 11): 2 Yes, 3 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) &= -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} \\ &= -(0.4)(-1.3219) - (0.6)(-0.7370) \\ &= 0.5288 + 0.4422 \\ &= 0.9710 \end{aligned} $$ **Overcast subset** (Days 3, 7, 12, 13): 4 Yes, 0 No. **Pure node!** $$H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) = 0$$ **Rain subset** (Days 4, 5, 6, 10, 14): 3 Yes, 2 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Rain}}) &= -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} \\ &= 0.9710 \quad \text{(by symmetry with Sunny)} \end{aligned} $$ **Weighted entropy after splitting on Outlook:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) \\ &= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 \\ &= 0.6936 \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.9402 - 0.6936 = 0.2466 \text{ bits}$$ ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Compute Information Gain for `Humidity` `Humidity` has 2 values: High (7), Normal (7). **High subset** (Days 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 12, 14): 3 Yes, 4 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{High}}) &= -\frac{3}{7}\log_2\frac{3}{7} - \frac{4}{7}\log_2\frac{4}{7} \\ &= -(0.4286)(-1.2224) - (0.5714)(-0.8074) \\ &= 0.5239 + 0.4613 \\ &= 0.9852 \end{aligned} $$ **Normal subset** (Days 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 11, 13): 6 Yes, 1 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Normal}}) &= -\frac{6}{7}\log_2\frac{6}{7} - \frac{1}{7}\log_2\frac{1}{7} \\ &= -(0.8571)(-0.2224) - (0.1429)(-2.8074) \\ &= 0.1906 + 0.4011 \\ &= 0.5917 \end{aligned} $$ **Weighted entropy after splitting on Humidity:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \frac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \frac{7}{14}(0.5917) \\ &= 0.4926 + 0.2959 \\ &= 0.7885 \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.9402 - 0.7885 = 0.1517 \text{ bits}$$ ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Information Gain for `Wind` `Wind` has 2 values: Weak (8), Strong (6). **Weak subset** (Days 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 13): 6 Yes, 2 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Weak}}) &= -\frac{6}{8}\log_2\frac{6}{8} - \frac{2}{8}\log_2\frac{2}{8} \\ &= -(0.75)(-0.4150) - (0.25)(-2) \\ &= 0.3113 + 0.5 \\ &= 0.8113 \end{aligned} $$ **Strong subset** (Days 2, 6, 7, 11, 12, 14): 3 Yes, 3 No. $$H(S_{\text{Strong}}) = 1.0$$ **Weighted entropy after splitting on Wind:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid \text{Wind}) &= \frac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \frac{6}{14}(1.0) \\ &= 0.4636 + 0.4286 \\ &= 0.8922 \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.9402 - 0.8922 = 0.0480 \text{ bits}$$ ### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Information Gain for `Temperature` `Temperature` has 3 values: Hot (4), Mild (6), Cool (4). **Hot subset** (Days 1, 2, 3, 13): 2 Yes, 2 No → $H = 1.0$. **Mild subset** (Days 4, 8, 10, 11, 12, 14): 4 Yes, 2 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Mild}}) &= -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\frac{2}{6} \\ &= 0.9183 \end{aligned} $$ **Cool subset** (Days 5, 6, 7, 9): 3 Yes, 1 No. $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Cool}}) &= -\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4} \\ &= 0.8113 \end{aligned} $$ **Weighted entropy:** $$ \begin{aligned} H(S \mid \text{Temp}) &= \frac{4}{14}(1.0) + \frac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{14}(0.8113) \\ &= 0.2857 + 0.3935 + 0.2318 \\ &= 0.9110 \end{aligned} $$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, \text{Temperature}) = 0.9402 - 0.9110 = 0.0292 \text{ bits}$$ ### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Select the Root Node and Tabulate Results | Attribute | Information Gain (bits) | Rank | |---|---|---| | **Outlook** | **0.2466** | **1 (Root)** | | Humidity | 0.1517 | 2 | | Wind | 0.0480 | 3 | | Temperature | 0.0292 | 4 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** `Outlook` is chosen as the **root node** of the decision tree because it provides the maximum information gain. The process then recurses on each child subset until pure leaves (or stopping criteria) are reached. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Criterion Comparison | Aspect | Entropy (ID3/C4.5) | Gini (CART) | Misclassification Error | |---|---|---|---| | Mathematical Form | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $1 - \max p_i$ | | Range (Binary) | $[0, 1]$ bit | $[0, 0.5]$ | $[0, 0.5]$ | | Computational Cost | High (logarithm) | Low (squares) | Lowest | | Sensitivity to Class Distribution | Higher | Moderate | Lowest | | Scikit-learn Parameter | `criterion='entropy'` | `criterion='gini'` (default) | Not directly available | | When to Prefer | When you need probabilistic leaf outputs | General-purpose, default choice | Theoretical baseline only | > [!NOTE] > In practice, the choice between Gini and Entropy has **negligible impact** on final tree performance for most datasets. They often produce nearly identical splits. The choice is more about theoretical interpretability and computational budget. ## 3.3 Reference Implementation Snippet (scikit-learn) > [!IMPORTANT] > Although the full experiment code is lab-specific, here is the canonical skeleton students should internalize. **Programming is permitted in this CS-lab course** (PCCDL607 is a programming lab). ```python import pandas as pd from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier from sklearn.metrics import (accuracy_score, confusion_matrix, classification_report) from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder # 1. Load data df = pd.read_csv('golf_dataset.csv') # 2. Encode categorical variables le = LabelEncoder() for col in df.select_dtypes(include='object').columns: df[col] = le.fit_transform(df[col]) # 3. Split features and target X = df.drop('Play', axis=1) y = df['Play'] # 4. Train / test split (stratified) X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split( X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=42, stratify=y ) # 5. Instantiate and train clf = DecisionTreeClassifier( criterion='gini', max_depth=4, min_samples_split=2, min_samples_leaf=1, random_state=42 ) clf.fit(X_train, y_train) # 6. Predict and evaluate y_pred = clf.predict(X_test) print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred):.4f}") print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred)) # 7. Visualize from sklearn.tree import plot_tree import matplotlib.pyplot as plt plt.figure(figsize=(20, 10)) plot_tree(clf, feature_names=list(X.columns), class_names=['No', 'Yes'], filled=True) plt.show() ``` # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Experimental Workflow The following block-level architecture flow maps the complete Decision Tree experiment pipeline, from raw data ingestion to evaluation and visualization, including the Big Data handling steps required by the KTU 2024 lab manual. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Dataset
CSV / Parquet / HDFS] --> B[Load with pandas
pd.read_csv chunksize=N] B --> C{Size Check
memory_usage} C -->|Fits in RAM| D[Full DataFrame in Memory] C -->|Exceeds RAM| E[Stratified Sampling
df.sample frac=0.1] D --> F[Data Cleaning
Handle Missing Values] E --> F F --> G[Feature Encoding
LabelEncoder / OneHotEncoder] G --> H[Train Test Split
70 30 stratified] H --> I[Initialize Classifier
DecisionTreeClassifier] I --> J[Hyperparameter Setup
criterion max_depth min_samples] J --> K[Model Training
clf.fit X train y train] K --> L[Prediction
y pred equals clf.predict X test] L --> M[Evaluation Metrics
Accuracy Precision Recall F1] L --> N[Confusion Matrix
confusion matrix y test y pred] K --> O[Tree Visualization
plot tree clf filled True] M --> P[Report Generation
classification report] N --> P O --> P P --> Q[Lab Record Submission
Screenshots and Analysis] ``` ## 4.2 Recursive Tree Construction Logic The following diagram illustrates the recursive logic that scikit-learn's `fit()` method executes internally when building the tree. Each node performs an *impurity-based feature selection* and spawns child nodes until a stopping criterion is met. ```mermaid flowchart TD R0[Root Node
Full Training Set S] --> Q1{Stopping Criterion
Met?} Q1 -->|Yes| L1[Leaf Node
Majority Class] Q1 -->|No| C1[Compute Gini or Entropy
for every feature A and split t] C1 --> C2[Select best feature A star
and threshold t star
maximizing Information Gain] C2 --> S1[Create left child node
S left = x in S colon x A star leq t star] C2 --> S2[Create right child node
S right = x in S colon x A star gt t star] S1 --> R1[Recurse on S left] S2 --> R2[Recurse on S right] R1 --> Q1 R2 --> Q1 ``` ## 4.3 Impurity Reduction at the Root Node (Worked Example) The following diagram visualizes the *information gain* computation we performed by hand in Section 3, mapping each attribute to its gain value as a horizontal bar. ```mermaid flowchart LR H0[Dataset Entropy
H of S equals 0.940 bits] --> IG1[IG Outlook equals 0.247 bits] H0 --> IG2[IG Humidity equals 0.152 bits] H0 --> IG3[IG Wind equals 0.048 bits] H0 --> IG4[IG Temperature equals 0.029 bits] IG1 --> WIN[Highest Gain
Root Node equals OUTLOOK] IG2 --> RANK2[Rank 2] IG3 --> RANK3[Rank 3] IG4 --> RANK4[Rank 4] WIN --> CHILD1[Sunny Branch
2 Yes 3 No] WIN --> CHILD2[Overcast Branch
4 Yes 0 No PURE LEAF] WIN --> CHILD3[Rain Branch
3 Yes 2 No] ``` ## 4.4 Data Partitioning Schematic This schematic illustrates how the original 14-instance "Play Golf" dataset is partitioned when `Outlook` is selected as the root split. The `Overcast` branch terminates in a pure leaf immediately, while the other two branches require further recursive splitting. ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[Root S equals 14 samples
9 Yes 5 No
Entropy equals 0.940] --> S[Outlook equals Sunny
5 samples
2 Yes 3 No
Entropy equals 0.971] ROOT --> O[Outlook equals Overcast
4 samples
4 Yes 0 No
Entropy equals 0.000 LEAF] ROOT --> RA[Outlook equals Rain
5 samples
3 Yes 2 No
Entropy equals 0.971] S --> S1[Humidity equals High
3 samples
0 Yes 3 No LEAF No] S --> S2[Humidity equals Normal
2 samples
2 Yes 0 No LEAF Yes] RA --> R1[Wind equals Weak
3 samples
3 Yes 0 No LEAF Yes] RA --> R2[Wind equals Strong
2 samples
0 Yes 2 No LEAF No] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define Information Gain in the context of a Decision Tree Classifier. How is it used to select the best split at a node?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Marking Scheme Breakdown:** > - 1 mark: Formal definition of IG. > - 1 mark: Formula. > - 1 mark: Selection logic. **Information Gain (IG)** is the expected reduction in entropy (or impurity) achieved by partitioning a dataset $S$ based on a particular attribute $A$. Mathematically, $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$ At every internal node of the decision tree, the algorithm evaluates the IG for **every** candidate feature and **every** candidate split threshold, and selects the feature-threshold pair that **maximizes** $IG$. This is a greedy, locally optimal strategy that ensures each split maximally homogenizes the child nodes with respect to the class label. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between the Gini Index and Entropy as impurity measures. Which one is the default in scikit-learn, and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Marking Scheme Breakdown:** > - 1 mark: Gini formula and characteristic. > - 1 mark: Entropy formula and characteristic. > - 1 mark: scikit-learn default + justification. | Aspect | Gini Index | Entropy | |---|---|---| | Formula | $G(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | | Range (Binary) | $[0, 0.5]$ | $[0, 1]$ bit | | Computational Cost | Lower (no logarithm) | Higher | scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier` uses **Gini Index** as the default (`criterion='gini'`) because it is computationally cheaper (avoids the `log` operation), which matters when training on Big Data with millions of samples, and because empirically it produces nearly identical trees to entropy-based splitting. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions offer an internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question B1 **or** Question B2. Both are provided below with full model solutions. --- ### Question B1 (14 Marks) — Option A **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a)** For the dataset given below, compute the **Entropy** of the target variable and the **Information Gain** for each of the three features `Color`, `Size`, and `Weight`. Identify which feature should be chosen as the **root node** of the decision tree. **(7 marks)** | # | Color | Size | Weight | Class | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Red | Small | Light | Yes | | 2 | Red | Large | Heavy | No | | 3 | Green | Small | Light | No | | 4 | Green | Large | Heavy | No | | 5 | Red | Small | Heavy | No | | 6 | Red | Large | Light | Yes | | 7 | Green | Small | Light | No | | 8 | Red | Small | Heavy | Yes | **(b)** Explain the role of the following scikit-learn hyperparameters in controlling decision tree overfitting. For each, state whether increasing the parameter makes the tree *more complex* or *less complex*: `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`, `max_features`, `criterion`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for B1(a) — 7 marks **Step 1: Class distribution and target entropy** - Class Yes: 3 (rows 1, 6, 8) - Class No: 5 (rows 2, 3, 4, 5, 7) - Total $N = 8$ $$ \begin{aligned} H(S) &= -\frac{3}{8}\log_2\frac{3}{8} - \frac{5}{8}\log_2\frac{5}{8} \\ &= -(0.375)(-1.4150) - (0.625)(-0.6781) \\ &= 0.5306 + 0.4238 \\ &= 0.9544 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **1 mark** for correctly computing the target entropy. **Step 2: Information Gain for `Color`** `Color = Red`: rows 1, 2, 5, 6, 8 → 3 Yes, 2 No (5 samples) $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Red}}) &= -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} \\ &= -(0.6)(-0.7370) - (0.4)(-1.3219) \\ &= 0.4422 + 0.5288 \\ &= 0.9710 \end{aligned} $$ `Color = Green`: rows 3, 4, 7 → 0 Yes, 3 No (3 samples). **Pure!** $$H(S_{\text{Green}}) = 0$$ **Weighted entropy:** $$H(S \mid \text{Color}) = \frac{5}{8}(0.9710) + \frac{3}{8}(0) = 0.6069$$ $$IG(\text{Color}) = 0.9544 - 0.6069 = 0.3475$$ **Step 3: Information Gain for `Size`** `Size = Small`: rows 1, 3, 5, 7, 8 → 2 Yes, 3 No (5 samples) → $H = 0.9710$ (computed earlier). `Size = Large`: rows 2, 4, 6 → 1 Yes, 2 No (3 samples). $$ \begin{aligned} H(S_{\text{Large}}) &= -\frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3} - \frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3} \\ &= 0.9183 \end{aligned} $$ **Weighted entropy:** $$H(S \mid \text{Size}) = \frac{5}{8}(0.9710) + \frac{3}{8}(0.9183) = 0.6069 + 0.3444 = 0.9513$$ $$IG(\text{Size}) = 0.9544 - 0.9513 = 0.0031$$ **Step 4: Information Gain for `Weight`** `Weight = Light`: rows 1, 2, 3, 6, 7 → 2 Yes, 3 No (5 samples) → $H = 0.9710$. `Weight = Heavy`: rows 4, 5, 8 → 1 Yes, 2 No (3 samples) → $H = 0.9183$. **Weighted entropy:** $$H(S \mid \text{Weight}) = \frac{5}{8}(0.9710) + \frac{3}{8}(0.9183) = 0.9513$$ $$IG(\text{Weight}) = 0.9544 - 0.9513 = 0.0031$$ **Step 5: Final Comparison and Root Selection** | Feature | Information Gain (bits) | |---|---| | **Color** | **0.3475 ← Root** | | Size | 0.0031 | | Weight | 0.0031 | > [!NOTE] > **Marking:** 1 mark for each feature's IG correctly computed (3 marks), 1 mark for the weighted-entropy arithmetic (2 marks), 1 mark for the final comparison and root selection (1 mark). The **7th mark** is for presenting the work neatly with a final summary table. **Conclusion:** `Color` is the root node with IG = **0.3475 bits**. --- #### Model Solution for B1(b) — 7 marks | Hyperparameter | Role | Effect of Increasing | |---|---|---| | `max_depth` | Maximum number of levels from root to deepest leaf. | **Less complex** (shallower tree, more underfitting). | | `min_samples_split` | Minimum number of samples required to attempt a split at a node. | **Less complex** (larger nodes stay unsplit, regularization effect). | | `min_samples_leaf` | Minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node. | **Less complex** (forces coarser leaves, prevents tiny over-fit leaves). | | `max_features` | Number of features considered when looking for the best split. | Less complex only if reduced; at extreme low values it acts as regularization. | | `criterion` | The impurity function (`'gini'` or `'entropy'`). | **No effect on complexity** — purely changes the splitting objective; trees are typically the same size. | > [!NOTE] > **1 mark per correct explanation, plus 2 marks for the final synthesis:** A common synthesis is the *bias-variance trade-off*: increasing any regularization parameter biases the model toward simpler trees (higher bias, lower variance), while the default unrestricted tree has zero bias on training data but very high variance. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Confusing `criterion` with a complexity control.** It is not. Examiners specifically test this. > 2. **Forgetting to state the *direction* of the effect** (i.e., does increasing make it *more* or *less* complex?). > 3. **Not relating the answer to bias-variance** in the final line — examiners look for this synthesis. > 4. **Writing `max_features` as a pure complexity control** — it controls the *randomness* of splits, relevant for ensemble methods, not a single tree's complexity directly. --- ### Question B2 (14 Marks) — Option B **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the complete **step-by-step algorithm** of the **ID3 Decision Tree** learning procedure. Include the role of entropy and information gain in your answer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider a binary classification dataset with the following confusion matrix produced by your trained `DecisionTreeClassifier`: | | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative | |---|---|---| | **Actual Positive** | 50 (TP) | 10 (FN) | | **Actual Negative** | 5 (FP) | 35 (TN) | Compute the **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, and **F1-Score** of the model. State whether the model is more biased towards *false positives* or *false negatives*, and suggest **two** hyperparameter changes to improve the F1-Score. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for B2(a) — 7 marks **The ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3) Algorithm:** > [!NOTE] > **Marking Scheme:** 2 marks for the procedure, 2 marks for entropy/IG role, 2 marks for stopping conditions, 1 mark for the recursive formulation. **Step 1 — Initialization.** Begin with the entire training set $S$ at the root node. Compute the entropy $H(S)$ of $S$ with respect to the target class. **Step 2 — Impurity Evaluation.** For every categorical feature $A$ and every possible value $v$ of $A$, compute: - The entropy of each child subset $H(S_v)$. - The weighted average entropy $H(S \mid A) = \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$. - The Information Gain $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$. **Step 3 — Best Split Selection.** Choose the feature $A^*$ that **maximizes** $IG(S, A)$ and create a decision node splitting on $A^*$. **Step 4 — Recursion.** For each child subset $S_v$ (one per value of $A^*$), recursively apply Steps 1–3 to build subtrees. **Step 5 — Termination / Leaf Creation.** Stop recursion and create a leaf node when **any** of the following holds: - All samples in the subset belong to the same class (pure node, $H = 0$). - No remaining features provide positive information gain. - The subset is smaller than `min_samples_split`. - The tree has reached `max_depth`. **Role of Entropy and Information Gain:** Entropy quantifies the disorder or impurity of a node. Information Gain measures how much that disorder is *reduced* by splitting on a particular feature. The ID3 algorithm uses IG as its **heuristic search function** — the feature with the highest IG is the most "informative" question to ask at that node. This greedy choice ensures that the tree, while not globally optimal, makes locally optimal decisions that progressively homogenize the leaf nodes. --- #### Model Solution for B2(b) — 7 marks **Given Confusion Matrix:** | | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative | Total | |---|---|---|---| | Actual Positive | TP = 50 | FN = 10 | 60 | | Actual Negative | FP = 5 | TN = 35 | 40 | | Total | 55 | 45 | 100 | **Step 1 — Accuracy** $$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{50 + 35}{100} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85 $$ **Step 2 — Precision** $$ P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} = 0.9091 $$ **Step 3 — Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)** $$ R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = 0.8333 $$ **Step 4 — F1-Score** $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9091 \times 0.8333}{0.9091 + 0.8333} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7576}{1.7424} = 0.8696 $$ **Summary Table:** | Metric | Value | |---|---| | Accuracy | 0.8500 | | Precision | 0.9091 | | Recall | 0.8333 | | F1-Score | 0.8696 | > [!NOTE] > **1.5 marks** for Accuracy, **1.5 marks** for Precision, **1.5 marks** for Recall, **1 mark** for F1, **0.5 mark** for the summary table. **Step 5 — Bias Analysis** Since $FN = 10 > FP = 5$, the model is more biased towards **False Negatives** (it is missing more actual positives than it is incorrectly flagging negatives). The Recall (83.33%) is lower than the Precision (90.91%), confirming this asymmetry. **Step 6 — Two Hyperparameter Suggestions to Improve F1** 1. **`class_weight='balanced'`** — This automatically adjusts the loss function to penalize misclassifications of the minority class more heavily, directly reducing FN and improving Recall (and therefore F1). 2. **Lower `min_samples_leaf`** or **Increase `max_depth`** — Allowing the tree to grow deeper captures finer patterns in the minority class, but must be balanced against overfitting. Alternatively, use an **ensemble** (`RandomForestClassifier` or `GradientBoostingClassifier`) which typically boosts F1 by 5–15% over a single tree. > [!NOTE] > **0.5 mark** for correctly identifying the bias direction, **0.5 mark** each for the two valid suggestions with justification = **1 mark total**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Formula swap:** Students often write $P = TP/(TP + FN)$ — that is Recall, not Precision. The board examiner deducts full marks for this. > 2. **Forgetting to convert to decimal/propercentage** — leaving the answer as $50/55$ without finalizing. > 3. **Ignoring the bias analysis** — the question specifically *asks* for it; many students skip it and lose 0.5 marks. > 4. **Suggesting `criterion='entropy'` as an F1 improvement** — it almost never changes F1 meaningfully. Examiners flag this as a "non-answer." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam Day** ### Core Concepts - A **Decision Tree** is a supervised, non-parametric, hierarchical classifier that recursively partitions the feature space. - The tree is built **top-down, greedy** (locally optimal splits, no backtracking). - Finding the **globally optimal** decision tree is **NP-complete**. ### Mathematical Foundations - **Entropy:** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Range: $[0, \log_2 k]$. - **Gini Index:** $G(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$. Range: $[0, 1 - 1/k]$. Default in scikit-learn. - **Information Gain:** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$. - The feature with the **highest IG** is selected at each split. ### scikit-learn API Essentials - **Class:** `sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeClassifier` - **Key Parameters:** `criterion` (`'gini'` or `'entropy'`), `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`, `max_features`, `random_state`, `class_weight`. - **Methods:** `fit(X, y)`, `predict(X)`, `predict_proba(X)`, `score(X, y)`, `.feature_importances_`. - **Visualization:** `sklearn.tree.plot_tree(clf, feature_names=..., class_names=..., filled=True)`. ### Evaluation Metrics (Don't Confuse Them!) - **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP + TN}{\text{Total}}$ — overall correctness. - **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — quality of positive predictions. - **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — coverage of actual positives. - **F1-Score** = $\frac{2 P R}{P + R}$ — harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. ### Pre-Processing Pitfalls - **Always** use `OneHotEncoder` or `pd.get_dummies()` for nominal categorical features. - **Never** feed raw strings to a Decision Tree — scikit-learn will raise a `ValueError`. - Handle **missing values** (`df.isnull().sum()`) before training — either impute or drop. - Use `train_test_split(..., stratify=y)` to preserve class balance in train/test sets. ### Big Data Considerations (PCCDL607 Context) - For datasets exceeding RAM, use **chunked reading** (`pd.read_csv(chunksize=N)`) or **stratified sampling**. - For truly massive datasets, migrate to **`pyspark.ml.classification.DecisionTreeClassifier`**, which implements the same algorithm in a distributed MapReduce framework. - A single decision tree is rarely the final model in production; it is a baseline or an **interpretability anchor** for ensemble methods (Random Forest, XGBoost). ### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid 1. Using $\vert$ (pipe) inside markdown table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` for LaTeX. 2. Confusing $H(S)$ with $H(S \mid A)$ in IG calculations. 3. Forgetting to multiply each child entropy by its weight $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ in the weighted-entropy step. 4. Stopping the entropy sum at the first class instead of summing over **all** classes. 5. Forgetting the convention $0 \log 0 = 0$. 6. Treating `criterion` as a complexity-control hyperparameter — it is not. ### One-Line Definition for Quick Recall > *"A Decision Tree Classifier recursively partitions the data using the feature that maximizes Information Gain (or equivalently minimizes Gini impurity) at each step, terminating when pure leaves or stopping criteria are met."*

topics.title: Clustering Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Clustering Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Clustering Algorithm — KTU Big Data Processing Lab

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering** is an **Unsupervised Machine Learning** technique used to group a set of data points into multiple clusters (or groups) such that data points in the same cluster are highly similar to each other, and data points in different clusters are highly dissimilar. In KTU's Big Data Processing Lab context, **K-Means Clustering** is the canonical algorithm implemented using **Apache Spark's MLlib** (`pyspark.ml.clustering.KMeans`) on massive distributed datasets.

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Given a dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ where each $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$, the goal of **K-Means clustering** is to partition the $n$ observations into $k$ disjoint clusters $C = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ such that the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also called **inertia** or **distortion** — is minimized.

$$\min_{C, \mu} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$

where $\mu_i$ is the **centroid** (mean) of cluster $C_i$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Library Organizer**
> Imagine a librarian receives 10,000 unsorted books dumped on the floor. The librarian has no labels, no genres, nothing — only the book dimensions, weight, and page count. The librarian stands in the middle, picks $k$ random desks, places one book on each desk (these are the **initial centroids**), and then:
>
> 1. **Assignment Step:** Every book is walked to the *nearest* desk (closest centroid).
> 2. **Update Step:** Each desk's location is moved to the **average position** of all books placed on it.
> 3. The process repeats until no book changes desks — at which point the books have self-organized into $k$ natural piles (clusters).
>
> This is *exactly* how K-Means works. The "desks" are centroids, the "books" are data points, and the "no book changes desks" condition is **convergence**.

### Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares):** $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ (inertia)
- **Silhouette Score:** Range $[-1, 1]$ — higher means better-defined clusters
- **Davies–Bouldin Index:** Lower is better (0 = perfect separation)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Algorithm Architecture (K-Means — Lloyd's Algorithm)

K-Means follows a two-step iterative refinement process known as **Lloyd's Algorithm (1957)**. The complete operational logic is broken down below:

### Step 1 — Initialization
- Choose $k$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \dots, \mu_k^{(0)}\}$.
- **Common strategies:**
  - **Random Initialization:** Pick $k$ random data points.
  - **Forgy Method:** Pick $k$ random observations.
  - **K-Means++:** Probabilistic seeding that spreads initial centroids apart (default in Spark MLlib).

### Step 2 — Assignment Step (Expectation)
- For each data point $x_j$, assign it to the *nearest* centroid using a **distance metric** $d(x_j, \mu_i)$.
- Standard choice: **Euclidean Distance** (squared) for computational efficiency.

$$C_i^{(t)} = \{x_j : \Vert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_j - \mu_p^{(t)} \Vert^2 \; \forall \; p = 1, 2, \dots, k\}$$

### Step 3 — Update Step (Maximization)
- Recompute each centroid as the **mean** of all points currently assigned to its cluster.

$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$

### Step 4 — Convergence Check
- Algorithm terminates when one of the following is met:
  - Centroids no longer change: $\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \mu_i^{(t)}$
  - **WCSS improvement** falls below a tolerance $\epsilon$
  - Maximum iterations reached (default: `maxIter = 20` in Spark)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "K-Means"?** The name comes from two facts: **(1)** $k$ represents the number of clusters (user-defined hyperparameter), and **(2)** each centroid update is the **mean** of assigned points.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Unit / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean Distance (2D) | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{(x_1 - y_1)^2 + (x_2 - y_2)^2}$ | Dimensionless (raw units) |
| Manhattan Distance | $d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | Sum of absolute differences |
| Squared Euclidean | $d^2(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^2$ | Used in K-Means (avoids sqrt) |
| Cosine Distance | $1 - \frac{x \cdot y}{\Vert x \Vert \, \Vert y \Vert}$ | Range: $[0, 2]$ |
| WCSS / Inertia $J$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | Lower = tighter clusters |
| Silhouette Coefficient $s(i)$ | $\frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$; higher better |
| Davies–Bouldin Index | $\frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} \frac{S_i + S_j}{M_{ij}}$ | Lower is better |
| Centroid Update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{N_i} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Vector mean of cluster |
| Elbow Formula | $\arg\min_k J(k)$ subject to inflection | Identifies optimal $k$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** K-Means is heavily used in **customer segmentation** (marketing), **image compression** (vector quantization), **document clustering** (NLP), **anomaly detection** (outliers = far from all centroids), and **distributed big data pipelines** (Spark MLlib, Mahout, MapReduce variants like K-Means||).

## MapReduce Parallelization (Big Data Context)

Since this is a **Big Data Lab**, the entire K-Means algorithm is parallelized using the **MapReduce / Spark Driver-Executor** paradigm:

- **Map Phase (Executors):** Each mapper/executor computes distances for its partition and emits $(centroid\_id, point)$.
- **Reduce Phase (Executors):** Sums points per cluster and divides by count → new centroid.
- **Driver:** Collects new centroids, checks convergence, broadcasts new centroids.

This allows K-Means to scale to **billions of points** across a cluster of commodity hardware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centroid Update Rule

**Why does taking the mean minimize WCSS for a given cluster assignment?**

We want to prove that for a fixed cluster $C_i$, the centroid $\mu_i$ that minimizes $\sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ is the **arithmetic mean**.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } f(\mu_i) &= \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2 \\
&= \sum_{x \in C_i} (x - \mu_i)^T (x - \mu_i) \\
&= \sum_{x \in C_i} \left( x^T x - 2 \mu_i^T x + \mu_i^T \mu_i \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** We expanded the squared L2 norm using the identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$, applied to vectors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{To minimize, take the gradient w.r.t. } \mu_i \text{ and set to zero:} \\
\nabla_{\mu_i} f(\mu_i) &= \sum_{x \in C_i} \left( -2x + 2\mu_i \right) = 0 \\
2 \vert C_i \vert \, \mu_i - 2 \sum_{x \in C_i} x &= 0 \\
\mu_i &= \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The derivative of a constant w.r.t. $\mu_i$ is zero, and $\sum \mu_i^T \mu_i = \vert C_i \vert \, \mu_i^T \mu_i$. Setting to zero gives the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The optimal centroid for a fixed assignment is the **arithmetic mean** of all points in the cluster. This is a *closed-form* solution — no iterative optimization required inside the Update step.

## 3.2 WCSS Convergence Proof (Monotonic Decrease)

K-Means is guaranteed to **monotonically decrease** (or maintain) the WCSS objective $J$ at every iteration.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At iteration } t, \text{ let } J^{(t)} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i^{(t)}} \Vert x - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2 \\
\text{After assignment step: } J^{(t)} &\leq J^{(t-1)} \\
\text{(because we re-assign points to nearest centroid)} \\
\text{After update step: } J^{(t+1)} &\leq J^{(t)} \\
\text{(because we move centroid to the mean, which is optimal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Both steps independently never *increase* $J$. Therefore, $J^{(0)} \geq J^{(1)} \geq J^{(2)} \geq \dots \geq 0$. Since $J$ is bounded below by 0, the sequence converges.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Given 4 points in 2D space — $A(1,1)$, $B(1,2)$, $C(5,4)$, $D(6,5)$ — perform **one full iteration** of K-Means with $k=2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1 = (1,1)$ and $\mu_2 = (5,4)$.

### Step 1 — Compute Squared Distances

$$
\begin{aligned}
d^2(A, \mu_1) &= (1-1)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0 \\
d^2(A, \mu_2) &= (1-5)^2 + (1-4)^2 = 16 + 9 = 25 \\
&\Rightarrow A \to C_1 \\
d^2(B, \mu_1) &= (1-1)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 0 + 1 = 1 \\
d^2(B, \mu_2) &= (1-5)^2 + (2-4)^2 = 16 + 4 = 20 \\
&\Rightarrow B \to C_1 \\
d^2(C, \mu_1) &= (5-1)^2 + (4-1)^2 = 16 + 9 = 25 \\
d^2(C, \mu_2) &= (5-5)^2 + (4-4)^2 = 0 \\
&\Rightarrow C \to C_2 \\
d^2(D, \mu_1) &= (6-1)^2 + (5-1)^2 = 25 + 16 = 41 \\
d^2(D, \mu_2) &= (6-5)^2 + (5-4)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\
&\Rightarrow D \to C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** For each point, we compared squared distances to both centroids and assigned the point to the *closer* one.

### Step 2 — Cluster Assignments After Iteration 1

- $C_1 = \{A, B\} = \{(1,1), (1,2)\}$
- $C_2 = \{C, D\} = \{(5,4), (6,5)\}$

### Step 3 — Recompute Centroids

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(1)} &= \left( \frac{1+1}{2}, \frac{1+2}{2} \right) = (1, 1.5) \\
\mu_2^{(1)} &= \left( \frac{5+6}{2}, \frac{4+5}{2} \right) = (5.5, 4.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Centroid is the arithmetic mean of all points in the cluster — applied separately to x-coordinates and y-coordinates.

### Step 4 — Compute WCSS

$$
\begin{aligned}
J^{(1)} &= d^2(A, \mu_1^{(1)}) + d^2(B, \mu_1^{(1)}) + d^2(C, \mu_2^{(1)}) + d^2(D, \mu_2^{(1)}) \\
&= [(1-1)^2 + (1-1.5)^2] + [(1-1)^2 + (2-1.5)^2] \\
&\quad + [(5-5.5)^2 + (4-4.5)^2] + [(6-5.5)^2 + (5-4.5)^2] \\
&= 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.5 + 0.5 \\
&= 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Squared distances were added for all 4 points using the *updated* centroids.

## 3.4 Elbow Method (Choosing Optimal $k$)

The **Elbow Method** is a heuristic for picking the optimal number of clusters $k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compute } J(k) \text{ for } k &= 1, 2, 3, \dots, k_{\max} \\
\text{Plot } J(k) \text{ vs. } k \\
\text{Find the "elbow"} &= \arg\min_k \left( J(k-1) - J(k) \right) \Big/ \left( J(k) - J(k+1) \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The elbow is the point where adding more clusters yields *diminishing returns* in WCSS reduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 K-Means Iterative Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start K-Means Algorithm]) --> inputB[Load Dataset X with n points in R-d]
    inputB --> pickC[Choose k and initialize k centroids]
    pickC --> initTypeD{Initialization Strategy}
    initTypeD -->|Forgy| forgyE[Pick k random data points as centroids]
    initTypeD -->|K-Means Plus| kmPPF[Probabilistic spread seeding]
    forgyE --> loopG
    kmPPF --> loopG
    loopG([Iteration t Begins]) --> assignH[Assignment Step: Assign each point x_j to nearest centroid mu_i using squared Euclidean distance]
    assignH --> updateI[Update Step: Recompute mu_i as mean of points in cluster C_i]
    updateI --> wcssJ[Compute WCSS J t at current centroids]
    wcssJ --> convK{Centroid shift less than epsilon or maxIter reached}
    convK -->|No| loopG
    convK -->|Yes| outputL[Output: Final cluster labels and k centroids]
    outputM([End Algorithm]) -.-> outputL
```

## 4.2 Spark Distributed Architecture (MapReduce K-Means)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph drv["DRIVER NODE"]
        ds[("HDFS / S3 Dataset")] --> sparkCtx[SparkContext]
        sparkCtx --> kmInit[KMeans Init: k=3, maxIter=20]
        kmInit --> broadcast1[Broadcast Initial Centroids]
        broadcast1 --> convergeCheck{WCSS Converged}
        convergeCheck -->|No| broadcast1
        convergeCheck -->|Yes| finalOut[Output: Cluster Centers and Labels]
    end
    subgraph exec1["EXECUTOR 1"]
        partA["Partition A: points 1 to 10K"] --> distA[Compute distance to each centroid]
        distA --> assignA[Emit nearest centroid ID per point]
    end
    subgraph exec2["EXECUTOR 2"]
        partB["Partition B: points 10K to 20K"] --> distB[Compute distance to each centroid]
        distB --> assignB[Emit nearest centroid ID per point]
    end
    subgraph exec3["EXECUTOR 3"]
        partC["Partition C: points 20K to 30K"] --> distC[Compute distance to each centroid]
        distC --> assignC[Emit nearest centroid ID per point]
    end
    broadcast1 -.-> distA
    broadcast1 -.-> distB
    broadcast1 -.-> distC
    assignA --> reduce1
    assignB --> reduce1
    assignC --> reduce1
    reduce1[Sum and count points per centroid across executors] --> newCentroids[Compute New Centroids: divide sum by count]
    newCentroids --> convergeCheck
```

## 4.3 Cluster Separation Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph cluster1["CLUSTER 1 - Centroid mu1"]
        ptA1[Point A1] --> ctr1[mu1]
        ptA2[Point A2] --> ctr1
        ptA3[Point A3] --> ctr1
        ptA4[Point A4] --> ctr1
    end
    subgraph cluster2["CLUSTER 2 - Centroid mu2"]
        ptB1[Point B1] --> ctr2[mu2]
        ptB2[Point B2] --> ctr2
        ptB3[Point B3] --> ctr2
    end
    subgraph cluster3["CLUSTER 3 - Centroid mu3"]
        ptC1[Point C1] --> ctr3[mu3]
        ptC2[Point C2] --> ctr3
        ptC3[Point C3] --> ctr3
        ptC4[Point C4] --> ctr3
    end
    intraA[Within-Cluster Distance Small] -.-> cluster1
    interA[Between-Cluster Distance Large] -.-> cluster2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme | Set A]**

### Q1. Define clustering. Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with a relevant example. [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> Clustering is an **unsupervised machine learning technique** that groups unlabeled data points into $k$ clusters based on intrinsic similarity, without prior knowledge of class labels. It uses a **distance metric** (typically Euclidean) to measure similarity.
>
> **Difference Table:**
>
> | Feature | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning (Clustering) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Labels | Uses labeled data $y$ | No labels — operates on $X$ only |
> | Goal | Learn mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Discover hidden structure / groups |
> | Example | Spam email classification | Customer segmentation |
> | Evaluation | Accuracy, F1-score | Silhouette, WCSS, Davies–Bouldin |

---

### Q2. What is WCSS? Why is it used as the objective function in K-Means? [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

> **WCSS (Within-Cluster Sum of Squares)** is the sum of squared distances between every data point and its assigned cluster centroid:
>
> $$J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$$
>
> It is used because **(1)** it has a **closed-form optimum** (centroid = mean), **(2)** it is **differentiable** and convex in $\mu_i$, and **(3)** it **monotonically decreases** during K-Means iterations, guaranteeing convergence.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme | Set B]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### **(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with a neat flowchart. Discuss any two distance metrics used in clustering. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**K-Means Algorithm Steps (Lloyd's Algorithm):**

1. **Input:** Dataset $X = \{x_1, \dots, x_n\}$, number of clusters $k$, max iterations.
2. **Initialize:** Choose $k$ initial centroids $\mu_1, \dots, \mu_k$ (random or K-Means++).
3. **Repeat until convergence:**
   - **Assignment:** For each $x_j$, assign to cluster $C_i$ such that $d(x_j, \mu_i) = \min_p d(x_j, \mu_p)$.
   - **Update:** Recompute each centroid: $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$.
4. **Output:** Final clusters $\{C_1, \dots, C_k\}$ and centroids $\{\mu_1, \dots, \mu_k\}$.

**Two Distance Metrics:**

- **Euclidean Distance:** $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d} (x_i - y_i)^2}$ — straight-line distance; sensitive to scale and outliers.
- **Manhattan Distance:** $d(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ — grid-based distance; more robust to outliers, used in high-dimensional sparse data (e.g., text).

---

#### **(b)** With a suitable example, explain the Elbow Method to find the optimal number of clusters $k$. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The **Elbow Method** plots WCSS $J(k)$ as a function of $k$ and identifies the *knee/elbow* point.

**Example Computation:**

| $k$ | WCSS $J(k)$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 |
| 2 | 450 |
| 3 | 180 |
| 4 | 165 |
| 5 | 158 |

**Analysis:** From $k=1 \to 2$, WCSS drops sharply (1000 → 450). From $k=2 \to 3$, still large drop (450 → 180). From $k=3 \to 4$, the drop is small (180 → 165) — this is the **elbow point**. Adding more clusters beyond $k=3$ yields **diminishing returns**.

**Conclusion:** Optimal $k = 3$ — chosen as the point that balances **model complexity** (small $k$) with **fit quality** (low WCSS).

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **(a)** Explain how K-Means clustering is parallelized in Apache Spark using the MapReduce paradigm. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

In **Spark MLlib's KMeans**, the algorithm is distributed across a cluster using the **Driver-Executor** model:

1. **Driver:** Holds the cluster centers, initializes $k$ centroids (using K-Means++ sampling on a fraction of data), and broadcasts them to all executors.
2. **Executors (Map Phase):** Each executor operates on a **partition** of the dataset (RDD/DataFrame), computes the **squared Euclidean distance** from each local point to every centroid, and emits a `(centroid_id, (point, 1))` tuple.
3. **Executors (Reduce Phase):** All tuples are grouped by `centroid_id` and the point vectors and counts are **summed**. The mean is computed → new centroid.
4. **Driver:** Compares new vs. old centroids; if change is below tolerance $\epsilon$ or `maxIter` reached, terminate. Else, broadcast updated centroids and loop.

**Advantages:** Scales to **billions of points**; tolerates node failures; achieves near-linear speedup.

---

#### **(b)** Discuss the limitations of K-Means clustering and explain how K-Means++ improves initialization. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Limitations of K-Means:**

1. **Sensitive to initialization:** Bad seeds → local minima, empty clusters, poor convergence.
2. **Requires $k$ upfront:** User must predefine cluster count.
3. **Assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters:** Fails for elongated, irregular, or overlapping clusters.
4. **Sensitive to outliers and noise:** Mean is not robust; a single outlier can drag the centroid.
5. **Curse of dimensionality:** Euclidean distance becomes meaningless in very high dimensions.
6. **Scale-dependent:** Features must be **standardized** (zero mean, unit variance) before clustering.

**K-Means++ Improvement:**

K-Means++ spreads initial centroids apart using **probabilistic weighted sampling**:

- Pick first centroid $\mu_1$ uniformly at random.
- For each subsequent centroid $\mu_i$, pick a point $x$ with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest already-chosen centroid:
$$P(x) = \frac{D(x)^2}{\sum_{y} D(y)^2}$$
where $D(x) = \min_{j < i} \Vert x - \mu_j \Vert$.

This guarantees initial centroids are **well-separated**, leading to faster convergence and better final WCSS. Spark MLlib uses **K-Means||** (parallelized variant) by default.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "centroid" with "medoid":** Centroid is the *mean*; medoid is the *actual data point closest to mean*. K-Means uses centroid, K-Medoids uses medoid.
> 2. **Forgetting to standardize features:** Mixing features with different scales (e.g., age in years vs. income in lakhs) will dominate the Euclidean distance. Always apply `StandardScaler` first.
> 3. **Saying "K-Means finds global optimum":** Wrong — K-Means finds a **local optimum** of WCSS. Run multiple `n_init` times and pick the best.
> 4. **Mixing up WCSS vs. Silhouette:** Lower WCSS is better; *higher* Silhouette is better. They move in opposite directions.
> 5. **Not specifying the distance metric in answers:** Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you say "compute distance" without naming it.
> 6. **Confusing `n_init` and `maxIter`:** `n_init` = number of independent runs with different seeds; `maxIter` = iterations *per run*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- Clustering = **unsupervised**; no labels used.
- K-Means minimizes **WCSS** $J = \sum \sum \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$.
- Algorithm: **Assign → Update → Check convergence** (Lloyd's).
- **Centroid = mean** of cluster points (closed-form optimum, derived via $\nabla_\mu J = 0$).
- **WCSS monotonically decreases** → guaranteed convergence (not necessarily global).
- **Euclidean distance** is default; Manhattan and Cosine are alternatives.
- **K-Means++** initialization spreads seeds via $D(x)^2$ weighted sampling.
- **Elbow method** identifies optimal $k$ from WCSS vs. $k$ plot.
- **Silhouette score** $\in [-1, 1]$: closer to $+1$ is better.
- **Davies–Bouldin Index:** lower is better.
- **Spark MapReduce parallelization:** Driver broadcasts centroids → Executors do Map (assign) + Reduce (sum/count) → Driver checks convergence.
- **Always standardize features** before clustering.
- **Limitations:** sensitive to outliers, assumes spherical clusters, requires $k$ upfront, can converge to local minima.
- **Big Data variants:** K-Means|| (parallel init), Mini-Batch K-Means (Spark MLlib), Bisecting K-Means (hierarchical).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exploring Hive with Basic Commands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exploring Hive with Basic Commands

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Apache Hive? (KTU Syllabus Definition)

**Apache Hive** is a distributed, fault-tolerant data warehouse system built on top of **Apache Hadoop** that enables analytics at a massive scale. It provides a mechanism to project structure onto already-stored semi-structured data in HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) and query that data using a SQL-like language called **HiveQL (Hive Query Language)**.

Formally, as per the KTU 2024 PCCDL607 syllabus, Hive is a **declarative, batch-oriented, SQL-on-Hadoop** engine that translates high-level SQL-like queries into **MapReduce**, **Tez**, or **Spark** jobs for execution across a Hadoop cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Examiner-Ready):**
> *Hive is an open-source data warehousing and SQL-like query software built on top of Hadoop for providing data summarization, query, and analysis. It supports querying and managing large datasets residing in distributed storage.*

### Key Architectural Properties of Hive

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Language** | HiveQL (SQL-92 subset + extensions) |
| **Default Execution Engine** | MapReduce (now Tez/Spark) |
| **Metadata Store** | **Hive Metastore** (typically MySQL/PostgreSQL/Derby) |
| **Data Storage** | HDFS / S3 / HBase / Alluxio |
| **Schema Model** | **Read Schema on Read** (vs RDBMS Read Schema on Write) |
| **Latency** | **Minutes to hours** (NOT real-time) |
| **Data Format** | TextFile, ORC, Parquet, Avro, RCFile, JSON, SequenceFile |
| **Data Granularity** | Table, Partition, Bucket |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Librarian's Catalog"**
>
> Imagine a massive library containing **billions of unsorted books** (raw HDFS files). You (the user) do not want to physically walk through the library searching for a book. Instead, you want to ask: *"Show me all books published in 2023 by Indian authors that mention 'Big Data'."*
>
> - **HDFS** = The actual library storage
> - **Hive** = The librarian's **catalog and index system**
> - **HiveQL** = Your natural English request to the librarian
> - **MapReduce/Tez** = The librarian's assistants who physically fetch, sort, and deliver the books
> - **Hive Metastore** = The cabinet where the catalog is stored
>
> The librarian (Hive) does **not** move or copy books. It simply tells the assistants (MapReduce jobs) **where** to look and **how** to filter the books based on the catalog's metadata.

### Why Not Just Use SQL Databases?

| RDBMS (MySQL/Oracle) | Hive |
|---|---|
| Schema-on-Write (strict) | **Schema-on-Read** (flexible) |
| Row-oriented storage | Columnar (ORC/Parquet) |
| Vertical scaling (scale-up) | **Horizontal scaling** (scale-out) |
| Real-time OLTP | **Batch analytics** (OLAP) |
| Terabytes | **Petabytes/Exabytes** |
| High write throughput | High **read throughput** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU One-Liner to Remember:** *Hive brings SQL familiarity to the unstructured/semi-structured world of Hadoop.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hive System Architecture — Component Breakdown

Hive architecture is composed of the following major layers. Understanding each is essential for KTU viva and lab evaluations.

### Layer 1: Hive Clients (Thrift Server, JDBC/ODBC, CLI)
Users submit HiveQL queries through:
- **Hive CLI (Command Line Interface)** — `hive>` prompt
- **Beeline** (Thrift-based CLI)
- **JDBC/ODBC drivers** for BI tools (Tableau, Python)
- **Hue / Web UI** (Hue Editor)

### Layer 2: Hive Services
- **Compiler (Driver):** Parses, plans, and optimizes the query.
- **Optimizer:** Pushes down filters, predicate analysis.
- **Execution Engine:** Converts plans to **MapReduce / Tez / Spark DAGs**.
- **HiveServer2:** Multi-client concurrent query handling (Thrift RPC).

### Layer 3: Processing & Resource Management
- **MapReduce / Tez / Spark** — actual execution layer
- **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — resource scheduling

### Layer 4: Distributed Storage
- **HDFS** — primary storage for Hive tables

### Layer 5: Metastore Layer
- Stores **schema**, **partitioning**, **table statistics**, **column types**
- Default: **Derby** (single-user, in-memory) — for production: **MySQL/PostgreSQL**

## 2.2 Hive Data Model Hierarchy

Hive data is organized in a clear hierarchy. KTU examinations frequently test this:

```
Database
  └── Table (or Partitioned Table)
        ├── Partition (key=value)
        │     └── Bucket (hash-based file groupings)
        └── Data files (stored in HDFS)
```

- **Database:** A namespace for tables (analogous to schema in RDBMS).
- **Table:** Logical entity with schema; physically a directory in HDFS.
- **Partition:** Sub-directory based on a column value (e.g., `year=2024`).
- **Bucket:** Hash-partitioned file segments within a partition (`clustered by`).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — HiveQL Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Families of HiveQL Commands**

| Category | Purpose | Example Commands |
|---|---|---|
| **DDL** — Data Definition Language | Define/modify schema | `CREATE`, `DROP`, `ALTER`, `TRUNCATE`, `SHOW`, `DESCRIBE` |
| **DML** — Data Manipulation Language | Load/insert data | `LOAD DATA`, `INSERT OVERWRITE`, `INSERT INTO` |
| **DQL** — Data Query Language | Read data | `SELECT`, `WHERE`, `GROUP BY`, `ORDER BY`, `JOIN` |
| **DCL** — Data Control Language | Permissions | `GRANT`, `REVOKE` |
| **Utility** | Settings & Functions | `SET`, `EXPLAIN`, `RESET`, **Built-in UDFs** |

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a HiveQL Query

1. **Parse:** Hive parses HiveQL into an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.
2. **Semantic Analysis:** Validates tables, columns, types via Metastore.
3. **Query Plan Generation:** Converts AST into **Logical Plan** (operator tree).
4. **Optimization:** Predicate pushdown, partition pruning, column pruning, join reordering.
5. **Physical Plan Generation:** Translates to **MapReduce / Tez DAG**.
6. **Execution:** Jobs are submitted to YARN and executed on cluster.
7. **Fetch Results:** Driver collects results and returns to client.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"What is the role of the Driver in Hive?"*
> **Answer:** The Driver acts as the controller. It receives queries, compiles them, manages the session, tracks query progress, and fetches results.

## 2.5 Hive Storage Formats — When to Use What

| Format | Type | Compression | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TextFile** | Row | Default | Raw, simple, debugging |
| **SequenceFile** | Row | Yes | Intermediate MR output |
| **RCFile** | Columnar | Yes | Hive legacy columnar |
| **ORC** (Optimized Row Columnar) | Columnar | Yes (Zlib/Snappy) | **Production analytics — recommended** |
| **Parquet** | Columnar | Yes (Snappy/Gzip) | Cross-tool analytics (Spark, Impala) |
| **Avro** | Row | Yes | Schema evolution, streaming |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Hive

- **Data Warehousing** at petabyte scale for enterprises (Netflix, Facebook).
- **ETL pipelines** in Hadoop ecosystems.
- **Log analytics** for clickstream, network, IoT data.
- **Ad-hoc SQL querying** by data analysts on raw HDFS data.
- **Data lake exploration** without forcing schema-on-write.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the **complete HiveQL workflow** as it would be performed in a KTU lab session. Each command is annotated with its functional category and explanation.

## 3.1 Launching Hive — Setup Phase

```bash
# Step 1: Start HDFS and YARN daemons
start-dfs.sh
start-yarn.sh

# Step 2: Verify Hadoop is running
jps
# Expected output includes: NameNode, DataNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager

# Step 3: Launch Hive (CLI)
hive
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `hive>` prompt indicates you are inside the Hive shell. Type `quit;` or `exit;` to leave.

## 3.2 Database Operations (DDL)

### Step 1: Create a Database
```sql
-- Create a new database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_lab;

-- Verify creation
SHOW DATABASES;
```

**Explanation:** The `IF NOT EXISTS` clause prevents an error if the database already exists. `SHOW DATABASES` lists all databases in the Metastore.

### Step 2: Use the Database
```sql
-- Switch to the new database
USE ktu_lab;
```

**Explanation:** All subsequent operations default to the `ktu_lab` namespace, similar to `USE schema_name;` in MySQL.

### Step 3: Drop a Database
```sql
-- Drop an empty database
DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS ktu_lab;

-- Drop a database containing tables (CASCADE)
DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS ktu_lab CASCADE;
```

**Explanation:** Without `CASCADE`, Hive refuses to drop a non-empty database. The `CASCADE` keyword removes all tables inside it.

## 3.3 Table Operations (DDL)

### Step 1: Create a Table with Internal Storage
```sql
-- Create a managed table for student records
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS student (
    student_id   INT,
    name         STRING,
    department   STRING,
    marks        INT,
    grade        STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;
```

**Explanation of each clause:**
- `ROW FORMAT DELIMITED` — Tells Hive the data is row-formatted text.
- `FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','` — Columns separated by commas (CSV format).
- `STORED AS TEXTFILE` — File format on HDFS.

### Step 2: Verify Table Creation
```sql
-- List all tables
SHOW TABLES;

-- View table structure
DESCRIBE student;
-- Verbose form (with Metastore details)
DESCRIBE FORMATTED student;
```

### Step 3: External Table Creation (Advanced)
```sql
-- External table — Hive does NOT delete the data on DROP
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS student_ext (
    student_id   INT,
    name         STRING,
    department   STRING,
    marks        INT,
    grade        STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/user/hive/external/student';
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal vs External Table — KTU Exam Favorite**

| Property | Internal (Managed) | External |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage location** | `/user/hive/warehouse/` | User-specified HDFS path |
| **Data on DROP** | **Data is deleted** | Data **retained** |
| **Ownership** | Hive owns the data | User owns the data |
| **Use case** | Exclusive Hive use | Shared with other tools |

## 3.4 Partitioned Table Creation

```sql
-- Partitioned table by year and department
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS student_partitioned (
    student_id   INT,
    name         STRING,
    marks        INT,
    grade        STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (year INT, department STRING)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS ORC;
```

**Enabling dynamic partitioning:**
```sql
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition = true;
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;
```

## 3.5 Data Loading (DML)

### Step 1: Prepare Local Data File
Create a local file `/home/hadoop/student_data.csv`:
```
101,Arjun,CS,85,A
102,Meera,CS,92,A
103,Rahul,EC,78,B
104,Priya,EC,88,A
105,Anjali,ME,65,C
```

### Step 2: Load Data into HDFS
```bash
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/hive/input
hdfs dfs -put /home/hadoop/student_data.csv /user/hive/input/
hdfs dfs -ls /user/hive/input/
```

### Step 3: Load Data INTO Hive Table
```sql
-- LOAD DATA does NOT execute a MapReduce job
LOAD DATA INPATH '/user/hive/input/student_data.csv'
INTO TABLE student;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** `LOAD DATA INPATH` **moves** the file from HDFS to Hive's warehouse. Use `LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH` for local files. After successful load, the original file is removed from the source path.

### Step 4: Insert Data via HiveQL
```sql
-- Single row insert
INSERT INTO TABLE student VALUES (106, 'Karthik', 'CS', 90, 'A');

-- Insert with query results
INSERT OVERWRITE TABLE student
SELECT * FROM student WHERE marks > 70;
```

**Explanation:**
- `INSERT INTO` — **appends** rows to existing data.
- `INSERT OVERWRITE` — **replaces** existing data in the table.

## 3.6 Querying Data (DQL) — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Basic SELECT
```sql
-- Retrieve all columns
SELECT * FROM student;
```

### Step 2: Filtering with WHERE
```sql
-- Students with marks greater than 80
SELECT name, marks, grade
FROM student
WHERE marks > 80;
```

### Step 3: Aggregation with GROUP BY
```sql
-- Average marks per department
SELECT department, AVG(marks) AS avg_marks
FROM student
GROUP BY department;
```

### Step 4: HAVING vs WHERE
```sql
-- Departments with average marks > 75
SELECT department, AVG(marks) AS avg_marks
FROM student
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(marks) > 75;
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** `WHERE` filters **rows before** aggregation. `HAVING` filters **groups after** aggregation.

### Step 5: Sorting
```sql
-- ORDER BY (single reducer, full sort)
SELECT * FROM student ORDER BY marks DESC;

-- SORT BY (per-reducer sort, faster)
SELECT * FROM student SORT BY marks DESC;
```

### Step 6: Joins
```sql
-- Create a second table for join demonstration
CREATE TABLE department_info (
    department   STRING,
    hod          STRING,
    location     STRING
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE;

LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH '/home/hadoop/dept.csv'
INTO TABLE department_info;

-- Inner join
SELECT s.name, s.marks, d.hod, d.location
FROM student s
JOIN department_info d
ON s.department = d.department;
```

### Step 7: Built-in Functions
```sql
-- String functions
SELECT UPPER(name), LENGTH(name) FROM student;

-- Date functions
SELECT FROM_UNIXTIME(UNIX_TIMESTAMP(), 'yyyy-MM-dd');

-- Aggregate functions
SELECT COUNT(*), MAX(marks), MIN(marks), SUM(marks) FROM student;
```

## 3.7 KTU Analytical Matrix — Hive Command Categories

| Operation | DDL/DML | HiveQL Command | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create database | DDL | `CREATE DATABASE` | `CREATE DATABASE lab;` |
| Create table | DDL | `CREATE TABLE` | See 3.3 |
| List databases | DDL | `SHOW DATABASES` | `SHOW DATABASES;` |
| View schema | DDL | `DESCRIBE` | `DESCRIBE student;` |
| Load data | DML | `LOAD DATA INPATH` | See 3.5 |
| Insert data | DML | `INSERT INTO/OVERWRITE` | See 3.5 |
| Select | DQL | `SELECT` | See 3.6 |
| Filter | DQL | `WHERE` | See 3.6 |
| Aggregate | DQL | `GROUP BY` | See 3.6 |
| Sort | DQL | `ORDER BY` | See 3.6 |
| Drop | DDL | `DROP TABLE` | `DROP TABLE student;` |
| Alter | DDL | `ALTER TABLE` | `ALTER TABLE student RENAME TO learner;` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hive High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Clients["CLIENT LAYER"]
        CLI["Hive CLI / Beeline"]
        JDBC["JDBC / ODBC"]
        UI["Hue Web UI"]
    end

    subgraph Services["SERVICE LAYER"]
        Driver["Hive Driver / Compiler"]
        Opt["Query Optimizer"]
        Exec["Execution Engine"]
        HS2["HiveServer2 (Thrift)"]
    end

    subgraph Processing["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        MR["MapReduce"]
        Tez["Apache Tez"]
        Spark["Apache Spark"]
        YARN["YARN Resource Manager"]
    end

    subgraph Storage["STORAGE LAYER"]
        HDFS["HDFS (Data Files)"]
        S3["S3 / Alluxio"]
    end

    subgraph Meta["METADATA LAYER"]
        MetaStore["Hive Metastore"]
        DB["MySQL / PostgreSQL / Derby"]
    end

    CLI --> HS2
    JDBC --> HS2
    UI --> HS2
    HS2 --> Driver
    Driver --> Opt
    Opt --> Exec
    Exec --> MR
    Exec --> Tez
    Exec --> Spark
    MR --> YARN
    Tez --> YARN
    Spark --> YARN
    YARN --> HDFS
    YARN --> S3
    Driver <--> MetaStore
    MetaStore --> DB
```

## 4.2 Hive Query Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User Submits HiveQL Query"] --> B["Driver Receives Query"]
    B --> C["Parser: AST Generation"]
    C --> D["Semantic Analysis using Metastore"]
    D --> E["Logical Plan Creation"]
    E --> F["Optimizer: Predicate Pushdown, Partition Pruning"]
    F --> G["Physical Plan: MR / Tez / Spark DAG"]
    G --> H["YARN Submits Jobs to Cluster"]
    H --> I["Executors Run Tasks on HDFS Data"]
    I --> J["Results Collected by Driver"]
    J --> K["Results Returned to Client"]
```

## 4.3 Hive Table Hierarchy and Storage Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DB["Database: ktu_lab"] --> T1["Table: student"]
    DB --> T2["Table: student_partitioned"]
    DB --> T3["External Table: student_ext"]

    T1 --> T1HDFS["/user/hive/warehouse/ktu_lab.db/student/000000_0"]

    T2 --> P1["Partition: year=2024"]
    T2 --> P2["Partition: year=2023"]
    P1 --> P1B["Bucket 1: dept=CS"]
    P1 --> P1C["Bucket 2: dept=EC"]
    P1B --> P1BFile["000000_0, 000001_0 files"]
    P1C --> P1CFile["000000_0 file"]

    T3 --> T3HDFS["/user/hive/external/student/student_data.csv"]
```

## 4.4 LOAD DATA vs INSERT Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Local["LOCAL FILESYSTEM"]
        LF["/home/hadoop/data.csv"]
    end

    subgraph HDFSIn["HDFS STAGING"]
        SI["/user/input/data.csv"]
    end

    subgraph HiveWH["HIVE WAREHOUSE /user/hive/warehouse/"]
        WT["student table data file"]
    end

    LF -->|"HDFS put / copyFromLocal"| SI
    SI -->|"LOAD DATA INPATH (moves file)"| WT
    LF -->|"LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH (copies + removes local)"| WT
    WT -->|"INSERT OVERWRITE/INTO"| WT
```

## 4.5 Schema-on-Read vs Schema-on-Write

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Write["SCHEMA-ON-WRITE (RDBMS)"]
        W1["Raw Data"] --> W2["Define Schema First"]
        W2 --> W3["Validate & Load"]
        W3 --> W4["Stored Table"]
        W4 --> W5["Query"]
    end

    subgraph Read["SCHEMA-ON-READ (Hive)"]
        R1["Raw Data"] --> R2["Store in HDFS (any format)"]
        R2 --> R3["Define Schema on Query"]
        R3 --> R4["Apply Schema at Read Time"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
**Q:** Define Apache Hive. List any two advantages of Hive over RDBMS.

**Model Answer:**

> **Apache Hive** is a data warehouse infrastructure built on top of **Apache Hadoop** that facilitates querying and managing large datasets stored in HDFS using a SQL-like language called **HiveQL**.

**Two advantages over RDBMS:**

1. **Scalability:** Hive supports **horizontal scaling** across commodity hardware, enabling it to handle petabytes of data, whereas RDBMS typically scales vertically (limited by hardware capacity).
2. **Schema Flexibility:** Hive uses **Schema-on-Read**, allowing raw, semi-structured, or unstructured data to be stored first and queried later — unlike RDBMS's strict **Schema-on-Write** which requires schema definition before ingestion.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two advantages — 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]`**
**Q:** Differentiate between Internal (Managed) and External tables in Hive.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Internal (Managed) Table | External Table |
|---|---|---|
| **Data location** | Default Hive warehouse path | User-specified HDFS path |
| **Data lifecycle** | Hive controls data; **data is deleted** on `DROP TABLE` | Hive does **not own** data; data **retained** on drop |
| **LOCATION clause** | Not required | **Required** |
| **Use case** | Hive-exclusive use | **Shared with Pig, Spark, MapReduce** |

> Hive considers Internal tables as its exclusive data asset, while External tables allow data sharing with other Hadoop ecosystem tools.

*(1 mark definition, 2 marks for differences — 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Option A**
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Frequently Asked]`**

**Q:**
**(a)** Explain the architecture of Apache Hive in detail with a neat diagram. Highlight the role of the **Hive Metastore** and **Driver**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write HiveQL commands to perform the following operations on an employee dataset (`emp_id`, `name`, `department`, `salary`, `join_date`):
   1. Create a **database** called `ktu_hr`.
   2. Create a **partitioned table** `employee` partitioned by `department` and bucketed by `emp_id` into 4 buckets.
   3. Load data from local CSV file `/home/hadoop/emp.csv`.
   4. Display the **average salary** per department, sorted in descending order.
   5. Display the **top 5 highest-paid employees** across all departments.
   (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of Apache Hive:**

The Hive architecture consists of the following key components:

1. **User Interface (UI):** Hive CLI, Beeline, Hue, or Thrift-based JDBC/ODBC clients submit HiveQL queries.

2. **Driver / Compiler:** Receives the query and creates **session handles**. The compiler parses the query, performs **semantic analysis** (using the Metastore), and generates an **execution plan** in the form of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) of MapReduce/Tez/Spark tasks.

3. **Metastore:** A **central repository** for all metadata — schemas, partition information, column types, table statistics, and SerDe properties. It is a **critical component**; without it, Hive cannot interpret data.

4. **Execution Engine:** Submits the compiled plan to Hadoop via YARN, which schedules and monitors the MapReduce/Tez jobs.

5. **HDFS:** Stores the actual data files in the warehouse directory.

> **Role of Driver:** Acts as the **controller** — manages query lifecycle, session state, progress, and result fetching.
> **Role of Metastore:** Stores **schema-on-read** metadata, enabling Hive to interpret raw HDFS data.

*(4 marks for components, 2 marks for roles, 1 mark for neat diagram — 7 marks total)*

**(b) HiveQL Commands:**

```sql
-- 1. Create database
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_hr;
USE ktu_hr;

-- 2. Create partitioned & bucketed table
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS employee (
    emp_id      INT,
    name        STRING,
    salary      DOUBLE,
    join_date   STRING
)
PARTITIONED BY (department STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (emp_id) INTO 4 BUCKETS
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS ORC;

-- 3. Load data from local CSV
LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH '/home/hadoop/emp.csv'
INTO TABLE employee PARTITION (department='CS');
-- Repeat for other departments with dynamic partition

-- Enable dynamic partition for non-first partitions
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition = true;
SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition.mode = nonstrict;

-- 4. Average salary per department (descending order)
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employee
GROUP BY department
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

-- 5. Top 5 highest-paid employees
SELECT emp_id, name, department, salary
FROM employee
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 5;
```

*(1.4 marks each for 5 sub-parts — 7 marks total)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Option B** (Internal Choice Alternative)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]`**

**Q:**
**(a)** What is the difference between `ORDER BY` and `SORT BY` in Hive? Explain `partitioning` and `bucketing` with examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Demonstrate the following operations on a Hive table `sales(pid`, `product`, `category`, `price`, `qty`):
   1. Create an **external table** with CSV format, data located at `/data/sales`.
   2. Insert 5 sample rows.
   3. Find the **total revenue** (price × qty) per category.
   4. Find all products in the category with revenue > `₹10,000`.
   5. Drop the external table and verify the HDFS file is **not deleted**.
   (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ORDER BY vs SORT BY, Partitioning & Bucketing:**

| Aspect | ORDER BY | SORT BY |
|---|---|---|
| **Sort scope** | **Global** sort (all data on single reducer) | **Local** sort (per reducer) |
| **Performance** | Slow for large data | Faster, parallel |
| **Use case** | Final sorted output | Pre-sorted data for downstream |

**Partitioning** splits a table into **sub-directories** based on column values, enabling **partition pruning** during queries (e.g., `WHERE year=2024`).

```sql
PARTITIONED BY (year INT)
```

**Bucketing** further subdivides partitions into **hash-based files**, useful for **efficient sampling** and **map-side joins**.

```sql
CLUSTERED BY (id) INTO 4 BUCKETS
```

*(3 marks for ORDER BY vs SORT BY, 2 marks for partitioning, 2 marks for bucketing — 7 marks total)*

**(b) External Table Operations on `sales`:**

```sql
-- 1. Create external table
CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sales (
    pid       INT,
    product   STRING,
    category  STRING,
    price     DOUBLE,
    qty       INT
)
ROW FORMAT DELIMITED
FIELDS TERMINATED BY ','
STORED AS TEXTFILE
LOCATION '/data/sales';

-- 2. Insert 5 sample rows
INSERT INTO sales VALUES
(1, 'Laptop', 'Electronics', 55000.0, 2),
(2, 'Headphones', 'Electronics', 2500.0, 10),
(3, 'Notebook', 'Stationery', 50.0, 200),
(4, 'Pen', 'Stationery', 20.0, 500),
(5, 'Chair', 'Furniture', 3500.0, 5);

-- 3. Total revenue per category
SELECT category, SUM(price * qty) AS total_revenue
FROM sales
GROUP BY category;

-- 4. Products in high-revenue categories
SELECT s.product, s.category, (s.price * s.qty) AS revenue
FROM sales s
JOIN (
    SELECT category
    FROM sales
    GROUP BY category
    HAVING SUM(price * qty) > 10000
) high_rev
ON s.category = high_rev.category;

-- 5. Drop the external table — verify HDFS file persists
DROP TABLE sales;

-- Verify:
-- hdfs dfs -ls /data/sales
-- The file remains in HDFS because it is an EXTERNAL table.
```

*(1.4 marks each for 5 sub-parts — 7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **LOAD DATA INPATH** does **not** run a MapReduce job — it only **moves/copies** files. Do not confuse it with `INSERT`.
> - Forgetting the **CASCADE** keyword while dropping a non-empty database is a common error.
> - Using **ORDER BY** on huge datasets is a common performance mistake; prefer **SORT BY + LIMIT** or **distribute by** for distributed sorting.
> - In **external tables**, the `LOCATION` clause is **mandatory**; data is not deleted on drop.
> - Dynamic partitioning requires `SET hive.exec.dynamic.partition = true;` — exam answers often miss this setup.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module Experiment 2**

- ☐ Hive is a **data warehouse** layer over Hadoop providing **SQL-like (HiveQL)** query capability.
- ☐ Hive supports **DDL, DML, DQL, DCL, and Utility** commands.
- ☐ **Hive Metastore** stores schema; production deployments use MySQL/PostgreSQL.
- ☐ **Hive Driver** is the central controller — parses, compiles, optimizes, and executes queries.
- ☐ **Internal (Managed) tables** store data in `/user/hive/warehouse/` and Hive owns the data.
- ☐ **External tables** use a user-specified HDFS `LOCATION` and Hive does not delete data on DROP.
- ☐ **LOAD DATA INPATH** moves files from HDFS source to Hive warehouse (no MR job).
- ☐ **LOAD DATA LOCAL INPATH** copies from local filesystem.
- ☐ **INSERT INTO** appends rows; **INSERT OVERWRITE** replaces them.
- ☐ **WHERE** filters rows before aggregation; **HAVING** filters groups after.
- ☐ **ORDER BY** = global sort (single reducer); **SORT BY** = local sort (per reducer).
- ☐ **Partitioning** creates sub-directories for column values (e.g., `year=2024`).
- ☐ **Bucketing** splits data into hash-based files; used for sampling and map-joins.
- ☐ **ORC** and **Parquet** are the recommended columnar storage formats for analytics.
- ☐ Built-in functions include **UPPER, LENGTH, UNIX_TIMESTAMP, FROM_UNIXTIME, AVG, MAX, MIN, SUM, COUNT**.
- ☐ Hive is **not** suitable for real-time/OLTP — designed for **batch analytics** (OLAP).
- ☐ Execution engines supported: **MapReduce** (default), **Tez** (faster), **Spark** (in-memory).
- ☐ KTU lab command sequence: **Start Hadoop → HDFS put → Launch Hive → CREATE → LOAD → SELECT**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Model Design Using NoSQL Databases Such as Hive or Cassandra
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Data Model Design Using NoSQL Databases Such as Hive or Cassandra

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Data Model Design** in the context of NoSQL databases refers to the process of logically and physically structuring semi-structured or unstructured data into a layout that is optimized for the specific query, write, and scalability characteristics of a non-relational storage engine. Unlike Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS), where the schema is fixed *before* data ingestion (schema-on-write), NoSQL systems adopt a flexible, often **schema-on-read** philosophy where the data is interpreted at query time.

For this experiment, the two canonical NoSQL paradigms are:

- **Apache Hive**: A data warehousing infrastructure built on top of Apache Hadoop that provides SQL-like query capabilities (HiveQL / HQL), batch-oriented OLAP processing, and schema-on-read semantics over HDFS-resident files.
- **Apache Cassandra**: A distributed, wide-column NoSQL store engineered for high write throughput, linear horizontal scalability, and tunable consistency, modeled around the **Google Bigtable** whitepaper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive**: The experiment requires you to *design* (not merely query) a data model. You are expected to justify the choice of storage layout, partition key, clustering column, and physical file format.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blueprint of a Skyscraper

Imagine you are an architect designing two completely different buildings on the same empty plot of land:

1. **Hive is a Library Stack Building**: The plot already exists (HDFS holds the raw files). You decide *after* the books are placed which shelves, sections, and partitions to use. Readers (analysts) interpret the data when they query. You can reorganize shelves cheaply.

2. **Cassandra is a Custom Mail-Routing Warehouse**: The moment a parcel arrives, you *must* know its exact bin number, which aisle, which shelf, and which slot — because a conveyor belt will never see it again. The bin number is derived from a **partition key**. Once placed, retrieval depends entirely on how cleverly you chose that bin number.

| Database | Storage Philosophy | Access Philosophy |
|----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Hive** | Schema-on-Read (flexible) | Batch / OLAP / SQL-like |
| **Cassandra** | Schema-on-Write (defined) | Real-time / Key-based / Tunable |
| RDBMS (for contrast) | Schema-on-Write (strict) | Transactional / ACID |

### 1.3 Core Vocabulary Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term Definitions for the Lab Record**
> - **Keyspace** (Cassandra): The top-level namespace analogous to a "database" in RDBMS. Defines replication strategy and replication factor.
