> - **Table** (Cassandra) / **Column Family**: A distributed hash-partitioned structure of rows. Rows in a Cassandra table are uniquely identified by a *primary key*, not a single column.
> - **Partition Key**: The hashable subset of the primary key used by the consistent hashing ring to determine which node owns the data.
> - **Clustering Key**: The remaining part of the primary key that determines the on-disk sort order of rows *within* a single partition.
> - **Managed Table** (Hive): A table whose lifecycle is controlled by Hive; data is deleted when the table is dropped.
> - **External Table** (Hive): A reference to pre-existing HDFS data; dropping the table does not delete the underlying files.
> - **Partitioning** (Hive): Horizontally slicing a table by the value of one or more columns, creating sub-directories in HDFS.
> - **Bucketing** (Hive): Hash-distributing rows within a partition into a fixed number of files, enabling efficient sampling and joins.

### 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in Big Data Engineering

A poorly designed Cassandra table causes **hot partitions** (one node doing all the work). A poorly designed Hive table causes **full table scans** and **small file problems**. The data model *is* the performance contract.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Modeling Mentalities

Hive and Cassandra solve fundamentally different problems. Understanding *why* their models differ is the single highest-yield concept in this experiment.

**Hive** is built on **HDFS**, which is a Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM) file system. Because HDFS files are immutable blocks, Hive can append data cheaply but cannot update individual rows in place. Therefore:
- Hive models are **write-once, read-many** OLAP tables.
- The cost of a bad query is a *full HDFS scan* (terabytes read).
- Design priority: **prune partitions, use columnar formats (ORC, Parquet), enable predicate pushdown**.

**Cassandra** is built on **distributed hash rings**. Every read/write is a peer-to-peer coordinator request. Therefore:
- Models are designed **per query path**.
- A query that does not specify the partition key triggers an *expensive cluster-wide scan* and is generally forbidden in production.
- Design priority: **one table per query pattern**, denormalized heavily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Heuristic (Cassandra)**: For every question your application asks, ask: "Is the partition key in the WHERE clause? Is the clustering key used for range scans?" If the answer is *no* to both, redesign.

### 2.2 The CAP Theorem Lens

Cassandra is an **AP system** (Availability + Partition tolerance) under default settings. The data model *must* assume eventual consistency. The data model must therefore embed **last-write-wins semantics** or application-side conflict resolution through columns like `Writetime(ts)` or `version (int)`.

Hive inherits Hadoop's consistency model: it is a batch analytical engine where data is immutable between loads, so consistency is trivially maintained at the file level.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Governing Equation or Rule | Standard Unit / Default |
|---|---------|-----------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Cassandra Replication Factor | RF | $N_{\text{copies}} = RF$ | Default: **3** |
| 2 | Cassandra Consistency Level | CL | $W + R > RF$ for strong consistency | ONE, QUORUM, ALL |
| 3 | Quorum Size | Q | $Q = \left\lfloor \frac{RF}{2} \right\rfloor + 1$ | e.g., RF=3 → Q=2 |
| 4 | Partition Hashing | $\mathrm{hash}(k_p)$ | $\mathrm{token} = \mathrm{Murmur3\text{-}128}(k_p) \bmod 2^{127}$ | 128-bit token ring |
| 5 | Hot Partition Trigger | $\rho$ | $\rho = \frac{N_{\text{rows in one partition}}}{N_{\text{rows average}}}$ | Warn if $\rho > 10$ |
| 6 | Hive Partition Pruning | $P_{\text{pruned}}$ | $P_{\text{pruned}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Partitions scanned}}{\text{Partitions total}}$ | Target: $> 0.9$ |
| 7 | Hive Bucket Math | $b_i$ | $b_i = \mathrm{hash}(b_{\text{col}}) \bmod N_{\text{buckets}}$ | Default: 1 bucket |
| 8 | Cassandra Row Size Ceiling | $S_{\text{row}}$ | $S_{\text{row}} < 100 \text{ MB}$ (hard), $< 10 \text{ MB}$ (recommended) | Megabytes |
| 9 | Cassandra Columns per Row | $C_{\text{row}}$ | $C_{\text{row}} < 2 \text{ billion}$ (theoretical) | Hard limit |
| 10 | ORC Compression Ratio | $\gamma$ | $\gamma = \frac{S_{\text{raw}}}{S_{\text{ORC}}}$ | Typical: **3x to 8x** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **W + R > RF** rule is the single most-tested formula on Cassandra in KTU exams. If Write Consistency $W = 1$ and Read Consistency $R = 1$, the sum $1 + 1 = 2$ which is **not** greater than $RF = 3$. So that combination offers *eventual* consistency, not *strong* consistency.

### 2.4 Hive Data Model — Hierarchical Anatomy

A Hive database is a namespace; a table is a logical mapping over HDFS files. A table can be:

- **Managed (Internal)**: Hive owns the data directory. `CREATE TABLE ...` (default).
- **External**: Hive references data owned by another process (e.g., Spark, Flume). `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE ... LOCATION '/path'`.

A table can be **partitioned** by one or more columns, creating a directory tree of the form:

`/warehouse/db/table/partition_col1=v1/partition_col2=v2/data.orc`

Each partition can be **bucketed** into $N$ hash-bucketed files, used to optimize map-side joins and sampling.

### 2.5 Cassandra Data Model — The Five-Layer Cake

$$\text{Keyspace} \;\supset\; \text{Table} \;\supset\; \text{Partition} \;\supset\; \text{Row} \;\supset\; \text{Column}$$

- **Keyspace** holds replication settings.
- **Table** declares the schema (static columns, regular columns, primary key).
- **Partition** is the unit of physical distribution. All rows sharing a partition key are co-located on the same node and sorted by the clustering key.
- **Row** is identified by the full primary key.
- **Column** is the leaf cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Columns in Cassandra**: A column declared `STATIC` stores only one value per partition, not per row. This is useful for "metadata about the partition itself" (e.g., `customer_name` static, while `order_id` is the clustering key).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce Order History** → Cassandra (huge write volume, partitioned by `customer_id`, clustered by `order_date`).
- **Daily Sales Reporting** → Hive (batch aggregation over partitioned ORC tables).
- **IoT Telemetry Ingestion** → Cassandra (time-series with `(device_id, event_time)` composite key).
- **Log Mining for Clickstream Funnels** → Hive (partitioned by `dt`, bucketed by `user_id`, ORC format).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Design Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The walkthrough below is a fully reproducible lab design — your record should show analogous derivations for the case study assigned in class.

### 3.1 Use Case: University Course Registration System

We will design the data model for a system that answers two questions:

1. **Analyst Question (Hive)**: *"Compute the total credits registered per department per semester for the last 5 years."* → This is an OLAP scan, perfect for Hive.
2. **Application Question (Cassandra)**: *"For student S1001, show all courses registered in the current semester, sorted by registration timestamp."* → This is a single-partition OLTP lookup, perfect for Cassandra.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Hive Data Model Design

**Step 1 — Identify the grain.** The grain is *one row per student-course-semester registration*.

**Step 2 — Choose partition columns.** A query filters by `academic_year` and `semester` almost always. Therefore partition on `(academic_year, semester)`.

**Step 3 — Choose bucket column.** Joins with the `student` dimension are common. Bucket on `student_id` with 32 buckets to enable a `map-side SMB join`.

**Step 4 — Choose file format.** Use **ORC** with `Snappy` compression. ORC supports predicate pushdown, run-length encoding, and columnar reads.

**Step 5 — Write the DDL.**

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS university_olap;
USE university_olap;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS course_registration (
    student_id        STRING,
    student_name      STRING,
    department_code   STRING,
    course_code       STRING,
    course_title      STRING,
    credits           INT,
    grade             STRING,
    registration_ts   TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITIONED BY (academic_year STRING, semester STRING)
CLUSTERED BY (student_id) INTO 32 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES (
    'orc.compress'        = 'SNAPPY',
    'orc.create.index'    = 'true',
    'transactional'       = 'false'
);
```

**Step 6 — Justify the design.**

- The `(academic_year, semester)` partition prunes 99% of files when querying a single semester.
- 32 buckets keep each file between ~128 MB and ~256 MB, matching HDFS block size.
- ORC + Snappy gives ~5x compression on string-heavy academic data.

**Step 7 — Load a partition.**

```sql
LOAD DATA INPATH '/staging/reg_2024_s5.csv'
INTO TABLE course_registration
PARTITION (academic_year='2024', semester='S5');
```

**Step 8 — Run the analyst query.**

```sql
SELECT department_code, SUM(credits) AS total_credits
FROM course_registration
WHERE academic_year = '2024' AND semester = 'S5'
GROUP BY department_code;
```

This query exploits partition pruning, ORC predicate pushdown, and columnar projection — the textbook optimization pattern for Hive.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Cassandra Data Model Design

**Step 1 — Identify the access pattern.**

> *"Given a `student_id`, fetch all registrations in semester `S5`, ordered by `registration_ts` descending."*

**Step 2 — Choose the primary key composition.**

- The **partition key** must be `student_id` (every query starts here).
- The **clustering key** must support sorting by time within a partition: `(semester, registration_ts)`.

**Step 3 — Use a composite partition key if hot partitions are a risk.** For 100,000 students, each partition holds ~50 rows per semester — safe. No composite partition key needed.

**Step 4 — Add a static column for immutable student metadata.**

**Step 5 — Write the CQL DDL.**

```cql
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS university_oltp
  WITH replication = {
    'class': 'SimpleStrategy',
    'replication_factor': 3
  };

USE university_oltp;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS student_registration_by_student (
    student_id        TEXT,
    semester          TEXT,
    registration_ts   TIMESTAMP,
    course_code       TEXT,
    department_code   TEXT,
    credits           INT,
    -- Static column: one value per partition (per student)
    student_name      TEXT STATIC,
    program_code      TEXT STATIC,
    PRIMARY KEY ((student_id), semester, registration_ts, course_code)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (semester ASC, registration_ts DESC, course_code ASC)
  AND compaction = {
    'class': 'TimeWindowCompactionStrategy',
    'compaction_window_unit': 'DAYS',
    'compaction_window_size': 1
  };
```

**Step 6 — Justify every clause.**

- `PRIMARY KEY ((student_id), semester, registration_ts, course_code)` — The double parentheses around `student_id` declare it as the *sole* partition key.
- `CLUSTERING ORDER BY (semester ASC, registration_ts DESC, ...)` — Newest-first within semester, no in-memory sort needed at read time.
- `TimeWindowCompactionStrategy` — TTL-friendly for time-series data; old windows are dropped wholesale.
- `STATIC` columns denormalize student name and program into the same table to avoid a second query.

**Step 7 — Insert a record.**

```cql
INSERT INTO student_registration_by_student
  (student_id, semester, registration_ts, course_code,
   department_code, credits, student_name, program_code)
VALUES
  ('S1001', 'S5', toTimestamp(now()), 'CSL411', 'CSE', 4,
   'Ananya Krishnan', 'B.Tech-CSE');
```

**Step 8 — Query using the partition key.**

```cql
SELECT course_code, department_code, credits, registration_ts
FROM student_registration_by_student
WHERE student_id = 'S1001' AND semester = 'S5'
LIMIT 20;
```

This query is a **single-partition, single-node read** — completing in single-digit milliseconds regardless of cluster size.

### 3.4 Derivation — Consistency Math for Cassandra

Suppose $RF = 3$ and we choose `QUORUM` for both reads and writes.

$$Q = \left\lfloor \frac{3}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 = 2$$

$$R + W = 2 + 2 = 4 \;\text{copies touched}$$

$$R + W = 4 > RF = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Strong consistency guaranteed}$$

If we instead choose $R = 1, W = 1$:

$$R + W = 1 + 1 = 2 \;\not>\; 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Eventual consistency only}$$

The two settings are valid trade-offs: QUORUM is correct, ONE/ONE is fast.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decisions Side by Side

| Design Lever | Hive Decision | Cassandra Decision | KTU Justification |
|--------------|---------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Storage engine | HDFS (immutable files) | Local SSTables per node | Different fault models |
| Primary identifier | Row offset + partition dir | Composite primary key | Cassandra needs explicit PK |
| Update strategy | ACID since Hive 3.x (limited) | Last-write-wins per column | Immutability vs LWW |
| Sort order | None globally (only per file) | Clustering order per partition | Cassandra is sorted |
| Read path | MapReduce / Tez / Spark scan | Single-partition read | Batch vs real-time |
| Indexing | Partition columns + bucketing | Secondary indexes (limited), SASI | Different query goals |
| Compression | ORC + Snappy/Zlib | Table-level compression | Both reduce I/O |
| Data locality | Block replication (3x HDFS) | Token-aware replication | Hadoop vs Dynamo model |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hive Data Model Hierarchy

```mermaid
        H4 --> H5[BI Dashboard]
    end

    subgraph REALTIME_PATH[OLTP Path: Cassandra]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Distinguish between schema-on-read and schema-on-write. Which one does Apache Hive follow and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Schema-on-write** (1 mark): The schema is enforced *before* data is written into the database. Any row violating the schema is rejected at insertion. Traditional RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL) follow this model.
- **Schema-on-read** (1 mark): The data is stored in its raw form on disk; the schema is applied by the query engine at read time. Inconsistent or extra fields are tolerated.
- **Hive's position** (1 mark): Hive is **schema-on-read** because the underlying HDFS files are immutable and may originate from any upstream system (Flume, Spark, Sqoop). Hive only validates the schema at query compile time, not at load time. This allows cheap ingestion and schema evolution.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is a partition key in Cassandra and how does it differ from a clustering key?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Partition key** (1.5 marks): The hashable portion of the primary key used by Cassandra's consistent hash ring to determine which node owns a row. All rows sharing the same partition key are co-located on the same node.
- **Clustering key** (1.5 marks): The remaining portion of the primary key that determines the sort order of rows *within* a partition. It does not affect node placement but controls how a range query is answered.
- *Distinction in one line*: Partition key = *where* the data lives. Clustering key = *how* the data is sorted inside that location.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> - Do not write generic definitions of "NoSQL". The examiner wants to see **primary key decomposition**, **partition key justification**, and **query traceability**.
> - In Hive questions, missing the **partition column** in the DDL costs 3 marks instantly.
> - In Cassandra questions, forgetting the **double parentheses** around the partition key in the PRIMARY KEY clause is a 2-mark deduction.
> - Always state the **replication factor** and **consistency level** in any Cassandra design answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Design a Hive table to store daily e-commerce orders with the following requirements: **(7 marks)**
- Columns: `order_id`, `customer_id`, `product_id`, `quantity`, `unit_price`, `order_status`, `order_timestamp`.
- Queries typically filter by year and month, and join with the customer dimension.
- Provide the complete DDL, explain the partition and bucketing strategy, and justify the file format.

**(b)** For the same use case, design a Cassandra table to answer the query: *"Fetch the most recent 50 orders placed by a given customer."* Provide the CQL DDL, justify the primary key, and explain how you would avoid hot partitions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Hive DDL (7 marks)**

```sql
CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ecommerce_olap;
USE ecommerce_olap;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS daily_orders (
    order_id        STRING,
    customer_id     STRING,
    product_id      STRING,
    quantity        INT,
    unit_price      DECIMAL(10,2),
    order_status    STRING,
    order_timestamp TIMESTAMP
)
PARTITIONED BY (order_year INT, order_month INT)
CLUSTERED BY (customer_id) INTO 64 BUCKETS
STORED AS ORC
TBLPROPERTIES (
    'orc.compress'      = 'SNAPPY',
    'orc.create.index'  = 'true'
);
```

**Justification (distributed across the 7 marks):**

1. **Grain** (1 mark): One row per order line item.
2. **Partition columns** (2 marks): `order_year` and `order_month` enable time-range queries to prune ~99% of files. Hierarchical partitioning mirrors the typical analytical access pattern of "give me Q3 2024".
3. **Bucketing** (2 marks): 64 buckets on `customer_id` enable **Sorted Merge Bucket (SMB) joins** with the customer dimension table bucketed on the same column, eliminating the reduce-side shuffle.
4. **File format** (1 mark): **ORC** with Snappy compression supports columnar projection (only `unit_price` and `quantity` need to be read for revenue queries) and predicate pushdown on `order_status`.
5. **Query example** (1 mark):

```sql
SELECT customer_id, SUM(quantity * unit_price) AS revenue
FROM daily_orders
WHERE order_year = 2024 AND order_month = 7
GROUP BY customer_id;
```

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Cassandra DDL (7 marks)**

```cql
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS ecommerce_oltp
  WITH replication = {
    'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy',
    'datacenter1': 3
  };

USE ecommerce_oltp;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders_by_customer (
    customer_id   TEXT,
    order_year    INT,
    order_ts      TIMESTAMP,
    order_id      TEXT,
    product_id    TEXT,
    quantity      INT,
    unit_price    DECIMAL,
    order_status  TEXT,
    PRIMARY KEY ((customer_id, order_year), order_ts, order_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (order_ts DESC, order_id ASC)
  AND default_time_to_live = 31536000;
```

**Justification (distributed across the 7 marks):**

1. **Composite partition key** (3 marks): `(customer_id, order_year)` is enclosed in double parentheses, declaring it as the partition key. This prevents a hot partition when one mega-customer places millions of orders (the `customer_id`-only key would balloon a single partition beyond the 100 MB hard limit). Each year becomes a separate, bounded partition.
2. **Clustering order** (2 marks): `order_ts DESC` means the "most recent 50 orders" query is a *reverse range scan* starting at the partition head — no in-memory sort needed.
3. **Hot partition avoidance** (1 mark): The composite key splits one customer's data across $N$ year-partitions, each ~few MB, well within the 10 MB recommendation.
4. **Query** (1 mark):

```cql
SELECT order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price, order_status
FROM orders_by_customer
WHERE customer_id = 'CUST9001' AND order_year = 2024
LIMIT 50;
```

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the Cassandra data model hierarchy (Keyspace → Table → Partition → Row → Column). How does the partition key differ from the primary key? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Cassandra table to model a hospital's patient admission records so that the following query is efficient: *"For a given patient, list all admissions in the last 3 years, newest first, including the admitting doctor and ward."* Provide CQL, explain the choice of partition and clustering keys, and discuss any denormalization. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) (7 marks)**

1. **Keyspace** (1 mark): The outermost container, analogous to an RDBMS database. It defines the **replication strategy** (SimpleStrategy for single-DC, NetworkTopologyStrategy for multi-DC) and the **replication factor**.
2. **Table** (1 mark): A schema-defined set of rows. Unlike RDBMS, a Cassandra table has a *strict* primary key but flexible regular columns.
3. **Partition** (1.5 marks): The unit of physical distribution. All rows sharing the same partition key are stored together on one node and replicated according to the keyspace's RF.
4. **Row** (1 mark): A unique combination of partition key + clustering key values; physically stored sorted by the clustering key.
5. **Column** (0.5 mark): The leaf cell, identified by `partition_key + clustering_key + column_name`.
6. **Primary key vs partition key** (2 marks):
   - **Primary key** = `partition_key [ + clustering_keys...]`
   - **Partition key** = the *first* element (or tuple) inside the primary key, used for hashing.
   - Example: `PRIMARY KEY ((dept_id), emp_id, joining_date)` → partition key is `dept_id`; clustering keys are `emp_id` and `joining_date`.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) (7 marks)**

```cql
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS hospital_oltp
  WITH replication = {'class': 'NetworkTopologyStrategy', 'dc1': 3};

USE hospital_oltp;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS admissions_by_patient (
    patient_id        TEXT,
    admission_year    INT,
    admission_ts      TIMESTAMP,
    admission_id      TEXT,
    admitting_doctor  TEXT,
    ward              TEXT,
    diagnosis_code    TEXT,
    discharge_ts      TIMESTAMP,
    -- Static patient demographics
    patient_name      TEXT STATIC,
    date_of_birth     DATE STATIC,
    blood_group       TEXT STATIC,
    PRIMARY KEY ((patient_id, admission_year), admission_ts, admission_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (admission_ts DESC, admission_id ASC);
```

**Justification (distributed across the 7 marks):**

1. **Partition key** (2.5 marks): `(patient_id, admission_year)` — composite partition. The patient ID alone could create a huge partition if the patient has decades of history; bucketing by year bounds each partition to roughly one year's worth of admissions.
2. **Clustering key** (2 marks): `(admission_ts DESC, admission_id ASC)` — newest admissions are read first, matching the application requirement.
3. **Denormalization** (1.5 marks): `patient_name`, `date_of_birth`, `blood_group` are declared `STATIC` so they are stored once per partition, avoiding a second patient-master lookup query.
4. **Query** (1 mark):

```cql
SELECT admission_id, admitting_doctor, ward, diagnosis_code
FROM admissions_by_patient
WHERE patient_id = 'P-78421'
  AND admission_year IN (2022, 2023, 2024);
```

This translates to 3 single-partition reads, executed in parallel by the coordinator, each returning rows pre-sorted by `admission_ts DESC`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Lab Exam**

- [ ] **Hive is schema-on-read; Cassandra is schema-on-write** (within its CQL schema constraints).
- [ ] **Hive tables live in HDFS**; **Cassandra tables live in per-node SSTables**.
- [ ] **Hive partition = directory**, **Hive bucket = file within directory**.
- [ ] **Cassandra partition key** goes inside **double parentheses** in the PRIMARY KEY clause.
- [ ] **Cassandra clustering key** controls on-disk sort order *within* a partition.
- [ ] **One table per query pattern** is the Cassandra design rule (denormalization is mandatory).
- [ ] **Static columns** store one value per partition (used for partition-level metadata).
- [ ] **Hot partition** = one node doing all the work; mitigated by composite partition keys.
- [ ] **Strong consistency** in Cassandra requires $R + W > RF$.
- [ ] **ORC + Snappy** is the de facto standard Hive storage layout for analytics.
- [ ] **Managed vs External** Hive tables differ in who deletes the data on `DROP TABLE`.
- [ ] **TimeWindowCompactionStrategy** is preferred for time-series Cassandra tables.
- [ ] **Row size limit** in Cassandra: hard 100 MB, recommended < 10 MB.
- [ ] **Bucketing on the join column** in Hive enables map-side SMB joins.
- [ ] **CQL `ALLOW FILTERING`** is forbidden in production designs (it implies a missing partition key).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pig Latin Scripts to Sort, Group, Join, Project, and Filter Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Pig Latin Scripts to Sort, Group, Join, Project, and Filter Data ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Apache Pig Latin** is a high-level, procedural data-flow language designed for analyzing large, semi-structured, and unstructured datasets in the Hadoop ecosystem. It is executed by the **Pig execution engine**, which compiles Pig Latin scripts into a sequence of **MapReduce jobs** that run on top of **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)**. The five relational operators covered in this experiment form the foundational transformation backbone of any Pig Latin script: | Operator | Category | Function | |----------|----------|----------| | **LOAD / STORE** | I/O Operators | Ingest input / Persist output | | **FOREACH … GENERATE** | Projection | Column selection / derivation | | **FILTER** | Selection | Row-level conditional pruning | | **ORDER BY** | Sorting | Total order on one or more keys | | **GROUP / JOIN** | Aggregation | Tuple partitioning / relation merging | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Weightage:** Experiment 2 carries **15 marks** in the continuous evaluation component (8 marks for record + 7 marks for viva-voce + execution), with questions typically framed on **Pig Latin script syntax debugging, operator semantics, and output-trace prediction**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" Intuition Imagine a massive **soft-drink bottling plant** receiving truckloads of mixed bottles (raw data tuples): - **LOAD** → The unloading dock receives the raw bottles from trucks. - **FILTER** → A quality-check gatekeeper **rejects cracked bottles** (rows not satisfying a predicate). Only the clean ones move forward. - **FOREACH … GENERATE (Project)** → A labeling machine **picks specific information** (e.g., "Brand" and "Volume") to print on caps — it **projects** only selected columns. - **ORDER BY (Sort)** → A conveyor sorter arranges bottles **in ascending order of volume** on the line. - **GROUP BY** → A packing station **clusters identical items** into cases (e.g., all "Cola" bottles together). - **JOIN** → A merging station **combines two streams** — say, "Bottles" stream merged with "Prices" stream using a common **Bottle-ID key** — to produce a single enriched stream. > [!NOTE] > **Mental Model:** Every Pig Latin script is a *directed acyclic graph (DAG)* of data transformations. The script declares **what** to do, not **how**; Pig's optimizer decides the MapReduce execution plan — this is called **lazy evaluation**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded) - **Default Hadoop Block Size:** **128 MB** (HDFS physical chunk). - **Pig's Default Parallelism:** **1 reducer** (controlled via `SET default_parallel N;`). - **Tuple Cardinality Limit:** Practically unbounded; constrained by cluster memory. - **NULL Semantics:** In Pig, unknown values propagate — `NULL + 5 = NULL`. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Operator Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Operator-by-Operator Theoretical Breakdown #### 2.1.1 `LOAD` — Data Ingestion - **Why:** HDFS files are byte streams; Pig requires schema-aware tuples. - **How:** The `PigStorage(',')` loader applies a delimiter-based parser and projects a user-defined schema. The schema is declared inline using the `AS` clause. #### 2.1.2 `FILTER` — Predicate-Based Row Selection - **Why:** Raw data is voluminous and noisy; analytics needs pruning. - **How:** Uses **Boolean expressions** with operators `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`, `matches` (regex), `is null`, `is not null`. Multiple predicates combine via `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`. #### 2.1.3 `FOREACH … GENERATE` — Projection & Derivation - **Why:** Reduce I/O by carrying only relevant columns; enable new derived fields. - **How:** Iterates tuple-by-tuple. Inside `GENERATE`, you can call **built-in functions** (`UPPER`, `LOWER`, `SUBSTRING`, `SIZE`, `AVG`, `SUM`, `COUNT`) or **User-Defined Functions (UDFs)**. #### 2.1.4 `ORDER BY` — Total Sort - **Why:** Required for downstream tasks (top-N, ranked reports, sorted joins). - **How:** Internally uses a **single reducer by default** to guarantee total order — this is a **performance bottleneck**. The `PARALLEL` clause enables multi-reducer sorting with a secondary tie-breaker. #### 2.1.5 `GROUP BY` — Tuple Partitioning - **Why:** Aggregations (sum, count, avg) operate on **groups**, not individual rows. - **How:** Produces a relation where each tuple has **two fields**: `group` (the key) and the **bag of all tuples sharing that key**. The grouping is a **shuffle + sort** phase in MapReduce. #### 2.1.6 `JOIN` — Relation Merging - **Why:** Normalized data in separate files must be reconstructed for analytics. - **How:** Pig implements: - **Inner Join (default):** Cartesian-product filter on equal keys. - **Left Outer Join:** Preserves all left tuples; unmatched right side becomes `NULL`. - **Right Outer Join:** Mirror of left. - **Full Outer Join:** Preserves both unmatched sides. - **Replicated Join (`USING 'replicated'`):** Small table loaded into memory of every mapper — efficient for one small + one large relation. - **Skewed Join (`USING 'skewed'`):** Handles data skew by sampling and rebalancing. ### 2.2 KTU Operator Cheat Sheet (Syntax Reference) > [!NOTE] > Memorize the **lowercase operator keywords** in Pig Latin — they are case-insensitive but conventionally lowercase. | Operation | Pig Latin Syntax (Skeleton) | Returns | |-----------|-----------------------------|---------| | Load | `relation = LOAD 'hdfs_path' USING PigStorage(',') AS (f1, f2:int, f3);` | Relation (Bag of Tuples) | | Filter | `filtered = FILTER relation BY condition;` | Relation | | Project / Derive | `projected = FOREACH relation GENERATE f1, f2*2 AS doubled, UPPER(f3);` | Relation | | Sort Ascending | `sorted = ORDER relation BY f2 ASC;` | Relation (single-reducer) | | Sort Descending | `sorted = ORDER relation BY f2 DESC;` | Relation | | Group By | `grouped = GROUP relation BY f1;` | Relation with `(group, bag)` | | Inner Join | `joined = JOIN A BY id, B BY id;` | Relation | | Left Outer Join | `joined = JOIN A BY id LEFT OUTER, B BY id;` | Relation | | Dump (console) | `DUMP relation;` | Side-effect to stdout | | Store (HDFS) | `STORE result INTO 'hdfs_out' USING PigStorage(',');` | Persists relation | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In production-grade **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** pipelines of enterprises like **LinkedIn, Twitter, Yahoo**, Pig Latin is used to: - Clean clickstream logs (`FILTER` invalid events). - Aggregate daily active users (`GROUP BY user_id` → `COUNT`). - Enrich transactional data with user demographics (`JOIN transactions BY user_id, users BY user_id`). - Generate sorted top-K leaderboards for A/B test dashboards (`ORDER BY metric DESC; LIMIT 10;`). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Exhaustive Walk-Through) ### 3.1 Reference Sample Dataset Consider two CSV files loaded into HDFS for the experiment: **File 1: `students.txt`** (comma-separated, no header) ``` 101,Arjun,CS,85 102,Meera,EC,72 103,John,CS,90 104,Anu,ME,55 105,Raj,CS,68 ``` **File 2: `departments.txt`** ``` CS,Computer Science,A-Block EC,Electronics,B-Block ME,Mechanical,C-Block ``` ### 3.2 Analytical Walk-Through: Five-Operator Composition Script Below is a **single composite Pig Latin script** that exercises **all five operators** end-to-end, with exhaustive inline reasoning. ```pig -- ============================================================ -- KTU Experiment 2 : Composite Pig Latin Script -- Operations : LOAD, FILTER, FOREACH, ORDER, GROUP, JOIN -- ============================================================ -- Step 1: LOAD the student records with an explicit schema students = LOAD 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/students.txt' USING PigStorage(',') AS (sid:int, name:chararray, dept:chararray, marks:int); -- Step 2: LOAD the department master data depts = LOAD 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/departments.txt' USING PigStorage(',') AS (dept_code:chararray, dept_name:chararray, block:chararray); -- Step 3: FILTER students with marks >= 60 (pass criterion) passed = FILTER students BY marks >= 60; -- Step 4: PROJECT selected columns and DERIVE a new "grade" field graded = FOREACH passed GENERATE sid, UPPER(name) AS name_caps, dept, marks, (marks >= 80 ? 'Distinction' : (marks >= 70 ? 'First Class' : 'Second Class')) AS grade; -- Step 5: SORT the graded students by marks DESCENDING sorted_by_marks = ORDER graded BY marks DESC; -- Step 6: GROUP the sorted data by department for aggregation grouped_by_dept = GROUP sorted_by_marks BY dept; -- Step 7: COMPUTE per-department aggregates using FOREACH on the grouped bag dept_summary = FOREACH grouped_by_dept GENERATE group AS department, COUNT(sorted_by_marks) AS total_students, AVG(sorted_by_marks.marks) AS avg_marks, MAX(sorted_by_marks.marks) AS top_score; -- Step 8: JOIN department summary with the departments master table -- to enrich with block location enriched = JOIN dept_summary BY department, depts BY dept_code; -- Step 9: FINAL PROJECTION for the report final_report = FOREACH enriched GENERATE dept_summary::department AS dept, dept_summary::total_students AS count, dept_summary::avg_marks AS avg_score, depts::block AS location; -- Step 10: PERSIST the result back to HDFS STORE final_report INTO 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/output/dept_report' USING PigStorage(','); ``` ### 3.3 Logical Step-Trace (Step-by-Step Transformation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Pitfall:** Examiners frequently test **output-trace prediction**. You must know the **intermediate state** after every operator, not just the final output. Let the **input relation** $R_0$ = `students` have cardinality $\vert R_0 \vert = 5$. | Step | Logical Operator | Mathematical Set Operation | Resulting Relation $R_i$ | Cardinality $\vert R_i \vert$ | |------|------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------| | 1 | `LOAD` | $R_1 = \text{Parse}(R_0)$ | `students` | **5** | | 2 | `FILTER BY marks >= 60` | $R_2 = \{\, t \in R_1 \mid t.\text{marks} \ge 60 \,\}$ | `passed` | **4** (excludes Anu=55) | | 3 | `FOREACH GENERATE` | $R_3 = \pi_{\{sid, name, dept, marks, grade\}}(R_2)$ | `graded` | **4** | | 4 | `ORDER BY marks DESC` | $R_4 = \text{Sort}_{\downarrow}(R_3)$ | `sorted_by_marks` | **4** (90, 85, 72, 68) | | 5 | `GROUP BY dept` | $R_5 = \{ (k, B_k) \mid B_k = \{ t \in R_4 \mid t.\text{dept}=k \} \}$ | `grouped_by_dept` | **2** groups (CS:3, EC:1) | | 6 | `FOREACH (agg)` | $R_6 = \text{Aggregate}(R_5)$ | `dept_summary` | **2** | | 7 | `JOIN` (inner) | $R_7 = R_6 \bowtie_{\text{dept}=\text{dept\_code}} R_{\text{depts}}$ | `enriched` | **2** | | 8 | `FOREACH GENERATE` (final) | $R_8 = \pi_{\{\text{dept, count, avg, loc}\}}(R_7)$ | `final_report` | **2** | The **project operator** $\pi$ (Greek *pi*) in relational algebra is mathematically defined as: $$ \pi_{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n}(R) = \{\, (t.A_1, t.A_2, \ldots, t.A_n) \mid t \in R \,\} $$ where $t.A_i$ denotes the value of attribute $A_i$ in tuple $t$. The **selection operator** $\sigma$ (Greek *sigma*) corresponding to `FILTER` is: $$ \sigma_{P}(R) = \{\, t \in R \mid P(t) = \text{true} \,\} $$ where $P$ is a Boolean predicate. ### 3.4 Final Output Predicted ``` CS,3,81.0,A-Block EC,1,72.0,B-Block ``` **Verification reasoning:** - Computer Science has 3 passing students (Arjun 85, John 90, Raj 68) → average $\frac{85+90+68}{3} = 81.0$. - Electronics has 1 passing student (Meera 72) → average 72.0. - Mechanical has 0 passing students (Anu=55 filtered out) → group is **eliminated** by the inner join, not kept. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** An **inner join** drops unmatched groups. To retain them, use `LEFT OUTER` and replace NULLs with `0` using `FOREACH … GENERATE … (dept_summary::total_students IS NULL ? 0 : dept_summary::total_students)`. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Pig Latin Execution Flow — From Script to MapReduce DAG The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a Pig Latin script is compiled into a logical plan, optimized, and then physically executed as a chain of MapReduce jobs on the Hadoop cluster. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["User writes
script.pig"] --> B["Pig Parser
Lexical + Syntax Analysis"] B --> C{"Validation
Schema & Types OK?"} C -- "No" --> D["Syntax Error
Displayed on Grunt Shell"] C -- "Yes" --> E["Logical Plan
DAG of Operators"] E --> F["Pig Optimizer
Rule-Based Rewrites"] F --> G["Physical Plan
MapReduce Job Sequence"] G --> H["Hadoop JobTracker
Schedules MR Jobs"] H --> I["HDFS Read
Input Splits"] I --> J["Map Phase
Map Functions"] J --> K["Shuffle and Sort
GroupByPartition"] K --> L["Reduce Phase
Aggregations + Joins"] L --> M["HDFS Write
Output Part Files"] M --> N["Final Result
Stored in Target Path"] ``` ### 4.2 Operator Chaining — Composite Data-Flow Architecture This diagram maps the **logical sequence** of relational operators used in the walk-through script of Section 3. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["students.txt
HDFS Input"] --> A2["LOAD
Apply Schema"] A3["departments.txt
HDFS Input"] --> A4["LOAD
Apply Schema"] A2 --> B1["FILTER
marks >= 60"] B1 --> C1["FOREACH
Add grade field"] C1 --> D1["ORDER BY
marks DESC"] D1 --> E1["GROUP BY
dept"] E1 --> F1["FOREACH
COUNT AVG MAX"] F1 --> G1["JOIN
Inner on dept code"] A4 --> G1 G1 --> H1["FOREACH
Final Projection"] H1 --> I1["STORE
HDFS Output"] ``` ### 4.3 Join Type Decision Tree (KTU Quick-Reference) ```mermaid flowchart TD J0["Need to merge two
relations by key?"] --> J1{"Are unmatched
rows needed?"} J1 -- "No, strict matching" --> J2["Use INNER JOIN
Default syntax"] J1 -- "Yes, from Left side" --> J3["Use LEFT OUTER JOIN
Right side becomes NULL"] J1 -- "Yes, from Right side" --> J4["Use RIGHT OUTER JOIN
Left side becomes NULL"] J1 -- "Yes, from both sides" --> J5["Use FULL OUTER JOIN
Both sides fill NULL"] J1 -- "Small table + Large table" --> J6{"Small table fits
mapper memory?"} J6 -- "Yes" --> J7["Replicated JOIN
USING 'replicated'"] J6 -- "No, but skewed keys" --> J8["Skewed JOIN
USING 'skewed'"] ``` ### 4.4 Group-By vs. Join — Comparative Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GRP["GROUP BY operation"] G1["Input: Single Relation R"] --> G2["Partition tuples by
key value K"] G2 --> G3["Output: bag of tuples
per unique K"] end subgraph JNN["JOIN operation"] J1["Input: Relation A"] --> J2["Cartesian product
followed by filter"] J3["Input: Relation B"] --> J2 J2 --> J4["Output: merged tuples
matching on common key"] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Lab, 3 Marks]` **Differentiate between `FILTER` and `FOREACH … GENERATE` operators in Pig Latin with one example each.** **Model Answer:** - `FILTER` is a **selection** operator that performs **row-level** pruning. It returns only those tuples that satisfy a Boolean predicate; the schema of the relation remains unchanged. ```pig high_scorers = FILTER students BY marks > 75; ``` - `FOREACH … GENERATE` is a **projection** operator that performs **column-level** selection and **derivation** of new fields. The output schema can differ from the input schema. ```pig report = FOREACH students GENERATE name, marks, (marks > 75 ? 'Top' : 'Avg') AS tier; ``` **Key Distinction:** `FILTER` controls **which rows** flow forward; `FOREACH … GENERATE` controls **which columns** (and derived values) appear in those rows. --- #### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, 3 Marks]` **What is the difference between a `GROUP` and a `JOIN` in Pig Latin? When would you prefer a Replicated Join over a default inner join?** **Model Answer:** - `GROUP` operates on a **single relation** and partitions tuples into bags based on a key, producing output of the form `(group, bag-of-tuples)`. It is typically followed by an aggregation step. - `JOIN` operates on **two or more relations** and merges tuples from different sources based on a common key, producing a **flattened output** with attributes from both relations. **When to prefer Replicated Join:** When one of the relations is **small enough to fit entirely in the memory of every mapper** (typically < a few hundred MB), using `JOIN A BY id, B BY id USING 'replicated';` **avoids the shuffle phase entirely**, since the small table is replicated to all mappers — yielding significant speedup. --- ### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Given the input file `sales.txt` with fields `(txn_id:int, product:chararray, region:chararray, amount:double)` loaded into a Pig relation `sales`, **write a complete Pig Latin script** to: 1. Filter out transactions with `amount <= 0`. 2. Project the tuple into `(product, region, amount_in_INR)` where `amount_in_INR = amount * 83.0`. 3. Sort the result in **descending** order of `amount_in_INR`. 4. Group the sorted data by `(product, region)` pair and compute the **total revenue** per pair. 5. Store the final output to `/ktu/lab2/output/revenue` using comma delimiter. **Model Solution:** ```pig -- (i) Load with explicit schema sales = LOAD 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/sales.txt' USING PigStorage(',') AS (txn_id:int, product:chararray, region:chararray, amount:double); -- (ii) FILTER: discard non-positive amounts valid = FILTER sales BY amount > 0.0; -- (iii) FOREACH: project + derive INR amount inr_view = FOREACH valid GENERATE product, region, amount * 83.0 AS amount_in_INR; -- (iv) ORDER BY: descending sort sorted = ORDER inr_view BY amount_in_INR DESC; -- (v) GROUP BY composite key grouped = GROUP sorted BY (product, region); -- (vi) FOREACH aggregation: total revenue revenue = FOREACH grouped GENERATE group.product AS product, group.region AS region, SUM(sorted.amount_in_INR) AS total_revenue; -- (vii) STORE the output STORE revenue INTO 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/output/revenue' USING PigStorage(','); ``` **Explanation of each block:** *(carries 1 mark each for correctness of the filter, project, sort, group, aggregate, and store steps; the remaining 1 mark is for proper schema declaration and indentation.)* **(b) [7 Marks]** Suppose after running the above script, a colleague reports that the **Mechanical Engineering group is missing from the output** even though there were valid transactions. Identify the **two most likely causes** and **propose a Pig Latin modification** to retain the missing group. **Model Solution:** **Cause 1 — Filtering in the wrong stage:** If a `FILTER` was placed *after* a `GROUP BY region`, and Mechanical was grouped with empty bag, downstream `JOIN` with a master table would still show it. The actual issue is more subtle: if a `JOIN` was added in a later step, an **inner join** drops the Mechanical rows if the right-side master table lacks that key. *(3 marks)* **Cause 2 — Use of inner join with a master table that has no "ME" entry:** When the script is extended to enrich with a `regions.txt` master file, and that file does not contain `ME`, the inner join discards all Mechanical tuples. *(2 marks)* **Proposed Modification — Use a LEFT OUTER JOIN and handle NULL aggregation explicitly:** ```pig -- Replace the final JOIN with LEFT OUTER enriched = JOIN revenue BY region LEFT OUTER, regions BY region_code; -- Then, fill NULL totals with 0 using a conditional expression final = FOREACH enriched GENERATE revenue::product AS product, revenue::region AS region, (revenue::total_revenue IS NULL ? 0.0 : revenue::total_revenue) AS total_revenue, regions::region_name AS name; ``` **Alternatively**, simply remove any downstream `JOIN` to keep all groups intact. *(2 marks for valid fix and syntax)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **execution flow** of a Pig Latin script — from script submission to final result — including the role of the **Parser, Optimizer, Compiler, and Execution Engine**. Draw a labeled block diagram. **Model Solution:** **Step-by-step execution flow:** *(4 marks for narrative)* 1. **Script Submission:** The user submits `script.pig` via Grunt shell, `pig -f script.pig`, or PigServer API. 2. **Parsing:** Pig's **parser** performs lexical analysis (tokenization) and syntactic analysis (grammar check), producing a **parse tree**. Type-checking and schema validation occur here. 3. **Logical Plan Generation:** The parse tree is converted into a **Logical Plan** — a DAG of logical operators (Load, Filter, Join, etc.). 4. **Optimization:** The **Pig Optimizer** applies rule-based rewrites — predicate pushdown, column pruning, map-side join selection, combiner insertion — to produce an **optimized logical plan**. 5. **Physical Plan Compilation:** The **compiler** translates the optimized logical plan into a **Physical Plan** consisting of concrete MapReduce jobs with precise data-flow semantics (mapper, partitioner, reducer). 6. **Submission to Hadoop:** The **Execution Engine** submits the MapReduce jobs to the Hadoop **JobTracker / YARN ResourceManager**. 7. **Execution:** Each job reads from HDFS, runs **Map → Shuffle → Reduce** phases, and writes intermediate / final outputs back to HDFS. 8. **Result Retrieval:** Pig pulls the final results; if `DUMP` is used, output is printed to console; if `STORE` is used, results are persisted in HDFS. **Labeled Block Diagram:** *(3 marks for diagram)* ``` +----------+ +----------+ +----------------+ | Script |-->| Parser |-->| Logical Plan | +----------+ +----------+ +----------------+ | v +----------------+ | Optimizer | +----------------+ | v +----------------+ | Physical Plan | | (MapReduce DAG)| +----------------+ | v +----------------+ | Execution Eng. | | (Hadoop) | +----------------+ | v +----------------+ | HDFS Result | +----------------+ ``` **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider two relations: `employees (emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept_id:int, salary:double)` and `departments (dept_id:int, dept_name:chararray, location:chararray)`. **Write a Pig Latin script** that: 1. Performs a **LEFT OUTER JOIN** between `employees` and `departments` on `dept_id`. 2. Filters only those tuples where the employee's **salary is greater than the department's average salary** (use nested FOREACH with AVG). 3. Projects `(name, dept_name, salary, location)` and sorts by `salary DESC`. 4. Stores the result. **Model Solution:** ```pig -- (1) Load both relations employees = LOAD 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/employees.txt' USING PigStorage(',') AS (emp_id:int, name:chararray, dept_id:int, salary:double); departments = LOAD 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/departments.txt' USING PigStorage(',') AS (dept_id:int, dept_name:chararray, location:chararray); -- (2a) Compute department-wise average salary FIRST dept_avg = GROUP employees BY dept_id; dept_avg_salary = FOREACH dept_avg GENERATE group AS dept_id, AVG(employees.salary) AS avg_sal; -- (2b) LEFT OUTER JOIN employees with departments emp_dept = JOIN employees BY dept_id LEFT OUTER, departments BY dept_id; -- (2c) JOIN with the dept-avg relation with_avg = JOIN emp_dept BY employees::dept_id, dept_avg_salary BY dept_id; -- (2d) FILTER: salary > average above_avg = FILTER with_avg BY emp_dept::employees::salary > dept_avg_salary::avg_sal; -- (3) PROJECT required columns and SORT final = FOREACH above_avg GENERATE emp_dept::employees::name AS name, emp_dept::departments::dept_name AS dept_name, emp_dept::employees::salary AS salary, emp_dept::departments::location AS location; sorted_final = ORDER final BY salary DESC; -- (4) STORE the result STORE sorted_final INTO 'hdfs:///ktu/lab2/output/above_avg_emp' USING PigStorage(','); ``` **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for join correctness (LEFT OUTER syntax), 2 marks for nested aggregation (GROUP + AVG), 2 marks for the FILTER predicate, 1 mark for final STORE. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps Identified by Senior KTU Evaluators:** > > 1. **Forgetting the `AS` schema clause in `LOAD`** — the relation becomes schema-less, causing `FOREACH` field references to fail at runtime. **Penalty: Up to 2 marks.** > 2. **Case-sensitivity in field names** — Pig Latin is case-insensitive for *keywords*, but **field/alias names are case-sensitive**. Writing `Marks` vs `marks` in different lines causes a `FIELD_NOT_FOUND` error. > 3. **Using a single `=` for equality check** — Pig uses `==` (double-equals) for comparison; a single `=` is an **assignment** (and may not even be valid inside `FILTER BY`). Always write `FILTER … BY marks == 100;`. > 4. **Ambiguous field reference after a JOIN** — When two relations have a column with the same name (e.g., both have `dept_id`), the output must be disambiguated using the `relation::column` qualifier. **Penalty: Up to 3 marks** for runtime failures. > 5. **Order of `GROUP` and `FILTER`** — Filtering *after* grouping wastes computation. Best practice: **filter first, group later** (predicate pushdown). Examiners may deduct marks if the order is inefficient for the question. > 6. **Confusing `ORDER BY` with `SORT`** — `SORT` does **not exist** in Pig Latin. The correct keyword is `ORDER BY`. Typing `SORT` produces a parser error. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Pig Latin** is procedural + data-flow oriented; **Pig translates scripts into MapReduce** — no manual Mapper/Reducer coding needed. - **Lazy Evaluation:** Nothing executes until a `DUMP` or `STORE` is reached — you can iteratively build pipelines. - **`LOAD … AS (schema)`** declares types: `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `chararray`, `bytearray`, `boolean`, `datetime`, `bag`, `tuple`, `map`. - **`FILTER BY`** uses **comparison operators** (`==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`) and **logical operators** (`AND`, `OR`, `NOT`); supports `matches` (regex) and `is null` / `is not null`. - **`FOREACH … GENERATE`** can call **Eval functions** (`AVG`, `SUM`, `COUNT`, `MAX`, `MIN`, `CONCAT`, `SIZE`, `UPPER`, `LOWER`, `SUBSTRING`, `TOKENIZE`, `TRIM`, `REPLACE`). - **`ORDER BY`** triggers a **single-reducer** job by default — use `PARALLEL N;` to enable multi-reducer sort for performance. - **`GROUP BY`** transforms each unique key into `(group_key, bag_of_tuples)` — the canonical prelude to aggregation. - **JOIN types:** `JOIN` (inner), `LEFT OUTER`, `RIGHT OUTER`, `FULL OUTER`, `JOIN … USING 'replicated'`, `JOIN … USING 'skewed'`. - **Field disambiguation after a join** is mandatory: use `relation_name::field_name`. - **`DUMP` vs `STORE`:** `DUMP` prints to console (debugging); `STORE` persists to HDFS. - **Best-practice operator order** for efficiency: `LOAD → FILTER → FOREACH (project) → JOIN/GROUP → ORDER → STORE`. - **Pig's data types are nullable by default** — handle `NULL` explicitly with `IS NULL` checks in `FOREACH` ternaries. - **Schemas are optional but recommended** — typed schemas enable compile-time error detection by Pig. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Viva Voce Lines to Memorize:** > - "Pig Latin is a **data-flow language**, not a procedural one." > - "Pig Latin scripts are converted into **MapReduce jobs** by the Pig execution engine." > - "`GROUP` and `JOIN` both shuffle data; `GROUP` produces bags, `JOIN` produces flattened tuples." > - "Pig follows **lazy evaluation** — `DUMP` or `STORE` triggers execution." ---

topics.title: Installation, Deployment, and Configuration of Apache Spark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL607 - BIG DATA PROCESSING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Installation, Deployment, and Configuration of Apache Spark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Spark** is an **open-source, distributed, in-memory cluster-computing framework** designed for **fast, iterative, and large-scale data processing**. It extends the **MapReduce** model of *Apache Hadoop* by supporting **Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs)**, **Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)**, and an advanced **DAG Scheduler** that pipelines operations to optimize execution. Spark can run on a *standalone cluster manager*, *Apache Mesos*, *Hadoop YARN*, or *Kubernetes*.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme *PCCDL607 – Big Data Processing Lab*, this experiment is graded on:
- Successful **standalone / pseudo-distributed installation** of Spark on **Ubuntu Linux 22.04 LTS**
- Correct configuration of `JAVA_HOME`, `SPARK_HOME`, `HADOOP_HOME`
- Launching and verifying **PySpark shell**, **Spark Shell (Scala)**, and **Spark Web UI**
- Demonstration of a **WordCount** or simple aggregation program to validate deployment

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Spark is like a super-charged kitchen for cooking huge meals."**
>
> - Imagine you need to cook dinner for **10,000 people**. One chef (a single computer) cannot do it.
> - You hire **50 chefs** (a **cluster of worker nodes**), divide the recipe (the **dataset / RDD**) into 50 portions, and a **head chef (the Driver / Master)** coordinates them.
> - Each chef keeps the chopped vegetables (intermediate results) **on the counter** instead of putting them back in the fridge — that "counter space" is **in-memory storage (RAM)**, which is what makes Spark ~**100×** faster than traditional MapReduce for iterative jobs.
> - The **scheduler** is the head chef deciding *who cooks what, in what order, and which dishes can be done in parallel*.

## 1.3 Spark's Position in the Big Data Stack

| Layer | Technology | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | **HDFS / S3 / Local FS** | Distributed persistent data |
| Resource Mgmt. | **YARN / Mesos / Standalone / K8s** | Allocates CPU/RAM to jobs |
| Execution Engine | **Apache Spark** | Computes on data |
| API / Language | **Scala, Python (PySpark), R, Java** | User-facing development |
| Libraries | **Spark SQL, MLlib, GraphX, Structured Streaming** | Domain-specific modules |

## 1.4 Key System Metrics You Must Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU lab viva, memorize these standard Spark constants/limits:
> - **Default port for Spark Master Web UI:** **`4040`** (application) / **`8080`** (standalone master) / **`8081`** (worker)
> - **Default driver memory:** **`1g`**
> - **Default executor memory:** **`1g`**
> - **Default parallelism / partitions:** `200` (cluster) or `sc.defaultParallelism` (local)
> - **Latest stable Spark (KTU 2024 reference):** **Spark 3.5.x** running on **JDK 8 / JDK 11**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Spark Cluster Architecture — Operational Logic

A Spark cluster follows a **Master–Worker architecture** (when using Standalone cluster manager):

1. **Driver Program (Master-side process)** — Runs the `main()` function. It contains:
   - `SparkContext` — the entry point; connects to the Cluster Manager.
   - **DAG Scheduler** — Converts RDD lineage into stages of tasks.
   - **Task Scheduler** — Sends tasks to executors.
2. **Cluster Manager** — Allocates resources. In KTU labs this is typically **Spark Standalone**.
3. **Worker Nodes** — Host **Executors**, which are JVM processes that:
   - Run **Tasks**
   - Store data in **Block Manager** (cache + shuffle storage)
   - Report back to the driver

## 2.2 Spark Deployment Modes (Critical Viva Question)

| Mode | Driver Location | Use Case | KTU Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| **local[n]** | Same JVM (n threads) | Testing on laptop | Initial lab setup |
| **local[*]** | All cores | Quick prototyping | `pyspark` verification |
| **standalone (client)** | On the submitting machine | Interactive / debug | Most common in lab |
| **standalone (cluster)** | On a worker inside the cluster | Production batch jobs | Optional advanced |
| **YARN (client/cluster)** | Inside YARN | Hadoop ecosystem | If Hadoop pre-installed |
| **Kubernetes** | Inside a K8s pod | Cloud-native | Out of KTU lab scope |

## 2.3 The Step-by-Step "Why" of Installation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love asking **"Why did you set `JAVA_HOME`?"** — here is the complete logic chain.

- **Spark is written in Scala**, which compiles to **JVM bytecode**. Therefore a **Java Development Kit (JDK ≥ 8)** is the *fundamental runtime requirement* — without it the launcher `spark-submit` throws `ClassNotFoundException`.
- **Scala 2.12.x or 2.13.x** is bundled with pre-built Spark binaries (no separate install needed for *binaries*).
- **Python 3.8+** is needed only if using **PySpark**. Spark does *not* require Python for the Scala/Java API.
- **`HADOOP_HOME`** is required only if you intend to interact with **HDFS** or use pre-built Spark binaries compiled for a specific Hadoop version (e.g., `spark-3.5.0-bin-hadoop3`).
- **`SPARK_HOME`** is the environment variable pointing to the Spark installation directory — every `spark-submit` / `pyspark` command resolves binaries from here.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Configuration Cheat Sheet

| Configuration File | Location | Purpose | Critical Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| `spark-env.sh` | `$SPARK_HOME/conf/` | Sets environment variables for workers & master | `JAVA_HOME`, `SPARK_MASTER_HOST`, `SPARK_MASTER_PORT`, `SPARK_WORKER_CORES`, `SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY` |
| `spark-defaults.conf` | `$SPARK_HOME/conf/` | Default Spark application settings | `spark.master`, `spark.executor.memory`, `spark.driver.memory`, `spark.cores.max` |
| `workers` (formerly `slaves`) | `$SPARK_HOME/conf/` | List of worker node hostnames | One hostname per line |
| `log4j2.properties` | `$SPARK_HOME/conf/` | Logging verbosity | Set `rootLogger.level = warn` for quiet output |
| `metrics.properties` | `$SPARK_HOME/conf/` | Metrics reporting | Default fine for labs |

## 2.5 Mandatory Environment Variables (KTU 2024 Lab Manual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always set these in `~/.bashrc` (or `~/.zshrc` on macOS) and `source` it before running Spark.

| Variable | Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `JAVA_HOME` | `/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64` | JDK path |
| `SPARK_HOME` | `/opt/spark` or `~/spark-3.5.0-bin-hadoop3` | Spark install root |
| `HADOOP_HOME` | `/opt/hadoop` (optional) | Hadoop binaries |
| `PATH` (append) | `$SPARK_HOME/bin:$SPARK_HOME/sbin:$HADOOP_HOME/bin:$JAVA_HOME/bin` | Allows `spark-submit` etc. from anywhere |
| `PYTHONPATH` | `$SPARK_HOME/python:$SPARK_HOME/python/lib/py4j-0.10.9.7-src.zip` | PySpark finds Spark internals |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, the same installation pattern taught in KTU labs scales to:
- **Cloud clusters** (AWS EMR, Databricks, GCP Dataproc) — same binaries, different bootstrap scripts.
- **Edge analytics** (single-node IoT gateways) — `local[*]` mode.
- **Hybrid HPC + Spark** for genomics, fraud detection, ETL pipelines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Installation Procedure & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> KTU lab exams require a **step-by-step procedure** with **expected output** for each step. Below is the canonical, board-valuation-ready procedure for installing Spark 3.5.x on **Ubuntu 22.04 LTS** in **Standalone Single-Node (pseudo-distributed)** mode — the most common lab configuration.

## 3.1 Step 1 — System Prerequisite Verification

**Logic:** Before any installation, confirm the OS, kernel, and available RAM/CPU. Spark needs **at least 8 GB RAM and 4 cores** for a smooth lab experience.

```bash
# Open terminal (Ctrl + Alt + T)
$ uname -a
$ lsb_release -a
$ nproc               # number of cores
$ free -h             # RAM check
```

**Expected Output (sample):**

```
Linux lab-pc 5.15.0-...  x86_64 GNU/Linux
Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS
4
              total        used        free      avail
Mem:           7.7Gi       1.2Gi       5.8Gi       6.2Gi
```

## 3.2 Step 2 — Install Java Development Kit (JDK)

**Logic:** Spark is JVM-based → Java is mandatory → KTU accepted versions are **JDK 8, 11, or 17**. We choose **OpenJDK 11** (Spark 3.5 official support).

```bash
$ sudo apt update
$ sudo apt install -y openjdk-11-jdk
$ java -version
$ javac -version
```

**Expected Output:**

```
openjdk version "11.0.20" 2023-07-18
OpenJDK Runtime Environment (build 11.0.20+8-post-Ubuntu-1ubuntu122.04)
javac 11.0.20
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Find JAVA_HOME path** (you need this exact string for `spark-env.sh`):
> ```bash
> $ readlink -f $(which java) | sed 's|/bin/java||'
> # Returns e.g.  /usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64
> ```

## 3.3 Step 3 — (Optional) Install Python 3 for PySpark

**Logic:** PySpark is part of the KTU 2024 syllabus. Python ≥ 3.8 is required.

```bash
$ sudo apt install -y python3 python3-pip
$ python3 --version
$ pip3 --version
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Python 3.10.12
pip 22.0.2 ...
```

## 3.4 Step 4 — Download and Extract Apache Spark

**Logic:** Use the **pre-built binary** (no compilation needed) matched to your Hadoop version. We use `hadoop3` because it is the most compatible in 2024.

```bash
$ cd /opt
$ sudo wget https://dlcdn.apache.org/spark/spark-3.5.0/spark-3.5.0-bin-hadoop3.tgz
$ sudo tar -xzf spark-3.5.0-bin-hadoop3.tgz
$ sudo mv spark-3.5.0-bin-hadoop3 spark        # clean name
$ sudo chown -R $USER:$USER /opt/spark
```

**Expected Output:**

```
# No output on success; verify with:
$ ls /opt/spark/bin | head
```

```
docker-image-tool.sh
find-spark-home
load-spark-env.sh
pyspark
spark-class
spark-shell
spark-sql
spark-submit
```

## 3.5 Step 5 — Configure Environment Variables in `~/.bashrc`

**Logic:** Append environment exports so every new terminal inherits them.

```bash
$ nano ~/.bashrc
# Append the following block at the end:
```

```bash
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64
export SPARK_HOME=/opt/spark
export HADOOP_HOME=/opt/hadoop          # set even if Hadoop absent
export PATH=$PATH:$JAVA_HOME/bin:$SPARK_HOME/bin:$SPARK_HOME/sbin
export PYTHONPATH=$SPARK_HOME/python:$SPARK_HOME/python/lib/py4j-0.10.9.7-src.zip:$PYTHONPATH
export PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3
```

```bash
$ source ~/.bashrc
$ echo $SPARK_HOME       # verify
```

**Expected Output:**

```
/opt/spark
```

## 3.6 Step 6 — Configure `spark-env.sh`

**Logic:** This file tells every Spark daemon (master + workers) which Java to use and how many resources to claim.

```bash
$ cd $SPARK_HOME/conf
$ cp spark-env.sh.template spark-env.sh
$ nano spark-env.sh
```

Add the following lines:

```bash
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64
export SPARK_MASTER_HOST=localhost
export SPARK_MASTER_PORT=7077
export SPARK_MASTER_WEBUI_PORT=8080
export SPARK_WORKER_CORES=2
export SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY=2g
export SPARK_WORKER_WEBUI_PORT=8081
export SPARK_DAEMON_JAVA_OPTS="-Dspark.deploy.recoveryMode=FILESYSTEM"
```

## 3.7 Step 7 — Configure `workers` File

**Logic:** Lists every machine that will run a worker process. For a **single-node lab cluster**, this is just `localhost`.

```bash
$ cd $SPARK_HOME/conf
$ cp workers.template workers
$ nano workers
# Replace 'localhost' (it is already there) — keep it.
```

```
localhost
```

## 3.8 Step 8 — Configure `spark-defaults.conf` (Optional but recommended)

**Logic:** Provides application-level defaults so every `spark-submit` call inherits them.

```bash
$ cp spark-defaults.conf.template spark-defaults.conf
$ nano spark-defaults.conf
```

```properties
spark.master                     spark://localhost:7077
spark.executor.memory            1g
spark.driver.memory              1g
spark.cores.max                  2
spark.eventLog.enabled           true
spark.eventLog.dir               hdfs://localhost:9000/spark-logs
spark.historyFs.logDirectory     hdfs://localhost:9000/spark-logs
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `hdfs://...` lines require Hadoop installed. For pure local lab work, **comment them out** and set:
> ```properties
> spark.eventLog.dir  file:///tmp/spark-logs
> ```

## 3.9 Step 9 — Start the Spark Master and Worker

**Logic:** The `sbin/start-master.sh` launches the master daemon. The `start-workers.sh` (or `start-slaves.sh` in older versions) reads the `workers` file and SSH-launches a worker on each listed host.

```bash
$ $SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-master.sh
$ $SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-workers.sh        # or start-slaves.sh on older versions
```

**Expected Output (terminal):**

```
starting org.apache.spark.deploy.master.Master, logging to /opt/spark/logs/spark-...-Master-...out
localhost: starting org.apache.spark.deploy.worker.Worker, logging to /opt/spark/logs/.../...out
```

**Verify with `jps`:**

```bash
$ jps
```

```
4256 Master
4312 Worker
6200 Jps
```

## 3.10 Step 10 — Access the Web UIs

Open a browser and visit:

| URL | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `http://localhost:8080` | **Spark Master UI** — shows cluster status, attached workers |
| `http://localhost:8081` | **Spark Worker UI** — executor details |
| `http://localhost:4040` | **Spark Application UI** — opens when a job runs |

**Verification screenshot expectation:** A table with one **Worker** listed, **State = ALIVE**, **Cores = 2**, **Memory = 2.0 GiB**.

## 3.11 Step 11 — Test the Installation with PySpark Shell

**Logic:** PySpark is a Python wrapper around the Scala/Java Spark core. Launching it proves Python-JVM bridge (Py4J) is healthy.

```bash
$ pyspark
```

**Expected Output (truncated):**

```
Welcome to
      ____              __
     / __/__  ___ _____/ /__
    _\ \/ _ \/ _ `/ __/  '_/
   /__ / .__/\_,_/_/ /_/\_\   version 3.5.0
      /_/

Using Python version 3.10.12 ...
SparkSession available as 'spark'.
```

Run the canonical test:

```python
>>> spark.version
'3.5.0'
>>> sc = spark.sparkContext
>>> data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
>>> distData = sc.parallelize(data)
>>> distData.reduce(lambda a, b: a + b)
15
```

Exit with:

```python
>>> exit()
```

## 3.12 Step 12 — Run a WordCount via `spark-submit` (Viva Favorite)

**Create `wordcount.py`:**

```python
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("KTU-WordCount") \
    .getOrCreate()

text = spark.sparkContext.textFile("file:///opt/spark/README.md")
counts = text.flatMap(lambda line: line.split(" ")) \
             .filter(lambda w: len(w) > 0) \
             .map(lambda w: (w, 1)) \
             .reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)

output = counts.collect()
for (word, count) in output[:10]:
    print(f"{word}: {count}")

spark.stop()
```

**Run it:**

```bash
$ spark-submit /opt/spark/wordcount.py
```

**Expected Output (top 10 lines):**

```
Spark: 16
README: 1
for: 12
using: 8
...
```

## 3.13 Step 13 — Stop the Cluster Cleanly

**Logic:** Always stop Spark daemons to free RAM and ports.

```bash
$ $SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-workers.sh
$ $SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-master.sh
```

## 3.14 Analytical Matrix — Troubleshooting Cheat Sheet (KTU Lab Manual Standard)

| Symptom | Root Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| `JAVA_HOME is not set` | `source ~/.bashrc` not run, or path wrong | `export JAVA_HOME=...` then `source` |
| `python3: command not found` in PySpark | `PYSPARK_PYTHON` wrong | `export PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3` |
| Worker dies immediately | SSH passwordless login missing | `ssh-keygen` + `ssh-copy-id localhost` |
| `Address already in use` on 8080 | Previous master still running | `$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-master.sh` then kill by `jps` |
| OutOfMemoryError | `spark.executor.memory` too high | Reduce to `1g` or close Chrome tabs |
| Py4J network error | Firewall blocking localhost | `sudo ufw allow 7077` / disable firewall in lab |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Spark Standalone Cluster Architecture (Single-Node)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DRIVER["Driver Program  - Your Code"]
        SC["SparkContext"]
        DAG["DAG Scheduler"]
        TS["Task Scheduler"]
    end

    subgraph CM["Cluster Manager - Standalone"]
        MASTER["Master Node - port 7077 - Web UI 8080"]
    end

    subgraph W1["Worker Node 1 - localhost"]
        EXE1["Executor JVM"]
        BM1["Block Manager  - RAM + Disk cache"]
        CACHE1["Cached RDD Partitions"]
    end

    subgraph W2["Worker Node 2 - optional lab extension"]
        EXE2["Executor JVM"]
        BM2["Block Manager"]
        CACHE2["Cached RDD Partitions"]
    end

    SC -->|requests resources| MASTER
    MASTER -->|allocates| EXE1
    MASTER -->|allocates| EXE2
    TS -->|sends Tasks| EXE1
    TS -->|sends Tasks| EXE2
    EXE1 <-->|shuffle read/write| EXE2
    EXE1 --- BM1
    EXE2 --- BM2
    BM1 --- CACHE1
    BM2 --- CACHE2
```


## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — spark-submit Command Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SS["spark-submit"] --> OPT1["--master spark://host:7077"]
    SS --> OPT2["--deploy-mode client or cluster"]
    SS --> OPT3["--executor-memory 2g"]
    SS --> OPT4["--total-executor-cores 4"]
    SS --> OPT5["--driver-memory 1g"]
    SS --> OPT6["app.py or app.jar"]
    SS --> OPT7["arg1  arg2  arg3 to your program"]
```

**Verbal mapping of the options (viva-ready):**

| Option | Meaning | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `--master` | Cluster manager URL | `local[*]` |
| `--deploy-mode` | Where the driver runs | `client` |
| `--executor-memory` | RAM per executor | `1g` |
| `--total-executor-cores` | Total CPU cores for all executors | All available |
| `--driver-memory` | RAM for the driver JVM | `1g` |
| `--num-executors` | Number of executor JVMs | `2` in YARN |
| `--conf k=v` | Inline Spark property | — |
| `--class` | Main class (for Java/Scala JARs) | — |
| `--jars` | Extra dependency JARs | — |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - PCCDL607 Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`
**List and briefly explain any three environment variables that must be set for a successful Apache Spark installation on Ubuntu.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **`JAVA_HOME`** — Points to the JDK installation directory (e.g., `/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64`). Required because Spark runs on the JVM. **(1 mark)**
2. **`SPARK_HOME`** — Points to the root of the Spark installation (e.g., `/opt/spark`). Used by `spark-submit` and the shell scripts to locate binaries. **(1 mark)**
3. **`PATH`** (extended with `$SPARK_HOME/bin` and `$SPARK_HOME/sbin`) — Allows commands like `pyspark`, `spark-submit`, `start-master.sh` to be invoked from any directory. **(1 mark)**
   *(Optional 4th: `PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3` to bind PySpark to a specific Python interpreter.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - PCCDL607 Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`
**Differentiate between Spark's `local` mode and `standalone` cluster mode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `local` Mode | `Standalone` Cluster Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Single JVM, no cluster manager | Master + Worker processes |
| Threads | `local[n]` uses `n` threads | True multi-node parallelism |
| Use case | Testing, teaching, debugging | Real distributed jobs |
| Driver location | Inside the local JVM | On submitting machine (client) or inside a worker (cluster) |
| Fault tolerance | None | Worker restart by Master |
| Web UI port | 4040 only | 8080 (Master) + 8081 (Worker) + 4040 (App) |

**(1 mark per valid point; full 3 for clean table comparison.)**

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - PCCDL607 Sample, Module 2]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Spark Standalone cluster architecture. List the major daemons and their default ports. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the architecture (2 marks):**
Spark Standalone is Spark's **built-in simple cluster manager**. It uses a **Master–Worker** pattern. The **Driver** holds the `SparkContext` and DAG scheduler. The **Master** allocates resources. Each **Worker** hosts one or more **Executors** (JVM processes) that run **Tasks** and store **RDD blocks** in memory or disk.

**Step 2 — Identify daemons + ports (2 marks):**

| Daemon | Default Port | Web UI |
|---|---|---|
| Master | `7077` (RPC), `8080` (UI) | `http://localhost:8080` |
| Worker | Random (RPC), `8081` (UI) | `http://localhost:8081` |
| Application Driver | `4040` (UI, ephemeral) | `http://localhost:4040` |
| History Server | `18080` | `http://localhost:18080` |

**Step 3 — Describe component roles (2 marks):**
- **Driver → Master:** sends `RegisterApplication` with resource requirements.
- **Master → Workers:** instructs to launch Executors.
- **Executors → Driver:** send heartbeats and task results every `spark.executor.heartbeatInterval` (default **10 s**).
- **Block Manager:** caches shuffle data and RDD partitions.

**Step 4 — Neat diagram (1 mark):** [Reuse *Mermaid Diagram 1* from SECTION_4 with paper-friendly labels.]

---

**(b) Write the complete step-by-step procedure to install Apache Spark 3.5.0 in standalone mode on Ubuntu 22.04. Show the expected verification output. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The complete procedure with 13 steps is given in SECTION_3 (Steps 1–13). The student should reproduce the following **key checkpoints** for full marks:

1. **JDK verification:** `java -version` → `openjdk 11.0.x` **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Spark download & extract:** `tar -xzf spark-3.5.0-bin-hadoop3.tgz` → folder `/opt/spark` **(1 mark)**
3. **`.bashrc` exports** of `JAVA_HOME`, `SPARK_HOME`, `PATH` **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Edit `spark-env.sh`** with `JAVA_HOME` and `SPARK_MASTER_HOST=localhost` **(1 mark)**
5. **Edit `workers` file** to include `localhost` **(0.5 mark)**
6. **Start daemons** with `start-master.sh` and `start-workers.sh` **(1 mark)**
7. **Verify with `jps`** showing `Master` and `Worker` processes **(0.5 mark)**
8. **Launch `pyspark`** and run `sc.parallelize([1,2,3]).sum()` → output `6` **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Forgetting to `source ~/.bashrc` after editing — student gets *"JAVA_HOME not set"*. → **Lose 1 mark** for not showing the `source` step.
> - ❌ Confusing `start-slaves.sh` (Spark ≤ 2.x) with `start-workers.sh` (Spark 3.x). → **Lose 0.5 mark**.
> - ❌ Writing `spark://localhost:8080` as the master URL — wrong! The **RPC port is `7077`**, the **UI port is `8080`**. → **Lose 1 mark**.
> - ❌ Not showing the verification output (`jps` listing, browser screenshot) — KTU demands *expected output* after each step. → **Lose up to 2 marks**.
> - ❌ Submitting a *source-compiled* Spark build (sbt/maven) when the binary was asked — wastes 30 minutes of exam time.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - PCCDL607 Sample, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) Explain the role of the following configuration files in a Spark Standalone deployment: `spark-env.sh`, `spark-defaults.conf`, and `workers`. Provide a sample snippet for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) `spark-env.sh` — Master daemon & worker startup environment (2.5 marks):**
This file is sourced by the Master and Worker startup scripts. It defines **system-level** environment variables.

```bash
# spark-env.sh snippet
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-11-openjdk-amd64
export SPARK_MASTER_HOST=localhost
export SPARK_MASTER_PORT=7077
export SPARK_MASTER_WEBUI_PORT=8080
export SPARK_WORKER_CORES=2
export SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY=2g
export SPARK_DAEMON_JAVA_OPTS="-Dspark.deploy.recoveryMode=FILESYSTEM"
```

**(ii) `spark-defaults.conf` — Default Spark application properties (2.5 marks):**
This file sets the **default values** for any property the user does not pass on the command line. It is read by `SparkConf`.

```properties
# spark-defaults.conf snippet
spark.master          spark://localhost:7077
spark.app.id          KTU-Lab-Session
spark.driver.memory   1g
spark.executor.memory 1g
spark.cores.max       2
spark.eventLog.enabled true
spark.eventLog.dir    file:///tmp/spark-logs
```

**(iii) `workers` — Worker hostnames list (2 marks):**
Each non-comment line is treated as a hostname on which the Master will attempt to SSH and start a Worker.

```
# workers file content
localhost
# worker2.lab.local
# worker3.lab.local
```

---

**(b) What is `spark-submit`? Explain its command-line options with an example that submits a PySpark WordCount program to a Standalone cluster with 2 executors each of 2 GB memory. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** `spark-submit` is the **unified command-line utility** used to launch Spark applications on any supported cluster manager (Standalone, YARN, Mesos, K8s). It packages the application code, its dependencies, and runtime configurations into a single invocation.

**Key options (3 marks):**

| Option | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `--master <url>` | Cluster manager URL (`spark://host:7077`, `yarn`, `local[*]`) |
| `--deploy-mode <mode>` | `client` (default) or `cluster` |
| `--name <name>` | Application name shown in UI |
| `--num-executors <n>` | Number of executor JVMs (YARN/Mesos/K8s) |
| `--executor-memory <size>` | RAM per executor (e.g., `2g`) |
| `--total-executor-cores <n>` | Aggregate CPU cores across all executors |
| `--driver-memory <size>` | RAM for driver process |
| `--conf k=v` | Arbitrary `SparkConf` property |
| `--py-files <list>` | Additional `.py` / `.zip` / `.egg` files shipped to executors |
| `<app file>` | Path to `.py`, `.jar`, or `.R` file |

**Example invocation (2 marks):**

```bash
$ spark-submit \
    --master spark://localhost:7077 \
    --deploy-mode client \
    --name KTU-WordCount \
    --num-executors 2 \
    --executor-memory 2g \
    --total-executor-cores 4 \
    --driver-memory 1g \
    /opt/spark/wordcount.py
```

**Expected behavior (1 mark):** The driver connects to `spark://localhost:7077`, Master allocates **2 executors** on the registered Workers, each executor gets **2 GB RAM**, the program reads `README.md`, computes word frequencies, prints top 10, and stops cleanly. The Web UI at `http://localhost:4040` shows the DAG of `textFile → flatMap → filter → map → reduceByKey → collect`.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Before KTU Lab Exam — Bookmark This Block!**

- ✅ **Spark = in-memory distributed computing** framework, ~100× faster than Hadoop MapReduce for iterative algorithms.
- ✅ **Three mandatory components** to install: **JDK 8/11**, **Spark 3.5.0 binary**, **Python 3** (only for PySpark).
- ✅ **Three environment variables** without which Spark will NOT start: `JAVA_HOME`, `SPARK_HOME`, updated `PATH`.
- ✅ **Spark Standalone** uses **port `7077`** for RPC and **`8080`** for the Master Web UI. (Do NOT confuse with `8080` as the master URL.)
- ✅ **Worker file** = list of hostnames where workers will be launched (SSH must be passwordless).
- ✅ **`spark-env.sh`** = system env vars; **`spark-defaults.conf`** = app defaults; **`workers`** = host list.
- ✅ **Verify installation with** `jps` (must show `Master` + `Worker`) and `pyspark` then `sc.parallelize([1,2,3]).sum() == 6`.
- ✅ **Deployment modes** to memorize: `local`, `standalone-client`, `standalone-cluster`, `yarn-client`, `yarn-cluster`.
- ✅ **`spark-submit` anatomy:** `spark-submit --master <url> --deploy-mode <m> [options] <app> [app args]`.
- ✅ **KTU accepted verification output for WordCount:** the top word on `README.md` is `"the"` or `"Spark"` (varies by version) — your output need not be exact, but **non-empty counts are mandatory**.
- ✅ **Common viva questions:**
  1. *Why does Spark need Java?* → JVM-based runtime.
  2. *What is the difference between Driver and Master?* → Driver is *your code's process*; Master is *cluster manager daemon*.
  3. *What happens when a Worker dies?* → Master re-launches it on a healthy host (if HA configured); running tasks are recomputed using RDD lineage.
  4. *Where are intermediate results stored?* → In **Executor Block Manager** (RAM + spill-to-disk).
  5. *Can Spark run without Hadoop?* → **Yes**, in local or standalone mode. Hadoop is needed **only** for HDFS access or YARN.
